component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not render visible UI. It initializes booking context before any visible elements are shown. Spatially, it sits conceptually at the very top of the page load sequence, but it occupies no space and adds no visual elements; the layout begins visually with the booking-header-context component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; no spatial footprint and no scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main, upper white booking card, the service-package-selector sits near the top, directly beneath the card’s title region (which is informed by the booking-header-context). It spans the full internal width of the card (about 60–70% of viewport width, centered), with 24–32px padding above and below. Service options are presented as a vertical stack of cards or radio rows, each running nearly the full width of the card with 8–16px vertical spacing between them. On desktop, each option row can follow a two-column internal structure (icon or small image on the left, title/description and price on the right), echoing the icon-plus-text rows from the mockup. There is about 24px of vertical space between the bottom of this selector and the session-option-selector below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally in the main content column within the upper white card; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-header-context is the first visible content block in the main column, positioned near the top center of the page, below any global site header. It spans roughly 60% of the page width on desktop and is fully centered. It includes a large, bold main heading in red and possibly a short subtitle in a softer tone. There is generous whitespace above (32–48px from the site header) and below (24–32px before the main white booking card) to give it a hero-like presence. Text is center-aligned, pulling the eye into the vertical flow and visually anchoring the subsequent white card underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main content column; scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main upper white card, the session-option-selector is positioned directly beneath the service-package-selector, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It also spans the full width of the card’s content area. Session options (e.g., single visit, multi-session package, virtual consult) are arranged as a vertical list of pill-like buttons or radio rows, left-aligned for easy scanning. Each option row has 8–16px vertical spacing between it and the next, with comfortable padding inside each option so the list feels airy, similar in spacing to the bullet list in the mockup. A subtle section label or small heading may sit above the first option, aligned left with 8–12px spacing below it. There is another 24–32px gap below this selector before the filters begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the upper card; scrolls with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-picker-control appears in the upper white card as part of a small filter cluster positioned beneath the session options. On larger screens, it sits in a horizontal row with the time-window-filter and the price-and-sort-controls, forming a three-part filter bar that spans the width of the card’s content area. The date picker occupies roughly the left third of this row. The row itself has about 16px of vertical padding above and below and is separated from the session options by ~24px. On narrower screens, the date picker drops into its own row above the other filters, maintaining a clear left alignment. The control is visually compact but given enough horizontal space so the calendar input doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within a responsive filter row; rearranges from horizontal to vertical stack on smaller viewports but otherwise scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Also within the upper white card’s filter cluster, the time-window-filter is positioned to the right of the date-picker-control when viewed on desktop, occupying the center third of the filter row. It aligns horizontally with the other filters, all sharing a common baseline. There is 16px of horizontal spacing between each filter element. The time-window-filter might present as a dropdown or segmented toggle (e.g., morning/afternoon/evening) with a compact label directly above or inside the control. On small screens, it stacks directly below the date-picker-control with about 12–16px vertical spacing. The whole filter cluster sits about 24px above the availability-slot-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive inline filter within the main card; participates in the same reflow rules as the date picker and sort controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Completing the filter cluster inside the upper white card, the price-and-sort-controls are placed on the right side of the horizontal filter row on desktop, occupying roughly the right third of the card’s width. They consist of one or two compact dropdowns or pill controls, aligned to the right edge of the main card’s content. There is 16px horizontal spacing between individual controls if more than one is present. On smaller viewports, these controls stack below the time-window-filter with 12–16px spacing, keeping a clear top-to-bottom progression of filters. The entire filter area retains generous white space around it so it reads as a light, secondary toolset rather than a dense toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, responsive UI within the filter row; reflows from right-aligned in a horizontal row to part of a vertical stack on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-slot-list occupies the lower half of the upper white booking card, directly beneath the filter controls, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating them. It spans the full internal width of the card. Slots are displayed as a vertically scrolling list of items (e.g., time blocks or small cards) that each run most of the width of the card, with 8–16px vertical spacing between items. Each slot row can use a subtle two-column internal structure (time/details on the left, select button or price on the right), mirroring the mockup’s icon-and-text rows but in a booking context. The list is tall enough to show multiple visible slots at once, but the card itself scrolls with the page (no inner scroll); the overall feel remains editorial and open rather than confined. There is 24–32px of vertical padding at the bottom of the card below the last slot, tying in with the card’s generous padding rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical list inside the main card; extends as needed, with the whole page scrolling rather than an internal scroll area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-summary-panel appears as a compact, floating card along the right side of the viewport, roughly aligned vertically with the mid-to-lower portion of the upper white booking card. It does not sit inside the main column; instead, it occupies a narrow vertical strip in the right margin (approximately 15–20% of viewport width), maintaining at least 16–24px spacing from the right edge of the main content card. The panel has a white background, subtle shadow, and rounded corners, with internal padding of about 16–24px. Inside, key details (selected package, session type, selected date and time, estimated price) are stacked in a short vertical summary, with the main call-to-action button (e.g., “Continue” or “Next step”) anchored at the bottom. The card’s size is modest enough to feel like a FAB-like helper rather than a full second column. It remains visible as the user scrolls through the slot list and into the form section, subtly reinforcing context and encouraging progression.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky floating panel on desktop: it maintains a fixed position relative to the viewport’s right side and mid-height as the user scrolls, while respecting top and bottom margins so it doesn’t collide with the header or footer. On smaller screens, it can snap into the main flow below the availability-slot-list as a full-width block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller is responsible for loading and managing payment options but does not itself render visible UI. Conceptually, it is associated with the bottom portion of the lower white card (the details and payment area). It occupies no explicit space; instead, it ensures that, when payment methods are ready, they appear inline within the booking-details-form’s layout near the form’s end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that has no direct spatial presence; its effects appear inside the booking-details-form component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-details-form lives in the second main white card, positioned directly beneath the olive-green bridge band. There is approximately 24–32px of background whitespace between the bottom of the upper booking card and the top of the olive band, and another 16–24px between the olive band and the top edge of this lower card. The card matches the width and horizontal centering of the card above (about 60–70% of viewport width, centered) and uses similar padding: 24–32px vertical and 32–40px horizontal. At the very top of this component, the form may include the olive-green full-width band as its own header or is tightly coupled with it, featuring a single, centered, uppercase line of text such as “NEXT: ENTER YOUR DETAILS TO CONFIRM” that echoes the mockup’s narrative bridge. Below that, user input fields (contact info, address, home details, notes) are arranged in a single-column layout with 16–24px vertical spacing between field groups. Toward the bottom of the form area, payment methods loaded via the booking-payment-options-loader appear as a subsection with its own small heading and 16–24px spacing around it. The main confirmation/booking button is placed at the very bottom, spanning most of the card’s width but respecting generous side margins. The entire card feels open and welcoming, with ample white space to prevent the form from feeling dense or intimidating.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block card in the main vertical flow; scrolls normally. On longer forms, the user scrolls within the page rather than inside an inner scroll region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-navigation is rendered as a compact, circular or pill-shaped floating button containing a cart or booking icon, positioned near the upper-right side of the viewport. Horizontally, it sits just to the right of the main content column, maintaining approximately 16–24px spacing from the main card’s right edge. Vertically, it aligns roughly with the top of the upper white booking card, hovering over the background rather than inside a card. Its size is modest—large enough to be easily clickable but visually lighter than the booking-summary-panel. This placement echoes the floating side FABs from the mockup, making it a peripheral yet persistent navigation shortcut back to a cart or multi-booking overview.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky floating control: it maintains a fixed position relative to the top-right of the content area as the user scrolls, staying visible but not overlapping the main cards or the booking-summary-panel. On small screens, it may collapse into the top navigation bar instead of floating.

name: Packages
title: Packages
description: Browse and filter professional organizing packages by category, home size, price, and sort order, then open a specific package to configure it in detail.

core_features: Load package categories via getPackageCategories and expose them as selectable filters (e.g., category tabs or dropdown).
Load package listings via listPackages using parameters derived from the current URL (packageCategoryId, minBedrooms, maxBedrooms, minPrice, maxPrice, sortBy).
Allow users to adjust filters for category, bedroom range, and price range, with each change updating the URL query parameters and reloading listPackages.
Allow users to change sorting (e.g., by price or rating) using the sortBy parameter and reloading listPackages.
Display returned packages as interactive cards showing summary information (name, category_name, description, base_price, total_hours, bedroom range, ratings, and booking/cart capability flags).
Handle deep-linking: initial filter and sort state is read from URL parameters so that shared/bookmarked links reproduce the same list view.
Provide navigation from a selected package card to package_detail.html with the appropriate packageId parameter.
Allow navigation to cart.html via a header cart link, without loading any cart data on this page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Land on Packages from another page with a preselected category
1. User clicks a link to packages.html from another page that includes a packageCategoryId (and optionally price/bedroom filters, sortBy) in the URL.
2. On page load, the URL-state logic parses packageCategoryId, minBedrooms, maxBedrooms, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy from the query string.
3. The page calls getPackageCategories to retrieve all available PackageCategory records.
4. Once categories are loaded, the category filter component highlights the category whose ID matches packageCategoryId (if it exists in the results); otherwise, no category filter is applied.
5. The page then calls listPackages with the parsed parameters (only passing parameters that are present and valid) to retrieve matching Package objects.
6. The package list component renders cards for each returned package using the data from listPackages.
7. The result summary component reflects the number of packages and the currently active filters (derived from URL/state).

Workflow 2: User adjusts filters and sorting to refine packages
1. User arrives on packages.html (with or without initial URL parameters) and sees the current list of packages.
2. User selects a different category in the category filter (e.g., switches to a different PackageCategory from those provided by getPackageCategories).
3. The category filter component updates the internal filter state and requests the URL-state manager to update the packageCategoryId query parameter accordingly (adding, changing, or removing it).
4. The URL-state manager writes the new query string to the browser URL (e.g., via history.replaceState) without reloading the page.
5. The page calls listPackages again with the updated packageCategoryId (and other current filters) and re-renders the package list.
6. User then modifies bedroom filters (minBedrooms and/or maxBedrooms) via numeric inputs or sliders; when the user confirms the change (e.g., blur or Apply button), the filter component updates the URL parameters and triggers listPackages with updated bedroom-related parameters.
7. User may also adjust minPrice and maxPrice; similar logic updates the query and reloads listPackages.
8. User changes the sort option in the sort control (e.g., to a value like price_asc or rating_desc); this updates sortBy in the URL and triggers listPackages again.
9. At all times, the currently applied filters and sort criteria are reflected both in the controls and the URL query string.

Workflow 3: Deep-link sharing and browser navigation
1. After refining the list, user copies the URL from the browser address bar and shares/bookmarks it.
2. Another visitor opens this URL later. On load, the URL-state manager parses all query parameters and initializes the filter and sort controls to match these values.
3. The page calls getPackageCategories, then listPackages with the parsed parameters to reproduce the original filtered/sorted view.
4. If the user uses the browser Back/Forward buttons after changing filters, the URL-state manager listens for URL changes (e.g., popstate) and re-applies the corresponding filter/sort state, then calls listPackages again so the visible results always match the URL.

Workflow 4: Navigate from a package to its detailed configuration
1. User browses the package list and finds a package card they want to explore.
2. User clicks anywhere on the interactive area of the package card or a dedicated action such as a 'View details' control within the card.
3. The package card component reads the package_id value returned by listPackages for that card.
4. The page navigates to package_detail.html with a query parameter packageId set to that package_id (e.g., package_detail.html?packageId=<package_id>).
5. The user then configures bedrooms and add-ons on package_detail.html (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow 5: Navigate to the cart from Packages
1. User clicks the cart icon in the global header while on packages.html.
2. The header navigation component triggers a client-side navigation to cart.html.
3. No cart data is fetched on packages.html; all cart-related logic occurs on cart.html.
interactions: Selecting a package category: Clicking a category tab or choosing a category in a dropdown updates the selected category in state, updates packageCategoryId in the URL, and triggers a new listPackages call with the updated categoryId.

Changing bedroom filters: Editing minBedrooms and maxBedrooms (via inputs or sliders) and confirming the change updates corresponding URL parameters and triggers listPackages with the new bedroom range.
Changing price filters: Editing minPrice and maxPrice and confirming the change updates corresponding URL parameters and triggers listPackages with the new price range.

Resetting filters: Clicking a Reset or Clear Filters control removes filter-related parameters (packageCategoryId, minBedrooms, maxBedrooms, minPrice, maxPrice) from the URL, resets the filter controls, and triggers listPackages with only sortBy (or with no parameters if sortBy is also cleared).
Changing sort option: Selecting a new sort option updates sortBy in the URL and triggers listPackages with the new sortBy value and current filters.
Clicking a package card: Navigates to package_detail.html?packageId=<package_id> where package_id is obtained from the listPackages result for that card.

Handling empty results: If listPackages returns an empty array for the current filters, an empty-state component is shown with a control to clear or relax filters; clicking that control resets relevant URL parameters and triggers listPackages again.
Browser Back/Forward: When the user navigates through history, the page re-parses URL parameters, updates UI controls, and re-calls listPackages so that the view always reflects the URL.
Clicking the cart icon: From the header navigation, clicking the cart icon performs a navigation to cart.html without any data fetching from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary state is fully synchronized with URL query parameters and is not persisted in localStorage. On initial load, the page parses the following query parameters: packageCategoryId (string), minBedrooms (number), maxBedrooms (number), minPrice (number), maxPrice (number), and sortBy (string). Any parameter that is missing or invalid (e.g., non-numeric for numeric fields) is treated as undefined and omitted from the initial listPackages call.

After parsing, the page calls getPackageCategories once to retrieve all PackageCategory records. The category filter UI then aligns its selected state with packageCategoryId if it matches one of the returned categories. If packageCategoryId does not match any available category, the page ignores it (removes it from the internal state and, on the next URL update, from the query string) and treats the selection as 'no category filter'.

For listPackages, the page constructs a parameter object based on the current internal state, which is itself initialized from URL parameters and updated via user interactions. Only defined and valid values are passed to listPackages; undefined filters are omitted so the backend can apply its own defaults. For example, if minPrice is not set, it is simply not included in the listPackages call.

When the user changes any filter or sort control, the page updates the internal state and then re-writes the URL query parameters to match. Parameters that are cleared by the user (e.g., removing a maxBedrooms value) are removed from the query string entirely. These URL updates are done without reloading the page, and each update then triggers a fresh call to listPackages with the new parameters.

The page also listens for URL changes caused by browser Back/Forward navigation. When such events occur, it re-parses the URL, updates the internal state and all filter/sort controls to match those parameters, and calls listPackages again. This ensures that the visible packages and controls always reflect the current URL query string and that any URL to packages.html can be treated as a stable, shareable representation of the current filter and sort state.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters for packageCategoryId, minBedrooms, maxBedrooms, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy; initializes the page state from the URL on load; listens to browser navigation events; and triggers calls to getPackageCategories and listPackages with the appropriate parameters.

Displays available package categories as a set of selectable options (e.g., tabs or dropdown) using data from getPackageCategories; allows the user to select or clear a category filter; and notifies the URL-state manager when the selection changes so that packageCategoryId and the package list are updated.

Provides inputs (e.g., numeric fields or sliders) for minBedrooms and maxBedrooms. Initializes its values from URL parameters provided by the URL-state manager, validates user input as numeric, and notifies the URL-state manager when the user applies changes so that the corresponding URL parameters and the listPackages call are updated.

Provides inputs for minPrice and maxPrice. Initializes its values from the URL via the URL-state manager, validates numeric input ranges, and upon user confirmation updates the URL-state manager so that minPrice and maxPrice are updated in the query string and a new listPackages call is triggered.

Presents sort options that map directly to the sortBy parameter used by listPackages (e.g., options that output values such as price_asc or rating_desc). Initializes from the sortBy URL parameter, and when the user changes the selection, it updates sortBy via the URL-state manager and triggers listPackages with the new sort order.

Provides a control (such as a Clear All Filters action) that resets packageCategoryId, minBedrooms, maxBedrooms, minPrice, and maxPrice in the internal state and URL, and then triggers listPackages with filters cleared while preserving or resetting sortBy according to the defined behavior.

Coordinates calls to listPackages whenever filters or sorting change. Receives the current valid parameters from the URL-state manager, invokes listPackages with those parameters, manages loading/error states, and passes the resulting package array to the package list and result summary components.

Displays a summary based on the current listPackages result (such as count of returned packages) and a concise representation of currently active filters and sort order. Updates whenever new package data or state changes are received from the package-list-loader and URL-state manager.

Receives the array of package objects from the package-list-loader (each containing package_id, name, category_name, description, base_price, total_hours, bedroom-related fields, ratings, and `can_be_booked`/`can_be_added_to_cart` flags) and renders them as a collection of interactive package-card components.

Represents a single package item as an interactive card. Displays the package’s key attributes from the listPackages response and exposes a click action that navigates the user to package_detail.html with packageId set to the card’s package_id.

Listens for updates from the package-list-loader. If listPackages returns no packages for the current filters, it displays an informational message and a control to relax or clear filters. When the user interacts with this control, it notifies the URL-state manager to reset or adjust filters, causing a new listPackages call.

Provides high-level navigation including a link or icon that navigates to cart.html. On this page it does not fetch or display any cart data, but simply routes the user when the cart control is clicked.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow. Start with the fixed header navigation across the top, then a centered main column. Inside this column, show a prominent white filter card near the top that holds category, bedroom, price, and sort controls in a vertically stacked, clearly ordered layout. Below the card, add a full-width olive-green band containing the result summary text as a narrative bridge. Under that, present the package list in a centered grid of cards. Floating/reset actions sit on the right edge of the viewport, outside the main reading column, so they are always visible but never interrupt the vertical reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow. Start with the fixed header navigation across the top, then a centered main column. Inside this column, show a prominent white filter card near the top that holds category, bedroom, price, and sort controls in a vertically stacked, clearly ordered layout. Below the card, add a full-width olive-green band containing the result summary text as a narrative bridge. Under that, present the package list in a centered grid of cards. Floating/reset actions sit on the right edge of the viewport, outside the main reading column, so they are always visible but never interrupt the vertical reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain the main content to a centered column spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop (roughly 8–10 columns of an implied 12-column grid), leaving 15–20% margins on each side. The header and olive band are full-width across the content area. The white filter card has generous interior padding (~24–32px vertical, ~32–40px horizontal). Vertical gaps between major sections (filter card, olive band, package grid) are about 32–48px. The floating action control on the right consumes minimal width and sits within the right margin area rather than the main column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a spacious, low-to-medium density feel. Controls inside the filter card are grouped but not cramped, with clear vertical separation using 16–24px gaps between control rows. Package cards use generous padding and warm white space so each card feels like a marketing tile rather than a tight app cell. Typography is large, with comfortable line heights and breathing room around section boundaries, mirroring the editorial/marketing look from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye downwards in a strong vertical line. After the user’s gaze passes the header, it hits the top of the centered filter card, reads filters from top to bottom, then naturally continues downward to the olive-green summary band (high contrast, uppercase text). From there the eye flows directly into the grid of package cards. Secondary/floating controls on the right (reset, possible quick actions) are present in peripheral vision, anchored at the mid-right of the viewport, so they are easy to access but do not compete with the central vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description: The page is framed by a full-width header navigation at the very top, sitting above a softly textured, warm background. Centered beneath this, a single dominant content column runs down the page, occupying around two-thirds of the viewport width with generous left and right margins that echo a 12-column grid. Near the top of this column a large, clean white card houses all filtering and sorting controls, padded generously on all sides so each control group breathes and feels editorial rather than utilitarian. Below this card, separated by a band of whitespace, a full-width olive-green strip spans the content area with a single centered, uppercase line of text that summarizes the current results, acting as a narrative bridge into the packages themselves. Immediately under the olive band, the package grid appears within the same central column: a series of warm, inviting package cards aligned in one or two columns depending on screen width, each card having ample padding and consistent spacing between them. A slim floating reset/filter action button sits along the right edge of the viewport, vertically around the mid-screen, hovering over the textured background and aligned with the central column’s height but not intruding into it. When data is loading, a subtle overlay or centered loader appears within the main column, temporarily dimming the package grid while leaving the overall layout intact. If no packages match the filters, a friendly, spacious empty-state message appears in place of the grid, still centered and aligned to the same column. Overall, the layout feels like a modern marketing page tuned specifically to browsing home-organizing packages: clean, roomy, and guided by a clear top-to-bottom reading path with gentle emphasis and color transitions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation sits at the very top of the page, spanning the full viewport width. It forms a horizontal bar with site branding on the left and primary navigation items to the right, all aligned within the same central content width as the main column below, creating a continuous vertical alignment. There is comfortable vertical padding (around 16–24px) within the header. Beneath it, a vertical gap of about 32px opens before the main white filter card begins, ensuring the header feels distinct but connected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays at the top of the document flow; may optionally use sticky positioning on scroll so it remains visible, but its height is compact enough not to dominate the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates invisibly, with no direct visual footprint. Conceptually, it sits behind all on-page components, synchronizing filter and sort state with the URL. It does not occupy any explicit space in the layout or alter spacing between visual elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely functional; no rendered position in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package category filter is placed within the top section of the main white filter card, aligned to the same centered column as the rest of the content. It runs horizontally across most of the card’s width (roughly 80–90% of the card’s inner width), centered with equal left and right padding. On desktop, it appears as a row of segmented buttons or pill-shaped options, left-aligned within the card but visually centered on the page. There is generous padding above and below this row—about 16–24px—to make it a clear first interactive element and to mirror the mockup’s spacious heading area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filter card; its width scales with the card up to the card’s max-width (around 1040px). On smaller screens, pills wrap to multiple lines while retaining comfortable vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bedroom filter controls are positioned directly below the package category filter inside the same white filter card, separated by a vertical gap of about 16–24px. They occupy approximately half to two-thirds of the card’s inner width, left-aligned so labels and inputs form a neat column starting under the left edge of the category filter. If multiple bedroom options exist (e.g., chips, toggles, or a dropdown), they are arranged in a compact horizontal row or small grid, but with enough spacing (8–16px between options) to preserve the airy feel. The entire bedroom filter block is visually grouped with its label on top, mirroring the simple two-part row pattern from the mockup (label + interactive control directly beneath).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the filter card; it stays stacked in vertical order beneath the category filter. On narrow screens, all bedroom controls stack vertically to maintain readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price filter controls sit immediately below the bedroom controls inside the same white card, following the same vertical stacking logic. They are separated from the bedroom block by about 16–24px of vertical space. The price filter spans a similar width (about half to two-thirds of the card’s inner width), aligned with the left edge of the bedroom filter to create a strong vertical alignment. If it uses sliders and/or min-max fields, the label appears at the top, with the slider running horizontally beneath it and any numeric inputs aligned along the same left-hand column. Vertical spacing between the label, slider, and inputs follows the 8–16px rhythm to keep the section visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filter card; on smaller screens, elements stack and expand to nearly full card width while preserving consistent padding from the card’s edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is placed toward the lower-right area of the white filter card, giving a visual sense of concluding the filter section. Horizontally, it aligns to the right edge of the content inside the card, occupying about 25–30% of the card’s inner width. Vertically, it sits on the same horizontal band as the lower edge of the price controls or slightly below them, with 16–24px of separation. This positioning creates a subtle two-column feel inside the card: filters grouped on the left, and the sort dropdown on the right, echoing the icon-plus-text line pattern from the mockup but applied at section level. The sort label is right-aligned with its control for a tidy, compact presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the card; on narrow screens it drops below the price controls and becomes left-aligned, expanding up to nearly full card width for usability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter reset control appears as a small, circular or pill-shaped floating button positioned along the right edge of the viewport, not inside the main card. Vertically, it is anchored roughly at the middle of the viewport height or slightly above center, so it feels like a subtle side utility. Horizontally, it sits in the right margin, about 16–24px away from the edge of the main content column, visually hovering over the textured background. Its size is compact (around 40–48px if circular, or similar height if pill-shaped), using a contrasting accent color (e.g., blue or red) to echo the mockup’s floating blue FABs. It avoids overlapping the white filter card or the package cards, staying visually separate but always accessible as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed or sticky relative to the viewport: it remains in the same right-side position as the user scrolls the page, with a slight shadow to reinforce its floating nature.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package list loader appears within the main content column where the package grid normally sits, directly below the olive-green result summary band. When active, it either shows a centered spinner or skeleton cards that align to the same column width and approximate spacing as real package cards. The loader is vertically centered in the initial visible portion of the package list area, with ample whitespace around it so it feels integrated into the content rather than overlaid on the entire screen. If used as an overlay, it slightly dims the package list area only, maintaining the visible structure of the page (header, filter card, and olive band remain fully visible above).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in place based on loading state. If overlay style is used, it is absolutely positioned within the package list container, covering only that container while respecting its boundaries and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result summary sits immediately below the white filter card, separated by a vertical whitespace band of about 32px to clearly distinguish it as the next section. It occupies a full-width olive-green horizontal band across the entire content area, visually spanning from the left page margin to the right page margin (or at least full-width inside any site-wide content container). Inside this band, the summary text is centered horizontally and vertically, running on a single line where possible, set in uppercase to mimic the mockup’s bold narrative bridge text. The band has generous vertical padding (about 24–32px top and bottom), so the text feels like a substantial, banner-like statement before the grid of packages begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying its own block-level section between the filter card and the package list. On narrow screens, the band remains full-width; text may wrap onto two lines but remains centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package list sits directly beneath the olive-green result summary band, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It is contained within the same centered main column as the filter card, with a max width similar to the card (roughly 60–70% of the viewport on desktop). Within this container, package cards are arranged in a responsive grid: on larger screens, two cards per row with equal width and consistent horizontal spacing (around 24px between cards); on very wide screens, optionally three cards per row while maintaining generous margins. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to a single column, with each card spanning nearly the full width of the content column. Vertical spacing between rows of cards follows a 24–32px rhythm, maintaining the airy, marketing-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main flow. The grid is responsive: number of columns adapts to the available content width while preserving card proportions and consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each package card is a warm white rectangular tile within the package list grid. It fills its grid cell with internal padding of about 24px on all sides, creating a comfortable frame around its content. Cards share a consistent width within each row, with subtle corner rounding and a faint shadow or border to gently lift them off the textured background. Internally, content likely follows a vertical hierarchy—title at the top, key details and benefits in the middle, price and CTA toward the bottom—mirroring a marketing card rather than a dense data tile. Cards are left-aligned internally, and vertical spacing between lines of text and key sections uses 8–16px increments to keep them easy to scan. The visual weight of each card is balanced so the grid reads as a cohesive set of options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the grid. Cards may slightly elevate or change shadow on hover, but they maintain fixed size relative to their grid column. On small screens they stretch to nearly full-width of the content column while retaining their padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message occupies the same area as the package list when no results are found. It is centered within the main content column directly below the olive-green result summary band, with generous vertical padding above and below (around 48–64px) so it feels like a deliberate, friendly panel rather than an afterthought. The message is center-aligned, with a prominent heading and supportive body copy stacked vertically, possibly accompanied by a small illustrative icon. The width of the text block is constrained to a narrower column (about 50–60% of the main content width) to maintain readability and a calm, open feel. It may optionally include a secondary inline action (e.g., clear filters) that visually aligns with the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing only when the package list has no items. It replaces the grid layout entirely but retains the same horizontal alignment and margins to preserve structural continuity.

name: Package Detail
title: Package Detail
description: View and configure a decluttering/organizing package or virtual coaching bundle (bedrooms and add-on services), see live pricing, then add the configured package to the cart or proceed to booking.

core_features: Load full details of a specific package or virtual coaching bundle using the packageId URL parameter via getPackageDetails.
Display core package information (name, category, description, in-person vs virtual, duration/session details, rating) from getPackageDetails.
Provide configuration controls for rooms/bedrooms when applicable, constrained by the package’s min_bedrooms and max_bedrooms values.

Offer an Add-On Services section that lists applicable add-ons from getPackageDetails.applicable_addons, with interactive filters (e.g., by maximum price up to addon_price_filter_max) and multi-select toggles.
Use previewPackageConfiguration to compute a live pricing preview (base_price, addons_total_price, total_price) for the current bedrooms and selected add-ons.
Show a configuration summary panel (e.g., configured bedrooms, selected add-ons count/list, and live total price) based on previewPackageConfiguration output.

Allow users to save or update their current configuration by syncing bedrooms, selected add-ons, and filter values into URL parameters so the page can be reloaded or shared with the same configuration.
Allow users to add the configured package to the cart via addPackageToCartFromConfiguration (creating a PackageConfiguration internally), then navigate to cart.html on success.
Allow users to proceed directly to booking for the current package by navigating to booking.html with the current packageId.
user_workflows:
Configure a package and add it to the cart:
1) User arrives on package_detail.html?packageId=<id> from a listing page.
2) Page calls getPackageDetails with packageId and displays package info.
3) User adjusts the bedrooms control (within min_bedrooms and max_bedrooms if defined).
4) User optionally filters add-ons (e.g., sets a maximum price within addon_price_filter_max) and selects multiple add-ons from the filtered list.
5) Each change triggers previewPackageConfiguration with the current packageId, bedrooms, and selectedAddonIds; the summary panel updates with recalculated pricing.
6) User clicks “Add Package to Cart”; the page calls addPackageToCartFromConfiguration with packageId, bedrooms, selectedAddonIds, and quantity, then on success navigates to cart.html.

Filter and manage add-on selections with a budget-like constraint:
1) After getPackageDetails returns applicable add-ons, the user opens the Add-On Services section.
2) User sets a maximum add-on unit price filter (up to addon_price_filter_max) and an optional maximum total for selected add-ons (both reflected in URL parameters).
3) The add-on list shows only add-ons within the price filter; when the user selects or deselects add-ons, the component checks whether the combined add-ons_total_price from previewPackageConfiguration exceeds the user’s specified total limit.
4) If a new selection would exceed the limit, the UI prevents the change or shows a validation message, and keeps the previous valid selection.

Resume a configuration from a shared URL:
1) User A configures bedrooms and add-ons, then clicks “Save Customization/Update Package,” which updates the page URL parameters (bedrooms, addonIds, filters) without leaving the page.
2) User A copies the URL and shares it with User B.
3) User B opens the URL; on page load, the state manager reads packageId, bedrooms, addonIds, and filter parameters from the query string.
4) The page calls getPackageDetails, initializes controls based on those parameters, then calls previewPackageConfiguration to restore the exact pricing and summary for that configuration.

Move from configuration into booking:
1) User configures bedrooms and add-ons as desired and sees the live price in the summary panel.
2) User clicks “Book This Package.”
3) The page ensures the current configuration is previewed (calling previewPackageConfiguration if needed) and then navigates to booking.html?packageId=<currentPackageId>.
4) The booking page uses the packageId to continue the scheduling flow (beyond the scope of this page).
interactions: On page load or when the packageId URL parameter changes, call getPackageDetails, then initialize all configuration controls and filters based on the returned package properties and any additional URL parameters (bedrooms, add-ons, filters).

When the user changes the bedrooms value via plus/minus buttons or a dropdown, validate it against min_bedrooms and max_bedrooms (if provided), update the URL parameter, then call previewPackageConfiguration with the new bedrooms and current selected add-ons.

When the user changes add-on filters (e.g., maximum price, text search), update corresponding URL parameters and re-calculate the list of visible add-ons without calling the server again, since applicable_addons is already loaded from getPackageDetails.

When the user selects or deselects an add-on (e.g., toggling a checkbox), update the selected add-on IDs list in state and URL, then call previewPackageConfiguration; if a user-defined total add-on budget is exceeded, revert the change and display an inline validation message.

When the user clicks “Save Customization” or “Update Package,” persist the current bedrooms value, selected add-ons list, and filter settings into URL parameters (replacing the current query string, keeping packageId), and optionally re-trigger previewPackageConfiguration to ensure the summary is in sync.

When the user clicks “Add Package to Cart,” validate that the configuration is currently previewed (or re-run previewPackageConfiguration), then call addPackageToCartFromConfiguration with packageId, bedrooms, selectedAddonIds, and quantity; handle success by navigating to cart.html and handle failure by showing an error message.
When the user clicks “Book This Package,” optionally ensure the configuration has been previewed, then navigate to booking.html with the current packageId as a URL parameter.

When the user interacts with any controls while a previewPackageConfiguration or addPackageToCartFromConfiguration request is in progress, show a loading state for the pricing/submit buttons and prevent duplicate calls until the prior request completes or fails.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL parameter for this page is packageId, which is required. Additional optional parameters represent the current configuration and filters: bedrooms (number), addonIds (comma-separated list of add-on IDs), addonMaxPrice (number used for per-add-on price filtering), addonTotalLimit (number representing the user’s maximum total add-on spend), and quantity (number of packages to add to cart). On initial load, the page reads packageId; if it is missing or invalid, the page shows an error state and does not call getPackageDetails. When packageId is valid, the page calls getPackageDetails(packageId). After package details are loaded, the page reads other URL parameters: if bedrooms is present and within min_bedrooms/max_bedrooms (when defined), it is used; otherwise, a default (such as base_bedrooms_included or min_bedrooms) is chosen and written back to the URL. The addonIds parameter is parsed into an array of selected add-on IDs and intersected with the returned applicable_addons list; any IDs not applicable are ignored and removed from the URL on the next sync. The addonMaxPrice parameter is clamped to be less than or equal to addon_price_filter_max when that value is returned by getPackageDetails; if addonMaxPrice is not provided, a default is derived from the add-on data and stored in the URL when the user first changes filters. The addonTotalLimit parameter is optional; if present, it is treated as the maximum total price allowed for selected add-ons and is used only on the client side to validate selections. Whenever bedrooms or addonIds change (either via direct user interaction or URL parameter changes), the page calls previewPackageConfiguration(packageId, bedrooms, selectedAddonIds) and updates its summary state with the returned pricing. The quantity parameter, if present and valid, is used as the default quantity when invoking addPackageToCartFromConfiguration; if absent, a default quantity is used and written back to the URL when the user explicitly edits the quantity. All changes initiated by the user that affect configuration or filters update the appropriate URL parameters without using localStorage, ensuring that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same configuration.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (packageId and optional configuration/filter params), calls getPackageDetails for the specified package, initializes in-memory state for bedrooms, selected add-ons, filters, and quantity, and triggers the first pricing preview using previewPackageConfiguration.

Displays static package details returned by getPackageDetails, such as name, category_name, description, is_virtual, total_hours, session_count, session_length_minutes, and rating information. It also indicates whether the package can be booked or added to the cart using can_be_booked and can_be_added_to_cart.

Allows the user to configure the number of bedrooms (when applicable) using plus/minus buttons or a dropdown, constrained by min_bedrooms and max_bedrooms. If the package is virtual or does not define bedroom-related fields, this control is disabled or hidden. Each valid change updates the URL bedrooms parameter and triggers previewPackageConfiguration.

Manages filter inputs for the Add-On Services list, including a maximum per-add-on price (bounded by addon_price_filter_max from getPackageDetails when available) and optional text-based filters. Changes update the addonMaxPrice and any other filter parameters in the URL and re-compute the filtered visible add-ons client-side, without additional API calls.

Provides an optional control where the user can specify a maximum total amount they wish to spend on selected add-ons. This value is stored in state and in the addonTotalLimit URL parameter, and is used by the add-on selection logic to prevent the total price of selected add-ons from exceeding this limit.

Displays the applicable_addons from getPackageDetails, filtered according to the current filter state from addon-filter-form. Each add-on has a selection control (e.g., checkbox). When the user toggles an add-on, the component proposes an updated selected add-on ID list, temporarily calls previewPackageConfiguration with the new list to obtain the projected addons_total_price, and if a user-defined addonTotalLimit is set and would be exceeded, it cancels the change and shows a validation message. If the change is valid, it commits the new list to state and updates the addonIds URL parameter.

Shows the live pricing and configuration summary based on the latest previewPackageConfiguration response, including bedrooms, selected add-ons (names and prices), addons_total_price, base_price, and total_price. It also displays loading and error states while previewPackageConfiguration requests are in flight or fail.

Provides an explicit “Save Customization” or “Update Package” control that, when used, synchronizes the current configuration (bedrooms, selected add-ons, filters, quantity) to the URL parameters. It ensures that the most recent configuration is also previewed by calling previewPackageConfiguration if any state has changed since the last preview.

Allows the user to set the quantity of the configured package to add to the cart. The selected quantity is reflected in component state and in a quantity URL parameter, and is later passed to addPackageToCartFromConfiguration.

Contains primary actions for the configured package: adding to cart and booking. The “Add Package to Cart” button validates configuration state, ensures a recent pricing preview (calling previewPackageConfiguration if needed), then calls addPackageToCartFromConfiguration with packageId, bedrooms, selectedAddonIds, and quantity. On success it navigates to cart.html; on failure it shows an error. The “Book This Package” button redirects to booking.html?packageId=<currentPackageId>, available only when can_be_booked is true.

Centralizes reading and writing of configuration-related URL parameters. On initialization and whenever the URL changes, it parses packageId, bedrooms, addonIds, addonMaxPrice, addonTotalLimit, and quantity, then updates the relevant components’ state. When components change these values, they call back into this manager, which updates the URL query string (without reloading the page when possible). This ensures that page state is fully represented in the URL, without using localStorage.

Displays non-visual status information such as errors from getPackageDetails, previewPackageConfiguration, and addPackageToCartFromConfiguration, as well as validation messages (e.g., when bedroom selections are out of range or add-on budgets are exceeded). It subscribes to status events from other components and shows or hides messages accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered main column with a strictly top-to-bottom narrative: package title and short intro at the top; immediately beneath, a prominent white configuration card that contains all core controls in logical order (bedroom configuration → filters → budget → add-on selection → quantity). Below this card, a strong, full-width olive band presents a pricing summary as a narrative bridge to checkout/booking. Secondary utilities (sync/state controls) float along the right edge of the viewport, and a persistent action bar anchors key CTAs at the bottom of the screen.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered main column with a strictly top-to-bottom narrative: package title and short intro at the top; immediately beneath, a prominent white configuration card that contains all core controls in logical order (bedroom configuration → filters → budget → add-on selection → quantity). Below this card, a strong, full-width olive band presents a pricing summary as a narrative bridge to checkout/booking. Secondary utilities (sync/state controls) float along the right edge of the viewport, and a persistent action bar anchors key CTAs at the bottom of the screen.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain the main content to roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (equivalent to ~8–10 columns of a 12-column grid), centered with generous side margins (~10–15% per side). The white configuration card occupies most of this content width but never goes full-bleed to the browser edge. The olive pricing band is visually full-width relative to the main content area, with increased vertical padding to feel banner-like. Floating controls on the right occupy a narrow vertical strip (about 10–15% width) and stay out of the main reading column. Vertical spacing between major blocks follows the 32–48px rhythm to keep sections clearly separated yet cohesive.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium density: each configuration group inside the card has clear breathing room above and below (16–24px), form controls are not tightly stacked, and the add-on list uses generous line height and 8–16px vertical spacing between items. Typography is larger than default with comfortable line spacing, making the relatively detailed configuration feel like a marketing/editorial experience rather than a dense form.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the package header (with red accent text), then flows naturally into the large white card. Inside the card, the flow is linear and predictable: top-to-bottom through each configuration section. The add-on list uses left-aligned chevron icons and bold keywords to create a clear vertical scanning path. After the last configuration element, the olive pricing band provides a strong horizontal visual anchor that signals a transition toward commitment (price and next steps). Floating controls on the right and the sticky action bar at the bottom are peripheral but always available, never interrupting the central reading/configuration flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a centered, marketing-style configurator for a decluttering package. Against a warm, subtly textured background, a single main column sits in the middle of the viewport with wide side margins. At the top of this column, a bold red-accented package title and short description establish context. Just below, any alerts appear, and then a large white card dominates the center of the page: this is the configuration canvas. Within this card, sections are stacked in a clear vertical sequence—from bedroom count to add-on filters, budget, and a comfortably spaced bullet-style list of services—culminating in a quantity control near the bottom. A narrow strip along the right edge of the viewport holds floating round buttons for saving and syncing the configuration, visible but unobtrusive. Immediately beneath the card, a full-width olive band stretches across the content area with centered, uppercase pricing summary text that feels like a decisive, banner-like statement. Finally, along the bottom of the viewport, a sticky action bar spans the width with the key CTAs and live price, ensuring that adding to cart or proceeding to booking is always just one click away. The overall impression is calm, guided, and editorial, with strong vertical flow and clear, spacious grouping of decisions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the structural page wrapper rather than a visible UI element. It establishes a full-viewport background in a warm, off-white or lightly textured tone, and centers the main content column within the browser window. It defines a max content width of roughly 1100–1200px and applies horizontal padding of about 32px on smaller screens so the main column never touches the edges. It also reserves vertical space at the top (around 64px) beneath the site header so that the package information and main card have generous breathing room before any content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page wrapper that controls background, max-width, and global padding for all other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the top of the main content column, this header panel spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width and is centered in the page, aligned with the later white configuration card. The text within is left-aligned to reinforce the editorial feel. It includes a small red-accented label or eyebrow at the very top, a large, bold package name beneath it, and a brief supporting description or subtitle below, each separated by about 8–16px. There is generous whitespace above this panel (about 48–64px from the browser’s visible header) and about 24–32px of space below it before the next element, allowing it to act as a clear entry point into the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored at the top of the main column and scrolling away naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This panel sits directly below the package-info-panel, within the same centered column and width constraints. When visible, it appears as a full-width strip relative to the column—about 70–80% of the viewport width—using a soft colored background (e.g., light red for errors, light olive or blue for info) and subtle rounded corners to differentiate it from both the textured page background and the white configuration card below. It has modest internal padding (about 16–24px) and stacks messages in a vertical list with 8–12px spacing between lines. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space between this panel and the top of the main configuration card that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a full row in the vertical flow when messages are present; completely collapses out of the layout when there are no messages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms the top section of a large central white card. The card itself appears as a single, cohesive surface that will visually include this and several subsequent controls: it is centered, spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, and has 32–40px of padding on all sides with a subtle shadow and slightly rounded corners. Within the card, the bedroom-config-control occupies the full internal width. It presents a section title (e.g., ‘Bedrooms to Organize’) in a medium-large font at the top of the card content, with controls (such as steppers, toggles, or pills) arranged in a single row or simple grid beneath, left-aligned. Vertical spacing within this section follows a 16–24px rhythm, and there is a clear 24px gap below it before the next configuration section starts, reinforcing its role as the starting point of the configuration flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acts as the first block inside a larger, visually unified configuration card that scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Placed immediately below the bedroom-config-control within the same white configuration card, this component spans the full internal width of the card. It reads as a compact filter bar or mini-form, with a small label aligned to the left and interactive filter controls (chips, dropdowns, toggles) arranged horizontally where space allows, wrapping to multiple lines on smaller screens. There is about 24px of vertical separation from the bedroom section above and 16–24px of internal padding above/below its contents to keep it airy. The left edges of its labels and fields align precisely with the left edge of the bedroom-config section’s text to maintain a consistent visual column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, embedded as the second section within the vertically stacked content of the main configuration card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This budget control follows directly under the addon-filter-form within the same card, maintaining the single-column, top-to-bottom structure. It uses the full internal card width for a slider or input row that feels substantial but not heavy: a label and current budget value aligned along the left, with a horizontal slider stretching toward the right side of the card. The component has about 16px of padding above and below and is separated from the filters by roughly 24px of vertical space. The overall visual impression is of a clear step in the flow—filter first, then budget—without any change to the overarching card boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, a full-width row within the main configuration card, adjusting its internal layout responsively but keeping its place in the linear flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component occupies the largest vertical portion of the white configuration card and visually echoes the bullet-list style from the original mockup. It sits below the budget control, with about 24–32px of vertical separation. The list spans the full internal width of the card and presents each add-on as a horizontal row: a chevron or caret icon on the far left, followed by the add-on title and description to the right. Bold, colored keywords (e.g., in red or orange) within the text help scanning, while secondary details appear in a lighter, smaller type. Each row has comfortable vertical padding (8–16px) and a subtle divider or extra space (4–8px) between items, creating a vertically flowing list that is easy to skim. The left edges of all chevrons align with each other, creating a clean visual rail that guides the eye down the list. Any per-item controls (checkboxes, toggles) sit toward the right edge of each row, keeping the text column uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the central, scrollable content block within the configuration card and expanding vertically as more add-ons are available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned near the bottom of the white configuration card, this component appears after the add-on list with about 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the final list item. The quantity selector is horizontally compact but visually important, aligning its label on the left and the interactive control (stepper, dropdown, or number input) to the right within the same row. On wider screens, this row can align the control to the right edge of the card, creating a subtle sense of a summary row, while the label stays aligned with the main text column. There is 16–24px of padding above and below this row so it doesn’t feel cramped at the bottom of the card, and a final 24–32px of padding between this component and the card’s lower edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sits as the final decision control inside the main configuration card, just above the transition to the pricing summary band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component becomes the prominent colored band that appears directly below the white configuration card, echoing the olive-green bridge section from the mockup. It stretches across the same content width as the card but visually feels full-width relative to the page: its background color extends to the edges of the main column, and on larger screens can lightly bleed to the viewport edges if desired. The panel uses generous vertical padding (24–40px) to feel substantial. The core pricing text is centered horizontally, with a small uppercase label above (e.g., ‘YOUR ESTIMATED INVESTMENT’) and the main price or summary line in a larger, bold type below, all in high-contrast white or very light text. On desktop, the price can be centered; on narrower screens, it can remain centered or align left while still sitting in the center of the page. There is about 32px of margin above (separating it from the bottom of the white card) and 32–48px below, before any subsequent content or page footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, a full-width horizontal band within the main content flow that scrolls normally, acting as a visual and narrative bridge from configuration to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: These controls appear as a slim vertical cluster of floating, circular buttons along the right side of the viewport, visually similar to floating action buttons (FABs). They sit roughly in the vertical middle of the screen (or slightly above center) and are horizontally aligned so that they overlap none of the main content column. On typical desktops, they occupy the rightmost 10–15% of the viewport, with about 16–24px of space between the edge of the main card and the buttons. Each button is around 40–48px in diameter, with 8–12px spacing between them, using a bright accent color (e.g., blue) to stand out but remain small enough not to compete with the primary content. The cluster remains visible as the user scrolls, allowing quick actions such as resetting or copying the configuration without interrupting reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the right side of the viewport (floating), maintaining its position relative to the screen as the user scrolls, and only adjusting or hiding on very small screens to avoid overlapping core content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms a sticky horizontal bar at the bottom of the viewport, spanning the full width of the main content area and visually anchoring the CTAs. On desktop it may align with the main content width but can visually stretch full-bleed to the viewport edges for a stronger presence. The bar has a slightly elevated style (subtle shadow, solid background color) and about 16–24px of vertical padding. Inside, content is arranged left-to-right: on the left, a concise line showing the current total price and key summary detail; on the right, prominent primary and secondary buttons (e.g., ‘Add to Cart’ and ‘Book Now’) sized comfortably with about 12–16px of spacing between them. The bar appears above the browser’s bottom edge and remains fixed as the user scrolls, with enough top margin (8–12px) so it doesn’t feel glued to the edge. On mobile, it compresses gracefully, keeping the primary action centered or right-aligned and ensuring legible tap targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport, always visible while the page is scrolled, ensuring CTAs and key pricing information are persistently accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This budget control follows directly under the addon-filter-form within the same card, maintaining the single-column, top-to-bottom structure. It uses the full internal card width for a slider or input row that feels substantial but not heavy: a label and current budget value aligned along the left, with a horizontal slider stretching toward the right side of the card. The component has about 16px of padding above and below and is separated from the filters by roughly 24px of vertical space. The overall visual impression is of a clear step in the flow—filter first, then budget—without any change to the overarching card boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, a full-width row within the main configuration card, adjusting its internal layout responsively but keeping its place in the linear flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no direct visual manifestation. It conceptually sits behind the main content, synchronizing the current configuration (bedrooms, filters, budget, selected add-ons, and quantity) with the URL or application state. It does not occupy any physical space on the page and does not modify the visual layout, but its presence ensures that the layout can be restored or shared consistently.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial, operating purely in the background without affecting layout or scroll behavior.

name: Shop Products
title: Shop Products
description: Browse, filter, and sort physical organizing products and storage solutions, with all selections kept in the URL for easy sharing and navigation.

core_features: Load and display product categories using getProductCategories so users can browse products by category, with an optional preselected category from the URL parameter 'categoryId'.

Retrieve dynamic filter ranges and options for the current category using getProductFilterOptions, and render filter controls for price, capacity, rating, color, material, style, and shipping-related options.

Run product searches using searchProducts, driven entirely by the current URL parameters (categoryId, minPrice, maxPrice, minRating, minCapacityLiters, color, material, style, freeShippingOnly, sortBy), and render the resulting product list.
Allow users to adjust filters and sorting; every change updates the URL query parameters and triggers a new searchProducts call so that the visible results always match the URL state.
Provide sorting controls that map to the 'sortBy' parameter used by searchProducts, so users can reorder results (for example by price or rating, using values supported by the backend).
Render clickable product cards based on searchProducts results; clicking a card navigates to product_detail.html with the appropriate productId in the URL.
Support direct, deep-linked filtered views: when the page loads, it reads all incoming URL parameters, applies them to UI components, and uses them when calling searchProducts.
Handle empty-result states when searchProducts returns no products, with an option to clear or relax filters (via URL parameter updates) to broaden the search.
user_workflows: Workflow: Landing on the Shop page from navigation without filters. 1) User clicks the 'Shop Products' link in the site header (no specific query parameters). 2) Page controller reads the URL (no categoryId or filters present). 3) Page calls getProductCategories to retrieve available categories. 4) The page sets a default categoryId based on the first or otherwise preferred category from getProductCategories. 5) Page calls getProductFilterOptions with that categoryId to get filter ranges and options. 6) The page initializes URL parameters for filters and sorting if missing (for example, using the full price/capacity ranges from getProductFilterOptions and a default sortBy value). 7) Page calls searchProducts with the resolved parameters. 8) Category selector, filter sidebar, sort controls, and product grid render using the data from these calls.

Workflow: Arriving via a deep link with predefined filters. 1) User opens a URL to shop.html that includes some combination of categoryId, minPrice, maxPrice, minRating, minCapacityLiters, color, style, freeShippingOnly, and sortBy. 2) Page controller parses all query parameters and uses categoryId (if present) as the active category. 3) Page calls getProductCategories and aligns the active categoryId to one of the returned categories (if it does not match, a fallback category is chosen and the URL is updated). 4) Page calls getProductFilterOptions with the active categoryId to retrieve allowed ranges and options. 5) The controller validates URL filter values against the allowed ranges/options and normalizes them if needed (for example, clamping prices within the min/max allowed by getProductFilterOptions); any normalization is reflected back into the URL. 6) Page calls searchProducts using the validated parameters. 7) Filters, category selector, and sort controls show the current active values from the URL, and the product grid displays the filtered results.

Workflow: User changes category and then adjusts filters. 1) User selects a different category in the category selector. 2) Category selector notifies the page controller, which updates the categoryId parameter in the URL (replacing any existing categoryId). 3) Page calls getProductFilterOptions with the new categoryId to retrieve filter ranges/options for that category. 4) Existing filter URL parameters are re-evaluated against the new filter options; incompatible values are removed or adjusted and the URL is updated. 5) Page calls searchProducts with the new categoryId and the remaining compatible filters. 6) User adjusts filters in the filter sidebar (for example price, rating, capacity, color, material, style, shipping toggle). 7) Each filter change immediately updates the corresponding URL parameter(s) and triggers a new searchProducts call with the updated parameters. 8) The product grid and result summary update to reflect the refined results.

Workflow: User changes sorting to re-order results. 1) After results are visible, user opens the sort control and chooses a sort option (mapped to a sortBy value supported by searchProducts). 2) Sort-bar component notifies the page controller. 3) Controller updates the 'sortBy' parameter in the URL. 4) Controller calls searchProducts again with all current filters and the new sortBy. 5) Product grid re-renders in the new order while category and filters stay unchanged.

Workflow: Opening a product detail page from the listing. 1) User scans the product grid and clicks on a product card. 2) Product-card component triggers navigation to product_detail.html, adding a productId parameter set to the product_id from searchProducts for that card. 3) Browser navigates to product_detail.html?productId={product_id}, where that page handles loading and purchase flows. 4) If user later returns to shop.html using the browser back button, the URL (with category and filters) is unchanged, so the shop page reloads the same state and re-calls searchProducts with the same parameters.

Workflow: Sharing a filtered product view. 1) User refines the product list by selecting category, filters, and a sort option. 2) The page continually updates the browser URL query parameters to reflect the current state after each change. 3) User copies the page URL from the browser address bar and shares it. 4) Recipient opens the shared URL; the shop page parses all parameters and repeats the deep-link workflow (calling getProductCategories, getProductFilterOptions, and searchProducts with those parameters). 5) Recipient sees the same filtered and sorted results as the original user, subject to any data changes on the backend.

Workflow: Recovering from an empty result set. 1) User selects a combination of filters that produces no results from searchProducts. 2) Controller detects that searchProducts returned an empty array. 3) Empty-state component is displayed with messaging that no products match the current filters and with actions such as clearing all filters or reverting selected filters. 4) When the user clicks 'clear filters' (or similar), the component clears filter-related URL parameters while keeping categoryId (and optionally sortBy) intact and notifies the controller. 5) Controller performs a new searchProducts call with the simplified parameters and the product grid updates with broader results.
interactions: Interaction: Initial page load. Event handler onInitialLoad in the page controller reads URL parameters, calls getProductCategories, derives the initial categoryId, then calls getProductFilterOptions and searchProducts; it then pushes any normalized parameter values back to the URL if needed.

Interaction: Browser back/forward navigation. An onUrlChange handler (listening to the popstate event) re-parses all URL parameters when the history state changes, updates local UI state (category selector, filter sidebar, sort-bar), and re-invokes searchProducts (and getProductFilterOptions if the categoryId changed) so the view reflects the navigated state.

Interaction: Category selection. When a user selects a category in the category-selector component, onCategoryChange updates the categoryId in the URL, resets or validates filters as appropriate for the new category, calls getProductFilterOptions for that category, and then calls searchProducts with the updated parameters.

Interaction: Price filter change. When a user adjusts the price filter controls in the filter-sidebar component, onFilterChange updates minPrice and/or maxPrice in the URL and triggers a new searchProducts call with the updated values.
Interaction: Rating filter change. Selecting a rating threshold updates the minRating parameter in the URL via onFilterChange and triggers a new searchProducts call.
Interaction: Capacity filter change. Adjusting the capacity control updates minCapacityLiters in the URL via onFilterChange and triggers a new searchProducts call.

Interaction: Color, material, and style changes. Selecting a color, material, or style option updates the corresponding 'color', 'material', or 'style' parameters in the URL and triggers a new searchProducts call.
