Interaction: Shipping-related filter change. Toggling a shipping-related filter (for example, free-shipping-only) updates the 'freeShippingOnly' parameter in the URL (to a boolean representation appropriate for the URL format) and triggers a new searchProducts call.

Interaction: Sort change. When the user changes the sort selection, onSortChange updates the 'sortBy' parameter in the URL and triggers a new searchProducts call with all current filters and the new sortBy value.

Interaction: Clear all filters. When user triggers a clear-filters action, onFiltersReset removes all filter-related parameters (such as minPrice, maxPrice, minRating, minCapacityLiters, color, material, style, freeShippingOnly) from the URL while keeping categoryId (and optionally sortBy), then calls searchProducts with the simplified parameter set.
Interaction: Product card click. When a user clicks a product card, onProductClick reads the product_id from that card and navigates to 'product_detail.html?productId={product_id}'.

Interaction: Header cart icon click. When user clicks the cart icon in the header, onCartIconClick navigates to 'cart.html' without changing shop-specific URL parameters; cart functionality is handled on the cart page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query string is the single source of truth for the Shop Products page state. On every page load or history navigation, the controller parses the query parameters categoryId, minPrice, maxPrice, minRating, minCapacityLiters, color, material, style, freeShippingOnly, and sortBy. First, getProductCategories is called to retrieve all categories. The controller validates any provided categoryId against these categories; if the provided categoryId does not match, a fallback categoryId is chosen (for example, the first category), and the URL is updated accordingly. With a valid categoryId, the controller calls getProductFilterOptions(categoryId) to obtain the allowed filter ranges and available discrete options (price_min/price_max, capacity_min/capacity_max, rating_options, color_options, material_options, style_options, and shipping_filters). The controller then reconciles the URL parameters for filters with these options: numeric values such as minPrice, maxPrice, and minCapacityLiters are clamped within the ranges from getProductFilterOptions; discrete values such as color, material, and style are only accepted if they exist in the corresponding options arrays; boolean values like freeShippingOnly are normalized to a consistent representation. Any adjustments are written back to the URL, ensuring that the URL always reflects a valid state. After the state is validated, searchProducts is called with exactly the current URL-derived parameters (categoryId, minPrice, maxPrice, minRating, minCapacityLiters, color, material, style, freeShippingOnly, sortBy). The response is used to render the product grid and to compute a result summary. Whenever the user interacts with filters, category selection, or sorting, the controller updates the relevant URL parameters and then re-invokes searchProducts (and getProductFilterOptions if the categoryId changed). There is no use of localStorage or other persistent client storage; the full state is reproducible from the URL plus the data returned by the assigned interfaces. Browser back/forward navigation is handled by listening to URL changes and re-running the same URL-parse and API-call flow, preserving navigable history of filter/sort states.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes the page, parses and updates URL parameters, orchestrates calls to getProductCategories, getProductFilterOptions, and searchProducts, and distributes the resulting data and state to child components.

Encapsulates reading from and writing to the URL query string. It exposes methods for getting and setting categoryId, filter parameters, and sortBy, and it emits events when the URL changes (such as via the browser back/forward buttons).

Displays available product categories retrieved via getProductCategories and allows the user to select a single active category; the selected category determines which filters are available and which products are shown.

Renders filter controls for the current category based on the metadata returned by getProductFilterOptions (price range, capacity range, rating options, color options, material options, style options, and shipping-related filters). When the user changes a filter, it updates the corresponding URL parameters via the controller and triggers a new product search.

Provides a control to select the sort order of the product results. Options map to the 'sortBy' values accepted by searchProducts. Changing the selection updates the URL parameter sortBy and triggers a new product search.

Shows a concise summary of the current results and filter state, such as the number of products returned by searchProducts and a textual representation of key active filters (for example, price range and selected attributes), all derived from the controller's state.

Displays a grid or list of product-card components based on the array returned by searchProducts. It also manages client-side pagination or 'load more' behavior if needed, without additional API parameters.

Represents a single product in the searchProducts results. It displays product fields such as name, category_name, price, capacity_liters, color, material, style, average_rating, review_count, free_shipping, and image_url, and handles click navigation to the product_detail.html page with the correct productId.
Shown when searchProducts returns no products. Provides messaging about the lack of results and actions like clearing filters or adjusting them, which modify URL parameters through the controller.
Displays a loading state while getProductCategories, getProductFilterOptions, or searchProducts calls are in progress, and hides when data is ready.
Part of the shared site header; on this page, it provides navigation to cart.html when the user clicks the cart icon, without altering the shop page's filter or sort state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strong, centered, top-to-bottom narrative similar to the mockup. A bold page heading and brief intro (outside these components) sit at the top, followed immediately by a large, central white "control card" that contains category selection and filters in a vertical flow. Below this card, a full-width olive-green band bridges into the browsing experience by summarizing results and exposing sort controls. Under that band, the product grid fills the main central area. Structural components (page controller, URL manager) are invisible, and the header cart link stays in the top-right as a persistent, secondary action.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strong, centered, top-to-bottom narrative similar to the mockup. A bold page heading and brief intro (outside these components) sit at the top, followed immediately by a large, central white "control card" that contains category selection and filters in a vertical flow. Below this card, a full-width olive-green band bridges into the browsing experience by summarizing results and exposing sort controls. Under that band, the product grid fills the main central area. Structural components (page controller, URL manager) are invisible, and the header cart link stays in the top-right as a persistent, secondary action.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A single main content column is centered on the page, roughly 70%–80% of the viewport width (equivalent to ~8–10 columns in a 12-column grid), with generous left/right margins. The white filter card uses ample padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal) and occupies most of the content column width, but not full-bleed. The olive band spans the same content width edge-to-edge, reading like a strong full-width strip. The product grid fills the central column below with equal left/right gutters. Floating elements and header controls occupy minimal width on the right edge, never disrupting the main reading column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls and text are spaced generously, matching a marketing/editorial feel rather than a dense ecommerce dashboard. Filter options are stacked with 8–16px vertical gaps, grouped inside a roomy card. Typography for headings and summaries is large with relaxed line height, while labels and filter controls remain comfortably spaced and easy to scan. Product cards are airy, with clear separation between image, text, and buttons. Even when many products are shown, the grid preserves breathing room around each tile.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the page heading (in a strong accent color) and immediately flows down into the large white filter card. Inside this card, the flow runs from the category selector at the top through the stacked filter options below. After exiting the card, the contrasting olive-green band pulls the eye downward and anchors the sense of context via the result summary and sort controls. From there, the gaze naturally continues down into the product grid, scanning left-to-right, row by row. The header cart link and any floating behaviors sit on the right periphery, acting as secondary attractors without competing with the vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description: The Shop Products page presents as a warm, marketing-style browsing experience framed around a strong central column. At the top, within a softly textured background, a bold, centered page title introduces the shop. Directly beneath, a large white card floats in the middle of the viewport with generous margins on both sides; this card holds the category selector at the top and a vertical stack of filters below, echoing the mockup’s chevron-list structure with clear left alignment and ample spacing. After a comfortable slice of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band stretches across the same central width, containing an uppercase results summary and tidy sort controls that feel like a narrative bridge between choosing filters and seeing products. Below this band, the product grid occupies the main central area, with rows of clean, white product cards set in a responsive grid that never touches the very edges of the viewport. Throughout, the vertical flow is uninterrupted: heading → filter card → olive band → grid. Peripheral elements like the cart link remain tucked into the top-right and optionally as a small floating button on scroll, staying out of the primary reading path while remaining easy to access.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This invisible wrapper component spans the full viewport width and height and defines the main content column. It centers all primary content within a max-width container (around 1040–1200px, equivalent to ~8–10 of 12 columns) with automatic left/right margins. It applies generous vertical padding at the top and bottom of the page (48–64px) and a softly textured, light neutral background behind the central white card, olive band, and grid, mirroring the homey marketing style. All visible components on the page sit inside this controller’s central column, ensuring a consistent single-column reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that governs the central alignment, max-width, and vertical spacing for the entire page. It scrolls with the page and does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visible UI; it lives in the background of the central content column. Conceptually, it is positioned near the top of the main content stack, behind the filter card and sort band, capturing changes from category selection, filters, and sorting. It occupies no measurable width or height and does not impact spacing; the layout around it is as if it isn’t physically present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-interactive layer; it does not participate in layout and simply tracks state as the user scrolls and interacts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category selector sits at the very top of the large central white card, directly under the page heading and within the main content column. It is centered horizontally and spans roughly 60%–70% of the card’s width, leaving comfortable whitespace on both sides. Vertically, it uses 16–24px of padding above and below, separating it from the card’s edges and the filters underneath. On desktop, category options appear in a single horizontal row of pills or a wide dropdown, aligned centrally, with 8–16px gaps between options. On smaller screens, it reflows into a stacked or scrollable horizontal arrangement, but always remains visually at the top of the card, acting as the first decision point before diving into filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filter card; it scrolls naturally with the page. On smaller viewports it may center and wrap or transform into a full-width dropdown, but always retains its position at the top of the white card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Despite the name, the filter sidebar is rendered as a vertical, in-card filter panel directly beneath the category selector, maintaining the mockup’s single-column character. It occupies the full inner width of the white card (minus 32–40px horizontal padding on each side), with filter groups stacked top-to-bottom. Each filter group reads like a bullet row: a small icon or marker on the far left and the filter label and controls immediately to its right, aligned in a simple two-column row within that group. Individual filters and groups have 8–16px vertical spacing between them, ensuring a spacious, scannable list. The whole filter area consumes roughly the lower two-thirds of the card’s height, with a clear bottom margin (24–32px) before the card’s edge, so it still feels airy. On mobile, it may collapse into accordions, but it remains in this same vertical position below the category selector.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content within the white card. On taller pages, it scrolls away with the card; it is not sticky or fixed. On small screens, filter sections may collapse/expand, but their overall container remains in-line in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort bar is placed within the olive-green horizontal band that sits immediately beneath the white filter card. Visually, the band spans the full width of the central content column and has generous vertical padding (24–32px top and bottom). Inside this band, the sort bar aligns centrally beneath or alongside the result summary. On wider screens, the summary can be centered at the top of the band with the sort control directly below it, also centered, occupying about 40%–50% of the band’s width. Alternatively, on large desktops, the summary might align slightly left of center and the sort control slightly right while still feeling part of one cohesive strip. The sort control itself is a compact, pill-like dropdown or segmented control that contrasts clearly against the olive background but doesn’t overpower the summary text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the olive band; scrolls with the page. It adapts responsively—stacked vertically with the summary on narrow screens, potentially in a single row with the summary on wider screens—but always remains inside the same horizontal band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result summary is the primary text content within the olive-green band beneath the filter card. It appears as a single, uppercase line (e.g., “SHOWING 24 STORAGE SOLUTIONS”), centered horizontally within the band. The text is large and bold enough to be easily read at a glance, spanning roughly 50%–70% of the content width but never touching the band’s edges. There is comfortable padding above it (16–24px) from the top of the band and similar spacing below before the sort bar, creating a strong narrative bridge between ‘choosing filters’ and ‘viewing results’. On smaller screens, it may occupy two lines but remains centered and visually dominant within the band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text inside the olive band; scrolls with the page. Its position relative to the sort bar (above or left of it) adjusts responsively, but it always remains within this single full-width strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product grid is positioned directly beneath the olive band, separated by a comfortable slice of white or neutral background space (~32–48px vertical gap) to reflect a new but closely related section. It occupies the same central content column used by the filter card—about 70%–80% of the viewport width with generous side margins. On desktop, the grid arranges products in 3–4 columns depending on screen width, with equal column widths and 24–32px gutters between cards both horizontally and vertically. The grid’s top aligns with the center of the page, and it grows downward as more products are loaded, always maintaining a spacious, editorial feel. On smaller viewports, it gracefully collapses to 2 columns and eventually 1 column while preserving generous spacing around each item.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable grid section. It extends vertically as many rows as needed and scrolls naturally; no fixed or sticky behavior is applied to the grid itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product card is a contained, white tile within the product grid. Cards are rectangular with a slightly rounded corner radius and subtle shadow or border, echoing the main filter card but at a smaller scale. A product image occupies the top portion of the card, spanning the full card width, followed by stacked text content—product name, short descriptor, and price—aligned left. At the bottom, action elements (e.g., add-to-cart button) align left or stretch full width, with 16–24px padding between content sections. Overall card padding is 16–24px on all sides, and there is consistent 24–32px spacing between adjacent cards in the grid. Cards feel airy rather than dense, with line heights and margins tuned for quick scanning rather than compact data display.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static blocks inside the grid. They expand and contract responsively with the grid columns but maintain fixed internal padding and vertical stacking order. No card is fixed or sticky; they scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no products match the selected filters, the empty state occupies the same vertical region as the product grid, centered within the main content column. It appears as a medium-sized white card floating on the neutral background, mirroring the style of the top filter card but slightly smaller, spanning roughly 50%–60% of the content width and centered horizontally. Inside, an illustration or icon appears at the top center, followed by a short heading and explanatory text, all center-aligned with generous vertical spacing (16–24px between elements). The card has 24–32px padding on all sides and ample space (48–64px) above and below it relative to the olive band and page bottom, creating an open, reassuring message that the user can adjust filters. It temporarily replaces the product grid region so the layout remains simple and single-column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and in-line: it appears exactly where the grid would be and scrolls with the page. When active, the grid is hidden, so the empty state becomes the primary visual element in the results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator sits on top of the product grid area (or empty-state area) as a lightweight overlay or inline element. In its overlay form, it centers a spinner or subtle progress indicator horizontally within the main content column, positioned roughly in the upper third of the visible grid area so it’s immediately visible without feeling like a full-screen takeover. The background behind it may be slightly dimmed or left as-is to keep the experience light. In inline mode, it appears directly below the olive band and above the first row of content, centered, with 16–24px vertical padding. The indicator itself is compact and minimally styled to avoid clashing with the warm, domestic brand feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or occupies space within the results area. It is not fixed to the viewport; it remains anchored to the grid region and scrolls away if the user scrolls while loading is in progress.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header cart link lives in the global site header at the very top of the page, horizontally aligned to the far right edge of the main content width. It appears as a small pill or icon-plus-text button (e.g., cart icon with item count), with modest padding (8–12px) and clear contrast against the header background, but smaller and less visually dominant than the main page heading. Vertically, it aligns with the header’s baseline and sits well above the central white card, so it doesn’t interfere with the main top-to-bottom narrative. As the user scrolls, the header may remain sticky, keeping the cart link anchored at the top-right. Optionally, to echo the mockup’s floating FABs, a small circular cart icon can appear as a floating button along the right side of the viewport, roughly mid-height, occupying minimal space and staying out of the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily sticky within the global header, remaining visible at the top-right as the page scrolls. If a floating FAB variant is used, that icon is fixed to the right edge of the viewport, offset slightly inward, and does not affect the layout of the central content column.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single shop product, lets the user configure variant and quantity, shows price and shipping details, and adds the configured item to the cart.

core_features: Load detailed product data (including variants) using getProductDetails(productId) based on the productId URL parameter.
Pre-select a specific product variant using the optional variantId URL parameter or fall back to the default variant when no variantId is provided.

Display key product details including title, category, description, price, capacity in liters, dimensions, material, style, rating, review count, image, and shipping details (including whether shipping is free).
Allow the user to select among available variants (e.g., different colors or materials) and ensure the selected variant stays consistent with any variantId in the URL.
Allow the user to adjust quantity and dynamically calculate and display the line item subtotal based on the selected variant’s effective unit price.
Provide an Add to Cart form that validates configuration (product, variant, quantity) and calls addProductToCart(productId, variantId, quantity).
On successful add-to-cart, navigate the user to cart.html to review their cart contents.
user_workflows:
Landing on a Product from the Shop:
1. User clicks a product tile on shop.html.
2. Browser opens product_detail.html with productId (and optionally variantId) in the query string.
3. The page reads productId (and variantId, if present) from the URL.
4. The product-detail-loader component calls getProductDetails(productId).
5. Once data loads, the page derives the initially selected variant (matching variantId if valid, otherwise the default variant or the base product if no variants exist).
6. The product information, specifications, rating, price, shipping details, and image are displayed.
7. The variant-selector (if variants exist) shows the available options with the URL-specified or default variant pre-selected.
8. The quantity-selector initializes with a default quantity and the price-and-shipping-summary shows the current unit price and calculated line subtotal.

Changing Variant:
1. User changes the variant selection in the variant-selector component.
2. The component updates the selectedVariantId in page state.
3. The state logic updates the URL query string to reflect the new variantId without reloading the page (e.g., using history APIs), so the current configuration is shareable.
4. The price-and-shipping-summary recomputes the effective unit price (using variant.price_override when present, otherwise the base product price).
5. The shipping display recalculates any free-shipping indicator using variant.free_shipping_override when present, otherwise product.free_shipping.
6. The line item subtotal is recalculated as unit price × current quantity.

Adjusting Quantity and Reviewing Subtotal:
1. User clicks plus/minus controls or edits the numeric quantity input within the quantity-selector.
2. The quantity-selector validates that quantity is a positive whole number; invalid values are corrected according to business rules (e.g., resetting to a minimum of 1).
3. The updated quantity is stored in page state.
4. The price-and-shipping-summary listens for quantity changes and recalculates the line item subtotal in real time.
5. The user reviews updated subtotal and shipping information before adding the item to the cart.

Adding Configured Product to Cart:
1. User confirms variant and quantity, then clicks the Add to Cart button in the add-to-cart-form.
2. The add-to-cart-form validates that product details are loaded, a variant is selected (if products have variants), and quantity is valid.
3. On successful validation, the form calls addProductToCart(productId, selectedVariantId (or null/undefined if no variants), quantity).
4. The page waits for the response from addProductToCart.
5. If the response indicates success, the page redirects the user to cart.html.
6. If the response indicates failure, an error message from the API is displayed inside the add-to-cart-form without navigating away, allowing the user to retry.

Revisiting a Product with a Saved URL:
1. User opens or pastes a previously copied URL to product_detail.html that includes productId and variantId.
2. The page reads both parameters and calls getProductDetails(productId).
3. After loading, the page verifies that the variantId from the URL belongs to the product’s variants.
4. If valid, that variant is set as the selected variant; otherwise, the page falls back to the product’s default variant.
5. All price, shipping, and subtotal calculations reflect the selected (or fallback) variant so the user sees the configuration intended by the URL.
interactions:
Page Load Interaction:
- Trigger: On initial page load or when the productId URL parameter changes (e.g., via navigation).
- Behavior: The product-detail-loader reads productId (and optional variantId) from the query string and calls getProductDetails(productId). All other components remain in a loading, disabled, or hidden state until the data is returned.

Variant Selection Interaction:
- Trigger: User selects a variant option in the variant-selector (e.g., via radio buttons, dropdown, or list).
- Behavior: The selectedVariantId in state updates. The URL’s variantId parameter is updated to the new variantId without a full reload. price-and-shipping-summary recalculates the unit price, free-shipping status, and line subtotal. The add-to-cart-form uses the new selectedVariantId in future addProductToCart calls.

Quantity Change Interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks increment/decrement buttons or types a value into the quantity input.
- Behavior: quantity is validated as a positive integer and stored in state. price-and-shipping-summary recalculates the line subtotal. The add-to-cart-form uses the updated quantity when submitting.

Add to Cart Interaction:
- Trigger: User submits the add-to-cart-form (e.g., by clicking Add to Cart).
- Behavior: Form validates presence of productId, selectedVariantId (if required), and quantity. If valid, it calls addProductToCart with the current configuration. While the request is pending, the button is disabled and may show a loading state. On success, the page navigates to cart.html. On failure, an error message is displayed and the button is re-enabled.

Error Handling Interaction (Product Load):
- Trigger: getProductDetails(productId) returns an error or empty result.
- Behavior: The product-detail-loader sets an error state. The page shows a functional error message indicating the product cannot be loaded and hides or disables configuration and Add to Cart interactions.

Error Handling Interaction (Add to Cart):
- Trigger: addProductToCart returns success: false or fails.
- Behavior: The add-to-cart-form displays the returned message or a generic error indicator. The user remains on product_detail.html and can adjust variant/quantity or retry submitting.

Navigation Interaction (Back to Shop or Category):
- Trigger: User clicks a category or back link (e.g., from breadcrumb-nav).
- Behavior: Browser navigates to shop.html or another configured list page, optionally including category or filter parameters. This interaction does not use additional APIs from product_detail.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL parameters and data returned from getProductDetails. On initial load, the page reads productId from the query string; if productId is missing or invalid, the page sets an error state and does not attempt to load product data. When productId is present, the page calls getProductDetails(productId) and stores the returned product object (including variants) in memory. The optional variantId parameter is also read from the URL. After product data loads, the page attempts to match the URL's variantId to one of the variants in product.variants. If a match is found, selectedVariantId is set to that variant; if not, selectedVariantId is set to the variant flagged as is_default in the variants array, or to a null/undefined value when no variants exist. When the user changes the variant through the variant-selector, the state updates selectedVariantId and also updates the URL's variantId parameter using the browser history API, so reloading or sharing the URL preserves the user's selection. Quantity is held in client state and may optionally be reflected in the URL via an additional query parameter if desired by implementation, but this is not required. The current unit price is computed from either the selected variant's price_override (when provided) or the base product price from getProductDetails. The free-shipping indicator is computed from either the selected variant's free_shipping_override (when provided) or the base product's free_shipping flag. The line item subtotal is derived dynamically as unit_price × quantity and is not stored in any persistent model. No state is stored in localStorage; all sharable configuration is represented via URL parameters and recalculated from API responses when the page loads.

components.functionality: Reads the productId and optional variantId from the URL, invokes getProductDetails(productId), and initializes page-level state including the resolved selectedVariantId and any error/loading flags.

Provides functional links back to higher-level pages (e.g., shop listing or product category) using data from the loaded product (such as category_name) and the browser history; does not call any additional APIs.
Displays core product information such as name, category_name, description, average_rating, review_count, and image_url. It updates automatically when product-detail-loader provides new product data.
Shows the primary product image using image_url from getProductDetails. It can respond to product changes (e.g., if a different productId is loaded) and update the displayed image accordingly.
Displays technical and functional product details such as capacity_liters, dimensions, material, style, and shipping_weight. It only presents fields that are available in the product data.

Renders a list or dropdown of available product variants based on product.variants from getProductDetails. Pre-selects the variant resolved by state logic (URL variantId or default). When the user selects a different variant, it updates selectedVariantId in state and triggers recalculation of price and shipping displays, as well as updating the URL's variantId parameter.

Allows the user to set the desired quantity for the current product/variant via increment/decrement controls or direct numeric input. Validates that quantity is a positive integer and publishes quantity changes to other components (such as price-and-shipping-summary and add-to-cart-form).

Computes and displays the effective unit price and line item subtotal based on the base product price and the selected variant's price_override (when present) and the current quantity. Uses free_shipping and free_shipping_override flags to show whether shipping is free for the selected configuration, and displays basic shipping details using shipping_weight if provided.

Coordinates Add to Cart behavior for the currently configured product. It reads productId from state/URL, selectedVariantId from the variant-selector, and quantity from the quantity-selector. On submission it validates the configuration and calls addProductToCart(productId, selectedVariantId, quantity). Based on the API response, it either redirects the user to cart.html or displays error feedback.

Displays an error message when getProductDetails fails or returns no product for the requested productId, and disables or hides interactive components like variant-selector, quantity-selector, price-and-shipping-summary, and add-to-cart-form.
Shows success or error messages related to the addProductToCart operation, based on the message property returned from the API. It updates in response to each Add to Cart attempt.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All core product information is organized in a single, centered column that reads from top to bottom. At the top of the column, a small breadcrumb trail anchors the user’s location. Directly beneath it, a bold, centered product heading (from product-main-info) introduces the page. Below the heading, a large, central white card groups together the image, specifications, configuration controls (variants and quantity), and the primary purchase interactions (price summary and add-to-cart form) in a vertical stack. Price/shipping details appear toward the bottom of this card, followed immediately by the add-to-cart form. Below the card, a full-width olive band (page-level, not tied to a specific component) can carry a short, uppercase reassurance or marketing line to mirror the mockup’s bridge section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All core product information is organized in a single, centered column that reads from top to bottom. At the top of the column, a small breadcrumb trail anchors the user’s location. Directly beneath it, a bold, centered product heading (from product-main-info) introduces the page. Below the heading, a large, central white card groups together the image, specifications, configuration controls (variants and quantity), and the primary purchase interactions (price summary and add-to-cart form) in a vertical stack. Price/shipping details appear toward the bottom of this card, followed immediately by the add-to-cart form. Below the card, a full-width olive band (page-level, not tied to a specific component) can carry a short, uppercase reassurance or marketing line to mirror the mockup’s bridge section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, leaving about 15–20% margins on each side. The white product detail card occupies most of this column with generous internal padding (24–32px vertically, 32–40px horizontally). Individual elements inside the card—image, lists, selectors, and forms—are stacked vertically with 16–32px gaps between them, following the spacing scale. The breadcrumb occupies a thin strip above with modest vertical spacing, while the olive band below stretches full-width across the content area with generous 24–32px vertical padding so the text feels substantial and banner-like.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low-to-medium: typography is large and legible, line heights are airy, and each functional block (image, specs, selectors, price, form) has room to breathe. Specifications are presented in a tidy, vertically spaced list reminiscent of the mockup’s bullets, with 8–16px vertical separation per item. Form controls (variant and quantity selectors) are not tightly packed; instead, they are separated by 16–24px and aligned in a clear vertical flow. The price and shipping summary is visually emphasized but not crammed, using whitespace and bold highlights rather than dense text to communicate key information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the breadcrumb (for orientation) and then is immediately pulled to the bold product heading in the center. From there, the flow moves straight down into the white card: first to the product image, then down into product specifications that echo the bullet-list rhythm of the mockup. Beneath that, attention slides into the variant and quantity selectors as the configuration step, then to the price and shipping summary, and finally rests on the add-to-cart form as the primary CTA. After the form, the gaze naturally continues downward to the contrasting olive-green band, which acts as a narrative bridge and reassurance element. Status and loader components appear in-context but are visually subordinate to this main vertical reading path, except when the loader overlay is active and temporarily becomes the primary focus.

layout.overall_description: The Product Detail page presents as a clean, centered marketing-style layout with a strong single-column structure. At the very top, a slim breadcrumb line sits within the main content width, left-aligned but still inside the generous side margins. Just below, the product’s name and key summary information appear in a bold, slightly oversized heading style that feels similar to the red title in the mockup, centered and given extra top and bottom whitespace. Directly under this heading, a large white card dominates the central portion of the screen. Inside this card, the product image is shown first, centered and comfortably sized, followed by a vertically stacked list of key product specifications styled like simple bullet rows. Below the specs, the user finds the variant selector and quantity selector, laid out one after the other with ample spacing, leading naturally into the price and shipping summary and then a prominent add-to-cart form and button at the bottom of the card. The card itself is surrounded by generous whitespace on all sides, reinforcing its importance. Beneath it, separated by a clear 32–48px vertical gap, a full-width olive band spans the content area with a single line of uppercase text that bridges into further site content and mirrors the mockup’s narrative strip. Loader and error messages appear centered within this same column, temporarily replacing or overlaying the card, so the experience remains coherent, calm, and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears centered within the main content area, aligned to the same central column as the product card. When active, it overlays a translucent layer above the white card region, spanning roughly the width of the card (about 60–70% of the viewport) and the vertical space where product content would normally render. A circular spinner or subtle motion element is placed exactly in the center of this space, with at least 32px of clear padding around it so it feels calm and deliberate. Background elements are dimmed but still faintly visible beneath, reinforcing that the focus is temporarily on loading while preserving the overall sense of layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays the main content card area in a centered, fixed-position layer within the viewport when loading; disappears entirely once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the very top of the content column, just below the global site header if present. It is left-aligned within the centered column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with about 24px of top padding from the header and 16–24px of space below it before the main product heading. The breadcrumb line is relatively compact and understated, occupying only a small vertical slice (around 1/10 of the initial visible height), ensuring it orients the user without competing with the main card below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow; scrolls away with the page content as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-main-info component is positioned directly beneath the breadcrumb, centered within the same main column. It includes the primary product title and any short subtitle or key descriptor. The title is large and bold, aligning visually with the mockup’s prominent heading and occupying roughly the full width of the centered column, with text center-aligned. There is generous vertical spacing—about 24–32px above (from the breadcrumb) and about 24–32px below—before the white product detail card begins, giving the heading time to breathe as a clear introduction to the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main vertical flow; remains at the top of the page content and scrolls off-screen as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white product detail card, the product-image-display sits at the top, directly under the card’s top padding. It is centered horizontally within the card and occupies roughly 60–80% of the card’s width, with equal left and right padding to maintain the card’s generous margins (32–40px). Vertically, it is given comfortable breathing room, with about 24px of space above and below the image area. The image is the first focal point inside the card, establishing a clear visual anchor before users scroll further down into text and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; scales responsively with the card width but remains constrained to preserve whitespace around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-specifications component is placed immediately beneath the product image within the same white card, separated by roughly 24px of vertical space. It stretches the full usable width inside the card’s padding, maintaining left-aligned text for easy scanning. Specifications are presented as a vertical list of rows, echoing the mockup’s bullet structure: each spec item aligns to an invisible left column where an icon, bullet, or small marker could appear, with the descriptive text to the right. Items are spaced with about 8–16px of vertical separation, creating a low-to-medium density block that feels tidy but not cramped. The list does not reach edge-to-edge; it respects the card’s internal margins to keep the look calm and editorial.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; expands vertically as needed while pushing the components below it further down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The variant-selector appears below the product specifications in the same vertical stack, with about 24px of vertical gap from the last spec item. It spans most of the card’s internal width, aligning its label and controls to the left, consistent with the spec text alignment. Any dropdowns, swatches, or buttons are arranged in a single column or a simple row that does not break the single-column feel; if multiple variant options exist, they stack with 8–16px spacing between them. The overall block height is moderate, ensuring the selector feels important but does not visually overshadow the image or specifications above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the card; its height adjusts based on the number of options without overlapping adjacent elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-selector is placed directly beneath the variant selector, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space to suggest a close relationship but distinct interaction. It aligns with the same left edge as the variant selector and spans a similar width inside the card. The control (stepper, input, or buttons) is kept compact horizontally, with the label to the left or above, so that the overall block feels like a simple, readable row within the single-column card. There is a small amount of padding—around 8–12px—inside the control area so it feels tactile and easy to tap or click without increasing content density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; remains grouped closely with the variant selector and shifts downward with the rest of the content as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-and-shipping-summary component sits below the quantity selector, with a slightly larger vertical gap of about 24–32px to signal a transition from configuration to purchase decision. Within the card, it spans the full width inside the padding and is left-aligned for numeric clarity, but key values like price can be slightly larger or highlighted in a warm accent color. Shipping details appear immediately under or beside the price in a more subdued style. The block is compact but surrounded by generous whitespace, making the price and shipping information easy to scan in a single glance before the user reaches the add-to-cart form directly below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; remains just above the add-to-cart-form and expands minimally as text length changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form is the final block inside the white product card, immediately beneath the price-and-shipping-summary with about 16–24px of vertical spacing. It spans nearly the full internal width of the card, with the primary button centered or left-aligned but visually dominant. The button itself is tall and wide enough to feel substantial—roughly 60–80% of the card’s width—yet still framed by ample side padding to preserve the airy layout. If additional fields (like notes or checkboxes) exist, they stack vertically above the button with 8–16px gaps, keeping a clean single-column structure. A bit of extra padding (24–32px) is added at the bottom of the card below the button to give the CTA breathing room before the card’s edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; acts as the final focal point of the main vertical flow and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the product fails to load, the product-load-error-message appears in place of the main white card, centered within the same content column beneath the breadcrumb and heading area. It is rendered within a smaller white card-like container that uses the same side margins (60–70% width centered) and padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal), but with less vertical height than the full product detail card. The message text is center-aligned and given clear spacing around it so it is readable at a glance, with a possible small icon on the left or above to echo the bullet/icon rhythm of the original mockup. There is at least 24px of whitespace above and below this error card to keep it visually distinct and calm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and replaces the normal product detail card area when an error state exists; scrolls with the page like other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-status-message appears directly beneath the add-to-cart-form inside the main white card, separated from the button by about 8–16px of vertical space so it feels attached to the action but not part of the button itself. It is left-aligned with the form content and constrained within the card’s padding, occupying only one or two lines of text in most cases. Success messages may use a subtle colored accent bar or icon on the left, aligning with the same visual column used for bullets/specs, while error messages might use a warmer or red tone, all kept compact to preserve the page’s spacious feel. There is still at least 16–24px of padding between the bottom of this message and the bottom edge of the card to avoid a cramped appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the card layout; appears and disappears beneath the add-to-cart-form without shifting the overall structure of the page beyond its own vertical space.

name: Virtual Services
title: Virtual Services
description: Page for browsing, filtering, and sorting virtual decluttering coaching bundles, with navigation to bundle detail and the cart, using URL parameters to control state.

core_features: Load and display virtual service categories from getServiceCategories, highlighting the category indicated by the categoryId URL parameter when applicable.
List virtual decluttering coaching bundles using listVirtualCoachingBundles, driven by URL parameters for sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy.
Provide filter controls for number of sessions, session length, and price range that update the URL parameters and re-fetch bundles via listVirtualCoachingBundles.

Provide sorting controls (e.g., by price) that set the sortBy URL parameter and re-fetch bundles via listVirtualCoachingBundles so that the least expensive qualifying bundle appears first when appropriate.
Display virtual bundle cards with key information from listVirtualCoachingBundles and link each card to package_detail.html with the corresponding packageId.
Allow navigation to cart.html via a header cart icon while preserving current filters and sorting in the browser history.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Browse virtual services from header navigation: 1) User clicks the “Virtual Services” link in the main header navigation. 2) Browser loads virtual_services.html without or with minimal query parameters. 3) On load, the page parses URL parameters (if present) and initializes internal state for categoryId, sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy. 4) The page calls getServiceCategories to retrieve all service categories and identifies any virtual categories for display and highlighting. 5) The page calls listVirtualCoachingBundles with the current filter and sort state derived from URL parameters. 6) The user reviews the list of virtual bundles and can click any bundle card to navigate to package_detail.html with the corresponding packageId.

Workflow 2 – Deep-linked filtered virtual coaching view: 1) User clicks a promo or blog link that points to virtual_services.html with query parameters such as categoryId, sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy. 2) On load, the URL-state manager parses these query parameters and sets the initial filter and sort state. 3) The page calls getServiceCategories to display categories and highlight the category matching categoryId when it exists and is virtual. 4) The page calls listVirtualCoachingBundles with the parsed filter and sort values. 5) Filter and sort controls are initialized to reflect the URL parameters so the user sees which constraints are applied. 6) The user can adjust filters or sorting; each change updates the URL parameters and triggers a new listVirtualCoachingBundles call, updating the bundle list accordingly.

Workflow 3 – Adjust filters and sorting while browsing: 1) User is on virtual_services.html with an initial set of bundles displayed. 2) User changes the “Number of Sessions” filter (e.g., via an input or selector). 3) The filter component updates the sessionCount URL parameter, removes it if cleared, and triggers a re-fetch of data via listVirtualCoachingBundles with the new sessionCount. 4) User adjusts the “Session Length” and price range controls; each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice) and re-calls listVirtualCoachingBundles. 5) User changes the sorting (e.g., chooses a price-based sort option); the sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers another listVirtualCoachingBundles call. 6) The bundle list re-renders each time based on the latest API response, always reflecting the current URL parameters.

Workflow 4 – Navigate to bundle detail from filtered results: 1) User applies desired filters and sorting so that a relevant set of bundles is displayed. 2) User identifies a bundle card of interest (for instance, a 3-session, specific-minute virtual decluttering coaching bundle that appears first under a price-based sort). 3) User clicks anywhere on the interactive area of that bundle card. 4) The bundle card component reads the bundle’s package_id from the listVirtualCoachingBundles response. 5) The page navigates to package_detail.html, appending packageId equal to that package_id in the query string. 6) User can then configure or add the package to the cart on the package_detail.html page (handled there, not on virtual_services.html).

Workflow 5 – Access the cart while exploring virtual services: 1) While viewing virtual_services.html, user clicks the cart icon in the header. 2) The header navigation component triggers a navigation to cart.html. 3) The current virtual_services.html URL (including filters and sorting parameters) remains in browser history, so the user can navigate back and see the same filtered view without needing localStorage.
interactions: Interaction – Page load and initial data fetch: On initial load or when the URL changes via browser navigation, the URL-state-manager parses categoryId, sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy from the query string; then the page triggers getServiceCategories (no parameters) and, once filters are known, triggers listVirtualCoachingBundles(sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, sortBy).

Interaction – Category selection: When the user clicks a virtual category tab or item in the category selector, the service-category-selector updates the categoryId parameter in the URL (or removes it if deselected). It then triggers a URL-state change event. Since listVirtualCoachingBundles is scoped to virtual coaching bundles, selecting the virtual coaching category primarily updates visual state and does not alter listVirtualCoachingBundles parameters, but the page will still re-validate the current view based on the URL.

Interaction – Change number of sessions filter: When the user types or selects a value in the “Number of Sessions” control, the filter-bar component validates the input (if needed), sets or removes the sessionCount query parameter, updates the URL via the History API, and calls listVirtualCoachingBundles with the updated sessionCount along with the current values for sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy.

Interaction – Change session length filter: When the user changes the session length control, the filter-bar component updates the sessionLengthMinutes URL parameter (or removes it if cleared), then re-calls listVirtualCoachingBundles with the new sessionLengthMinutes and the current sessionCount, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy values.

Interaction – Change price range filters: When the user edits the minimum or maximum price fields, the filter-bar component updates minPrice and/or maxPrice in the query string. It then calls listVirtualCoachingBundles with these updated price bounds and the existing sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, and sortBy values.

Interaction – Change sorting: When the user selects a sorting option, the sort-control component sets the sortBy URL parameter to the appropriate value (or clears it for default ordering) and triggers a listVirtualCoachingBundles call with the existing sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, and maxPrice, plus the new sortBy. The bundle list re-renders in the new order.

Interaction – Click bundle card: When the user clicks on a bundle card, the bundle-card component reads the package_id from its bound bundle data and navigates to package_detail.html?packageId={package_id}. No additional API calls are made from virtual_services.html for this navigation.

Interaction – Empty or no-results state: After any listVirtualCoachingBundles call, if the returned array is empty, the results component displays an appropriate no-results message and keeps filter and sorting controls active so the user can adjust criteria.

Interaction – Click cart icon: When the user clicks the cart icon in the header, the header-cart-link component navigates to cart.html without altering the current virtual_services.html URL. No cart-related API calls are performed on this page.

Interaction – Browser back/forward navigation: When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons, the URL-state-manager detects the changed query parameters, re-parses them into state, and triggers a fresh listVirtualCoachingBundles call so that the visible filters and bundle list always match the current URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The core state of virtual_services.html is fully represented by URL query parameters and is not stored in localStorage or other client-side persistence. The following parameters are recognized: categoryId, sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy. On page initialization, a URL-state-manager parses these parameters; missing parameters are treated as undefined and passed as such to listVirtualCoachingBundles. The categoryId parameter is used to determine which service category is highlighted in the category selector after getServiceCategories returns; if the specified categoryId does not correspond to any returned category or is not virtual, the selector displays available categories without highlighting the invalid one. The filters sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, and maxPrice are bound bidirectionally to their respective UI controls: updating a control immediately updates the URL query parameter (adding, changing, or removing it), and any URL change (including via browser navigation) updates the control values. The sortBy parameter is similarly bound to the sort-control. After any change to these parameters, the page calls listVirtualCoachingBundles with the current sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, and sortBy values. The list of bundles is always derived solely from the latest listVirtualCoachingBundles response, ensuring that no static or hardcoded bundle data is used. Navigation to package_detail.html includes only the packageId parameter derived from the selected bundle’s package_id. Navigation to cart.html does not modify any URL parameters on virtual_services.html, allowing users to return via browser navigation to the exact same filter and sort state reflected in the query string.

components.functionality: Parses URL query parameters (categoryId, sessionCount, sessionLengthMinutes, minPrice, maxPrice, sortBy) on load and on browser navigation events, exposes them as page-level state, and notifies dependent components (filters, sort control, results) when they change.

Retrieves all service categories via getServiceCategories, filters or emphasizes virtual categories, and displays them as selectable options. Highlights the category matching the current categoryId URL parameter when present and virtual. Updates the URL when a category is clicked.

Provides user controls for filtering virtual bundles by number of sessions, session length (in minutes), and minimum/maximum price. Reads initial values from URL parameters, updates those parameters on user input, and triggers a new listVirtualCoachingBundles call via a shared state update.

Allows the user to select the sorting order for virtual bundles (for example, by price ascending). Synchronizes with the sortBy URL parameter and causes a refresh of the bundle list when the sort option changes.

Executes listVirtualCoachingBundles using the current filter and sort state from URL parameters and displays the returned array of bundles. Handles loading and empty states and renders one virtual-bundle-card child component per bundle.

Represents a single virtual decluttering coaching bundle using data from listVirtualCoachingBundles. Displays name, description, base_price, session_count, session_length_minutes, rating information, and whether it can be added to cart. When clicked, navigates to package_detail.html with the corresponding packageId.

Displays a summary of the current results (such as the number of bundles returned) and reflects the active filters based on URL parameters. Updates automatically when the bundle list or URL state changes.
Provides a cart icon or link in the page header that navigates the user to cart.html when clicked. Does not call any cart APIs from this page and does not alter current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, strong top-to-bottom flow. At the top of the content area, a bold, centered page heading introduces 'Virtual Services'. Directly beneath it, a large, centered white card contains the interactive controls (service category selector and filters) arranged in a simple vertical stack, echoing the mockup’s bullet list card. Below this card, after a slice of white space, a full-width olive-green band acts as a narrative bridge that holds the results summary and the sort control. Under that band, the virtual bundle results appear in a centered grid of cards. URL state is managed invisibly across the whole page, while the cart link lives in the top-right header and then acts as a secondary floating access point as the user scrolls.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, strong top-to-bottom flow. At the top of the content area, a bold, centered page heading introduces 'Virtual Services'. Directly beneath it, a large, centered white card contains the interactive controls (service category selector and filters) arranged in a simple vertical stack, echoing the mockup’s bullet list card. Below this card, after a slice of white space, a full-width olive-green band acts as a narrative bridge that holds the results summary and the sort control. Under that band, the virtual bundle results appear in a centered grid of cards. URL state is managed invisibly across the whole page, while the cart link lives in the top-right header and then acts as a secondary floating access point as the user scrolls.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All primary content is constrained to a centered column occupying roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (equivalent to 8–10 of 12 grid columns) with generous left/right margins. The white filter card uses substantial internal padding (around 24–32px vertically and 32–40px horizontally), visually occupying the main middle slice of the viewport. The olive band spans the full content width horizontally with generous vertical padding (24–32px) to feel like a substantial banner. The results grid sits below with clear vertical separation (~32–40px) from the band and uses comfortable gutters between cards. The floating cart link consumes minimal width on the right edge and never overlaps primary reading flow.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium density throughout. Controls inside the white card are vertically separated with at least 16–24px between rows and ample breathing room around labels and inputs, similar to the spacious bullet list in the mockup. The olive band’s text is bold and well-spaced, with uppercase styling and ample line-height so it reads like a clear, scannable statement, not clutter. The results grid uses large card padding and generous spacing between cards so the page feels like an editorial/marketing experience rather than a cramped app interface.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves down the page in a strong vertical sequence: first to the red-toned page heading, then immediately into the large central white card that invites interaction with categories and filters. From the bottom of this card, the eye is naturally drawn down to the contrasting olive-green band, which summarizes the results and offers sorting, acting as a clear bridge into the browsing experience. From there, the user flows into the card-based results grid. The floating cart link on the right acts as a subtle, peripheral attractor—visible but secondary—never competing with the main column’s reading and interaction path.

layout.overall_description: The Virtual Services page is structured as a tall, centered column floating in a warm, lightly textured background, echoing the homey marketing style of the mockup. At the top of the main content, a bold, red-accented 'Virtual Services' heading is centered and framed by generous whitespace. Directly beneath, a large white card sits in the middle of the screen, with soft shadows and rounded corners, occupying roughly 70–80% of the page width and clearly standing out from the background. Inside this card, the service category selector and filter controls are stacked vertically like tidy list items, each spanning the card’s width with consistent left alignment and comfortable spacing. Below the card, after a modest vertical gap, a full-width olive-green band stretches across the same centered content area, with ample vertical padding. On the left, it states the current results summary in uppercase, while on the right the sort control is aligned, giving the band a clear, banner-like presence that bridges the filter area and the browsing results. Under this band, separated by another slice of whitespace, the virtual bundle cards appear in a relaxed grid—two or three cards per row on desktop within the same central column—each card airy, with clear hierarchy and strong typography. Along the right edge of the viewport, the cart link lives in the site header at the very top and then becomes a small, floating pill as the user scrolls, mirroring the mockup’s subtle side FABs without intruding into the main reading column. The overall effect is a professional yet approachable browsing experience that guides the user smoothly from understanding and narrowing options to exploring individual virtual decluttering bundles.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and spans the entire page context. It sits conceptually behind all visible components, tracking URL parameters for filters, sort, pagination, and selected bundles. It has no on-screen footprint, no margins or padding, and does not affect the visual layout; instead, it binds state changes from the filter card, olive band (sort control), and results grid to updates in the URL and vice versa.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, page-wide behavior layer that listens to and updates state for all interactive components without occupying any visual space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service category selector is positioned near the top of the large central white card, immediately below the page heading, acting as the first interactive row within the card. It is left-aligned inside the card, spanning nearly the full inner width (with 24–32px horizontal padding between the selector and the card edges). Vertically, it sits with about 24px of top padding from the card’s upper border and at least 16–24px of spacing below before the next filter controls. On desktop, its visual weight is similar to a prominent bullet item in the mockup’s list, making it a clear starting point for narrowing services while remaining within the single-column center alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; moves naturally with the page scroll as part of the white filter card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The virtual bundle filter bar sits directly beneath the service category selector inside the same white card, forming a vertical sequence of filter controls reminiscent of the mockup’s stacked bullet items. It occupies the full inner width of the card, with consistent left alignment and 24–40px of vertical space shared across its elements. Individual filter chips or dropdowns are arranged horizontally within this band but constrained so they never exceed the card’s width; line wrapping is allowed on smaller screens to preserve readability. There is a clear vertical gap (around 16–24px) between this filter bar and both the selector above and any card footer content below, preserving the spacious, low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the white card; sits in the main scrollable content, expanding vertically as filters wrap or additional options appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is visually anchored inside the olive-green band that sits just below the white filter card. Within this band, the results summary is placed on the left, and the sort control is right-aligned, forming a simple two-column horizontal layout across the band’s width. The sort dropdown or segmented control sits flush to the right margin of the centered content column, with approximately 24–32px of padding to the band’s right edge and vertically centered within the band’s height. On narrower screens, the sort control drops below the results summary but remains within the same olive band, still centered horizontally, with at least 12–16px vertical spacing between them. This placement makes the sort control clearly associated with the results without cluttering the filter card above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the olive band; it scrolls with the page but remains visually grouped with the results summary as a single banner-like unit.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results component occupies the main central area beneath the olive-green band, maintaining the same centered column width (about 70–80% of the viewport) with generous side margins. It begins about 32–40px below the bottom edge of the band to clearly separate the filter/sort area from the browsing cards. Inside this zone, virtual bundle cards are laid out in a responsive grid: on larger screens, two to three cards per row with 24–32px gaps between cards horizontally and vertically; on smaller screens, cards stack into a single column while maintaining full width within the content column. The top of the results area visually aligns with the left edge of the band’s text, reinforcing the strong single-column flow observed in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content block that expands vertically as more bundle cards are loaded or paginated.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each virtual bundle card appears as a distinct, medium-sized white card within the results grid, echoing—but smaller than—the main filter card. Cards have generous internal padding (around 24px on all sides), a subtle shadow, and rounded corners to maintain the warm, marketing feel. On desktop, each card occupies roughly one-half to one-third of the content column width, depending on the number of columns chosen (e.g., 3 cards per row at ~30% width each with gutters), and they align in clean rows with consistent spacing. All cards share a common top alignment per row, ensuring a tidy vertical rhythm. Typography within the card is large and spacious with clear hierarchy (title at top, key details in the middle, primary CTA at the bottom), preserving the low-density, easy-to-scan feel from the mockup’s bullet list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results grid; cards rearrange responsively (e.g., from 3-column to 2-column to 1-column) based on viewport width but do not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary text is embedded inside the olive-green horizontal band directly below the white filter card. It is left-aligned within the centered content column and sits with comfortable horizontal padding (about 32–40px from the band’s left edge) and generous vertical padding (24–32px top and bottom), giving it a substantial, banner-like presence. The text uses uppercase styling and bold or semi-bold weight to mirror the mockup’s bridging statement. On wider screens, it occupies the left two-thirds of the band, visually balanced against the right-aligned sort control. On smaller screens, when the sort control wraps below, the results summary remains at the top of the band, centered or left-aligned, maintaining strong readability and acting as the narrative bridge from filtering to browsing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static as part of the olive band; it scrolls with content and remains tightly coupled to the sort control within the same full-width strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link initially appears in the global site header, anchored to the far right of the header’s horizontal layout, aligned with the right edge of the main content column. It is visually compact but clearly identifiable (e.g., icon plus label or item count), sitting in the same top band as the main navigation, above the page’s red heading. To reflect the mockup’s floating FAB pattern, once the user scrolls past the header, the cart link transitions into a small, pill-shaped floating button attached to the right side of the viewport, roughly mid-height. This floating version is vertically aligned with the main content column but offset to the right so it never overlaps text or cards—typically hovering over the background margin area. It maintains a minimal footprint and subtle accent color (e.g., blue or brand accent) so it remains noticeable yet clearly secondary to the central reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/floating: anchored in the site header at the very top; after scrolling, it becomes a fixed-position side pill along the right edge of the viewport, maintaining its position as the page content scrolls beneath it.

name: In-Home Assessment
title: In-Home Assessment
description: This page explains the in-home assessment and lets visitors submit an assessment request form that is sent to the organizing service team.

core_features: Load and display dynamic in-home assessment information using the getAssessmentInfo interface.
Show a clear "Request Assessment" call-to-action that scrolls or jumps to the assessment request form on the same page.
Provide an assessment request form that captures required contact and home details and submits them through the submitAssessmentRequest interface.
Handle and display submission states (submitting, success, error) based on the response from submitAssessmentRequest.
Respect an optional source URL parameter to adjust page state or copy variants without changing the core behavior.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Viewing assessment details:
1) User navigates to assessment.html (optionally with ?source=someOrigin).
2) The page controller reads the source URL parameter and stores it in in-memory state for this page load.
3) The assessment-info-loader component calls getAssessmentInfo with no parameters.
4) When the response is returned, the assessment-details-section binds to the response fields (headline, description, typical_duration_minutes, benefits) and renders them.
5) User can read what the in-home assessment includes and how it supports larger organizing projects.

Workflow 2 – Using the "Request Assessment" CTA:
1) While viewing the assessment details, user clicks the "Request Assessment" button.
2) The button’s click handler triggers an in-page scroll or jump to the assessment-request-form component (e.g., by focusing the first input in the form or adjusting the URL hash).
3) The form becomes visible and focused (if it was previously collapsed) so the user can start filling out the fields.

Workflow 3 – Submitting an assessment request:
1) In the assessment-request-form, the user enters their name, email, phone, address or city/area description, selects a home type, optionally provides number of bedrooms and square footage, and selects preferred day and time window.
2) The user adds additional details and budget information into the notes and budget fields (if desired).
3) On clicking the "Submit Request" button, the form validates required fields on the client side (e.g., presence and basic format checks) without storing any data in localStorage.
4) If validation passes, the form’s onSubmit handler constructs a payload that maps directly to submitAssessmentRequest parameters (including combining or prefixing any source information into the notes field if needed).
5) The form sets a "submitting" state (disabling the button and optionally showing a non-static loading indicator) and calls submitAssessmentRequest.
6) When a successful response is returned, the form updates its state to "success", shows a confirmation message using the returned message and/or status, and may display the assessment_request_id and submitted_at values.
7) If the response indicates failure or an error occurs, the form switches to an "error" state, displays a generic error message (not hardcoded to specific error codes), and re-enables the submit button so the user can try again.

Workflow 4 – Navigating to the cart:
1) At any time on assessment.html, the user can click the cart icon in the shared header.
2) The header-cart-link component navigates the user to cart.html according to the site’s standard routing, without altering or persisting form data from the assessment page.
3) No interfaces specific to this page are called as part of this navigation; it is purely a page transition.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads the optional source query parameter from the URL and stores it in in-memory state for use by other components during this session.
On page load, the assessment-info-loader component automatically calls getAssessmentInfo and updates the assessment-details-section when the data is received.
Clicking the "Request Assessment" button triggers a scroll or jump to the assessment-request-form (and may focus the first form field) without reloading the page.

Typing into any form input (name, email, phone, address/city, bedrooms, square footage, notes, budget) or changing any selection (home type, preferred day, preferred time window) updates the form’s local state but does not persist data beyond the page session.
Clicking the "Submit Request" button triggers client-side validation. If validation fails, inline validation messages are shown and submission is not attempted.

If validation passes, clicking the "Submit Request" button calls submitAssessmentRequest with parameters mapped directly from the form state, then updates the UI based on the returned success flag and response fields.

On a successful submission, the assessment-request-form disables further edits (or optionally keeps fields read-only) and shows a non-hardcoded confirmation area bound to the returned message, status, assessment_request_id, and submitted_at.
On an unsuccessful submission, the assessment-request-form shows an error message, re-enables the submit button, and allows the user to correct inputs and resubmit.
