Clicking the header cart icon navigates to cart.html according to site navigation rules.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state for this page is the optional URL query parameter `source`. When the page is loaded, the page controller parses the URL and extracts `source` if present, without modifying it. This value is stored only in in-memory state for the current page lifecycle and is not written to localStorage or any other persistent client storage.

The `source` value can be used in two ways:
1) Conditional copy or behavior on this page (without changing core functionality): components may adjust microcopy or emphasis (e.g., slightly different explanatory text or an alternate form intro) based on the stored `source` value. Any such variations must not change the required fields, visible form structure, or which interfaces are called.
2) Optional inclusion within the `notes` parameter of submitAssessmentRequest: before submission, the form controller may prepend or append a short, programmatically generated note containing the current `source` value to the user-entered notes content, so that the backend receives the origin context without needing a dedicated API parameter.

No other URL parameters are required for core functionality. The page does not rely on URL fragments for state, although the "Request Assessment" button may set a hash (e.g., `#form`) purely to allow the browser to scroll to the form anchor. All other state (field values, validation status, submission status) is maintained in memory within the page’s component hierarchy and reset if the user reloads or navigates away.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, reads the source URL parameter, triggers loading of assessment info, and supplies shared state (such as `source` and loading flags) to child components.
On page load, calls getAssessmentInfo with no parameters, manages loading and error states for that request, and passes the resulting content to the assessment-details-section.

Displays the current in-home assessment information (such as headline, overall description, typical duration, and a list of benefits) using the data supplied by assessment-info-loader, and renders a prominent "Request Assessment" button that scrolls the user to the form.

Provides input fields to collect name, email, phone, address or city/area, home type (selectable from allowed home type options), number of bedrooms, square footage, preferred day, preferred time window, additional notes, and budget amount. Validates required fields, and on submit, calls submitAssessmentRequest with a payload built from the form state (optionally augmented with the source indicator in the notes) and updates its internal status based on the response.

Listens to the assessment-request-form submission state and renders appropriate status messages, such as in-progress indicators, success confirmation using the returned message, status, assessment_request_id, and submitted_at, as well as any generic error messages if submission fails.
Provides a clickable cart icon in the site header that, when activated from assessment.html, navigates the user to cart.html according to global navigation rules.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered main column for all primary content. At the top of the scrollable area, show the page heading ('In-Home Assessment') in large red type, centered. Directly beneath it, place a prominent white card that contains the assessment-details-section as a vertical list of points (each in a row: chevron icon on the left, text on the right). After a clear slice of whitespace, place a full-width olive-green band containing a short, uppercase bridging statement centered horizontally. Below that band, place the assessment-request-form as a second white card of similar width to the first. The assessment-form-status-panel floats on the right side of the viewport as a compact, peripheral element, and the header-cart-link sits in the global header at the top right, separate from the main column content. The assessment-info-loader appears inline within the main column where content/form will render, showing a loading state when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered main column for all primary content. At the top of the scrollable area, show the page heading ('In-Home Assessment') in large red type, centered. Directly beneath it, place a prominent white card that contains the assessment-details-section as a vertical list of points (each in a row: chevron icon on the left, text on the right). After a clear slice of whitespace, place a full-width olive-green band containing a short, uppercase bridging statement centered horizontally. Below that band, place the assessment-request-form as a second white card of similar width to the first. The assessment-form-status-panel floats on the right side of the viewport as a compact, peripheral element, and the header-cart-link sits in the global header at the top right, separate from the main column content. The assessment-info-loader appears inline within the main column where content/form will render, showing a loading state when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain the main content to a centered column about 8–10 of 12 grid columns wide (roughly 60–70% of viewport width on desktop), leaving 15–20% margins on each side. The white cards (details and form) both sit inside this main column and never go full-bleed. The olive band spans the full content width horizontally (edge-to-edge of the page background, not just the card width) with generous vertical padding. The floating status panel consumes a narrow strip along the right edge of the viewport (~15–20% width or less, fixed width), ensuring it does not overlap the main reading column. The header-cart-link occupies a small area at the top right of the header bar. Vertical spacing between major blocks (heading → card, card → band, band → form) follows a 32–48px rhythm to preserve clear separation.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium content density. Inside cards, use 24–32px padding on all sides with 16–24px vertical spacing between bullet rows or form groups. Typography should be relatively large with comfortable line height (around 1.4–1.6), making the descriptive text and labels easy to scan. The olive band uses a single line or short block of uppercase text with ample breathing room above and below, so it feels like a strong, but not crowded, banner. The floating status panel is compact but not cramped, with enough padding for legibility at a glance.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a top-to-bottom narrative: first the red heading centered at the top; then immediately into the white assessment-details card where the user reads the bulleted explanation from top to bottom, aligned along a single left rail of chevron icons and text. When the last bullet ends, whitespace pushes the eye down into the contrasting olive-green band, which acts as a narrative bridge ('What happens next / Ready to schedule?'). From there, the visual flow continues downward into the assessment-request-form card, where the user can act. Throughout, the floating status panel on the right and the cart link in the header remain peripheral: visible but not intruding into the central reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, centered marketing-style layout with a warm, home-service feel. Below the site header (with the cart link in the top-right), the main content begins with a bold red 'In-Home Assessment' heading centered in a wide band of whitespace. Immediately beneath, a large white card floats in the middle of the screen, occupying roughly two-thirds of the page width with generous side margins, and contains a vertical list explaining the in-home assessment, each point marked by a chevron icon on the left and text on the right. After this card, a comfortable slice of background space leads into a full-width olive-green band that stretches edge-to-edge, with a short uppercase line of text centered inside it, visually acting as a bridge from explanation to action. Scrolling slightly further, another centered white card of similar width contains the assessment request form, laid out in a simple, vertical sequence of fields and a primary submit button. Along the right edge of the viewport, a compact status panel floats midway down, updating the user about the form state without ever overlapping the main column. Overall, the layout feels linear and inviting, with strong vertical storytelling and subtle secondary elements kept to the periphery.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component governs the full-page canvas. It establishes a full-width background (e.g., subtle texture or light neutral tone) and centers a main content column within a 12-column grid. On desktop, this column spans roughly 8–10 columns (about 60–70% of the viewport width) and is horizontally centered, leaving substantial left and right margins (15–20% each). Vertical padding from the top of the viewport (below the global header) to the page heading is generous (about 64–96px) to create a calm, editorial entry. The controller also defines vertical spacing between the primary sections: heading, the first white card (assessment-details-section), the olive band, and the second white card (assessment-request-form), using a consistent 32–48px rhythm. On tablet and mobile, it expands the main column toward full width while preserving at least 16–24px side padding. All other components (info-loader, sections, form, status panel) are positioned relative to this baseline layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a structural wrapper with normal document flow; controls max-width and section spacing but does not itself float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears inline within the main content column wherever assessment details or the form are expected to render. When active, it occupies the same approximate width as the white cards (about 60–70% of the viewport) and is horizontally centered. Vertically, it sits in place of the card content, maintaining the same top and bottom margins as the cards so the overall page rhythm remains intact. Visually, it might present as a simplified white or lightly tinted block with skeleton lines or a centered spinner, padded with 24–32px on all sides to mirror the card feel. It never extends full-bleed; it stays within the centralized column and preserves the same generous surrounding whitespace so that loading states feel integrated rather than disruptive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline in the normal flow; no fixed or sticky behavior. When visible, it temporarily replaces or overlays content in the main column region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section forms the first major white card under the page heading. It is centered horizontally within the main column and spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with a max width similar to a standard marketing content card (e.g., 800–960px). The top of the card sits roughly 24–32px below the page heading, preserving a tight but comfortable relationship. The card has 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Inside, content is laid out as a vertical bullet list: each bullet is a horizontal row with a chevron icon aligned to the far left and the text block immediately to its right. The icon column is narrow (about 10–15% of the card’s width), and the text column takes up the remaining 85–90%, ensuring the text wraps neatly in a single left-aligned column. Bullets are spaced with 16–24px vertical gaps, keeping the list airy and readable. Any key phrases may be highlighted in a warm accent color (e.g., orange/red) to echo the mockup’s styling. The bottom of this card ends with 32–48px of whitespace before the olive band begins, forming a clear visual separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow as a central content card; scrolls with the page with no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment request form sits below the olive-green band as the second major white card. It is centered in the same main column and uses the same width and alignment as the assessment-details-section, creating a strong visual echo and cohesion between explanation and action. The top edge of the form card begins 32–48px below the bottom of the olive band. Inside, the card is padded 24–32px on all sides. The form content is arranged in a simple, vertical stack: a short heading or intro line at the top, followed by form fields (e.g., name, contact, address, preferred dates) each occupying the full internal width of the card. Fields are separated by 16–24px vertical spacing, with slightly larger spacing (24–32px) above the primary submit button row. The submit button is left-aligned or centered within the card (but kept within the same text column), and it should stand out with a strong accent color consistent with the brand. Below the button, there is space for inline validation messages or small helper text, but overall density remains low, with ample whitespace so the form feels approachable. The bottom margin of this card mirrors the top margin (~48px) to close the page in a balanced way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable card within the main column; no sticky behavior. It vertically follows the olive band in normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears as a compact, floating status panel aligned to the right side of the viewport, similar in spirit to a floating action button in the mockup. On desktop, it sits roughly mid-height vertically (centered between top and bottom of the viewport) and is inset slightly from the right edge (e.g., 16–24px from the edge). It has a fixed width (around 240–320px) and a modest height, appearing as a pill or small card with 16–24px internal padding. The content (status text, icons, progress) is arranged vertically, left-aligned within the panel. The panel’s background contrasts gently with the page (e.g., soft blue or neutral) while not competing with the main cards. It never overlaps the main content column; the column’s width is set so that even on smaller desktops the panel remains in the right gutter area. On tablet and mobile, this panel can shift toward the bottom of the screen (e.g., bottom-right corner) and shrink in width to avoid covering form fields. It is only visible when there is form-related feedback (e.g., submission in progress, success, or error) and remains peripheral, subtly attracting attention without disrupting reading or form completion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as fixed (or sticky to viewport) on the right side so it remains visible while the user scrolls through the form. It floats above the main page content without affecting the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link resides in the global site header at the very top of the page, outside the main content column. The header spans full width across the viewport; within it, the logo or site name is aligned to the left, and the cart link is aligned to the far right, sitting on the same horizontal baseline. The cart link may appear as an icon with or without a small label (e.g., 'Cart') and possibly a badge indicating item count. Vertical padding in the header is moderate (16–24px), keeping it slim compared to the main content sections so it doesn’t compete with the prominent page heading below. The header remains visually distinct, with a solid or slightly translucent background, and the cart link maintains sufficient right margin (16–24px) from the viewport edge. On scroll, if the header is sticky, the cart link stays pinned to the top-right, consistently available while the user reads the details and completes the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the global header, which may be sticky at the top of the viewport. The cart link itself is not floating independently; it moves with the header bar as part of the site’s primary navigation.

name: Blog & Resources
title: Blog & Resources
description: Browse, filter, and access organizing and decluttering articles and resources, with quick access to small-kitchen content and a removable reading list.

core_features: Load available blog filters (categories, tags, date presets) using getBlogFilterOptions and render them in an interactive filter bar.
List articles using listArticles with support for categoryId, tagId, datePreset, and sortBy URL parameters, and update the list whenever filters change.

Ensure small-kitchen-related content is easily accessible by providing a dedicated quick filter that navigates with tagId for the small-kitchen tag so that the newest small-kitchen article appears first.
Display a reading list of saved articles using getReadingList, show which listed articles are in the reading list, and allow removing items via removeReadingListItem.
Navigate from any article preview to its detail page (article.html) with the appropriate articleId URL parameter.

Link from article previews or resource blocks to shop.html with a preconfigured categoryId for related products (e.g., kitchen storage), and allow navigation to cart.html via the global header cart icon.
user_workflows: Landing on the page (possibly pre-filtered): 1) User opens blog.html, potentially with categoryId, tagId, datePreset, and sortBy in the URL. 2) The page parses these URL parameters into a currentFilters state. 3) The page calls getBlogFilterOptions to retrieve categories, tags, and date_presets. 4) The page calls listArticles with the parsed URL parameters. 5) The filter bar is rendered with the available options and the current selections reflected from URL state. 6) The article list is rendered from the listArticles response, respecting the current filters and sort. 7) The page calls getReadingList and uses this data to mark which listed articles are in the reading list.

Using filters and sorting: 1) User adjusts the category, tag, date preset, or sort order in the filter bar. 2) The filter bar emits a filter-change event with the new filter values. 3) The URL-state manager updates the querystring (categoryId, tagId, datePreset, sortBy) to reflect the new filters without reloading the entire site shell. 4) The page calls listArticles again with the updated parameters. 5) The article list rerenders with the new results. 6) The reading list markers on articles are refreshed based on the existing getReadingList data or re-fetched if needed.

Accessing small-kitchen content quickly: 1) User sees a dedicated quick-filter control associated with the small-kitchen tag (using the known small_kitchens tag ID from the tag set). 2) When the user activates this quick filter, the URL is updated to include tagId set to the small-kitchens tag ID (optionally combined with a relevant categoryId and sortBy). 3) The page calls listArticles with these parameters. 4) The resulting article list shows only small-kitchen-related articles, with the first item being the most recent one according to the active sort. 5) The user can click that first article card to go to article.html with the articleId in the querystring.

Opening full articles and related products: 1) User clicks on an article card in the list. 2) The page navigates to article.html?articleId={article_id_from_listArticles}. 3) From within certain article cards or resource areas on the listing, user can click a “Shop Related Products” style link. 4) This triggers navigation to shop.html with a categoryId corresponding to the appropriate product category (for example, kitchen storage).

Managing the reading list: 1) On load, the page calls getReadingList and renders the reading list panel (or summary). 2) User opens the reading list panel (e.g., via a button or icon). 3) The panel displays each saved article with its title, category_name, and saved_at from getReadingList. 4) User clicks on a reading list item’s title to navigate to article.html?articleId={article_id}. 5) User may choose to remove an item from the reading list; clicking the remove control triggers removeReadingListItem with the corresponding reading_list_item_id. 6) On success, the item is removed from the reading list panel and any reading-list indicator for that article in the main article list is updated accordingly.
interactions: Changing category filter: User selects a category option; current categoryId is updated in URL; listArticles is re-called with the new categoryId.
Changing tag filter: User selects or clears a tag option; tagId is updated in URL; listArticles is re-called with the new tagId.
Changing date preset: User selects a date preset; datePreset is updated in URL; listArticles is re-called with the new datePreset value.

Changing sort order: User selects a sort option (for example, newest-first vs other supported options); sortBy is updated in URL; listArticles is re-called with the new sortBy to reorder the article list.

Using the small-kitchen quick filter: User clicks the small-kitchen quick filter control; URL is updated to set tagId to the small_kitchens tag (and any associated categoryId/sortBy as needed); listArticles is called with these parameters; the article list shows small-kitchen articles only.
Navigating to an article: User clicks an article preview card; the browser navigates to article.html?articleId={article_id_from_listArticles}.
Navigating to shop from a resource block: User clicks a related-products link on an article card; the browser navigates to shop.html?categoryId={configured_kitchen_storage_category_id}.
Opening the cart: User clicks the cart icon in the header while on blog.html; the browser navigates to cart.html, preserving the current blog.html URL in history.
Opening the reading list: User clicks a control to open the reading list panel; if not already loaded, getReadingList is called; saved articles are displayed.

Removing an item from the reading list: User clicks a remove/delete control on a saved article in the reading list panel; removeReadingListItem is called with the item’s reading_list_item_id; on success, the item is removed from the panel and any indicator on the corresponding article in the main list is updated.
page_functionality.state_logic: The single source of truth for page state is the URL querystring, specifically the categoryId, tagId, datePreset, and sortBy parameters. On initial load, the page parses these parameters and uses them to call listArticles. When the user changes filters or sorting, the corresponding parameter(s) in the URL are updated (added, changed, or removed) and listArticles is re-called with the updated values. No filtering or sorting state is stored in localStorage; all such state is encoded in the URL so that the current view is shareable and restorable via direct link. The filters displayed in the UI are driven by getBlogFilterOptions; the selected values are determined exclusively by the URL parameters. The reading list state is not controlled by URL parameters; instead, it is loaded from getReadingList and kept in memory for the session. Removing items from the reading list via removeReadingListItem modifies server-side state; the client reflects this by updating the in-memory representation after a successful response. The small-kitchen highlighting behavior relies on URL-based filtering: activating the dedicated small-kitchen quick filter sets tagId to the known small_kitchens tag ID (and any associated categoryId/sortBy if configured), ensuring that listArticles returns small-kitchen articles with the first returned (typically the newest when sortBy favors recency) immediately visible at the top of the list.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and layout composition for the blog listing page. On initialization, it parses categoryId, tagId, datePreset, and sortBy from the URL, calls getBlogFilterOptions and listArticles with those parameters, then calls getReadingList to fetch saved articles. It passes the fetched filters, articles, and reading list down to child components (filter bar, article list, reading list panel) and listens for events from them to update the URL and re-fetch articles as needed.

Manages synchronization between UI state and URL query parameters. It reads categoryId, tagId, datePreset, and sortBy from location.search on initialization and exposes a normalized filter state to the page-shell. When child components emit filter-change events (e.g., selecting a category, tag, date preset, or sort order, or activating the small-kitchen quick filter), it updates the URL querystring accordingly using history APIs and notifies the page-shell that filters have changed so that listArticles can be re-called with the new values.

Renders the available blog filters and exposes interactions for refining the article list. It receives arrays of categories, tags, and date_presets from getBlogFilterOptions, along with the current selection derived from URL parameters. It presents controls for selecting a category (mapped from blog_categories), selecting a tag (mapped from tags), selecting a date preset (mapped from date_presets strings), and choosing a sortBy option (configured to match supported backend sort codes). It also includes a dedicated quick-filter control wired to the small-kitchen tag ID (small_kitchens) so that clicking it will emit a filter-change event with tagId set to that value. On any change, it emits a consolidated filtersChanged event containing the new categoryId, tagId, datePreset, and sortBy values.

Displays the list of articles returned by listArticles and enables navigation to article detail pages and related products. It receives the current listArticles result (an array of objects containing article_id, title, category_name, tag_names, excerpt, published_at, reading_time_minutes) and a set of reading list items (from getReadingList) to determine which articles are saved. For each article, it renders a card with metadata and a primary click target that navigates to article.html?articleId={article_id}. Each card can show an indicator if its article_id appears in the reading list. Where applicable, it can render a 'Shop Related Products' style link that navigates to shop.html with a preconfigured categoryId corresponding to a relevant product category (for example, kitchen storage). The component does not perform additional filtering or sorting; it reflects the data exactly as returned by listArticles.

Shows the user’s saved articles and allows removal of items. It receives the array from getReadingList, displaying each item using its reading_list_item_id, article_id, title, category_name, and saved_at. When the user clicks on a saved article’s title, the component navigates to article.html?articleId={article_id}. When the user clicks the remove control for a saved article, the component calls removeReadingListItem with the corresponding reading_list_item_id. On a successful response (success true), the component removes the item from its local view and emits an event so the page-shell can optionally reconcile any indicators within the main article list.

Provides a dedicated control for quickly accessing small-kitchen-related articles. It is configured to use the known small_kitchens tag ID (from the Tag model). When activated, it emits an event with an updated filter set where tagId is set to small_kitchens (and, if desired, a corresponding categoryId and sortBy that favor recency). The URL-state-manager uses this event to update the URL and trigger a new listArticles call, ensuring that small-kitchen articles are listed and the first one (typically the newest) is immediately visible.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strong, linear, top-to-bottom flow with a centered single main column. At the top of the page shell, a bold red page heading ('Blog & Resources') introduces the content. Directly beneath it sits a large, prominent white card that combines the filter bar at the top and the article list below it, mirroring the 'card with vertical bullet list' pattern from the mockup. After this card, a slice of neutral background separates it from a full-width olive-green band that carries a concise, uppercase message and the small-kitchen quick filter. A floating reading-list control on the right edge follows the mockup’s floating FAB pattern without interrupting the main vertical reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strong, linear, top-to-bottom flow with a centered single main column. At the top of the page shell, a bold red page heading ('Blog & Resources') introduces the content. Directly beneath it sits a large, prominent white card that combines the filter bar at the top and the article list below it, mirroring the 'card with vertical bullet list' pattern from the mockup. After this card, a slice of neutral background separates it from a full-width olive-green band that carries a concise, uppercase message and the small-kitchen quick filter. A floating reading-list control on the right edge follows the mockup’s floating FAB pattern without interrupting the main vertical reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the main content is constrained to a centered column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (about 8–10 of 12 grid columns), with 15–20% margins on each side. The white card fills this column, with generous internal padding (~24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal). The olive-green band below is visually full-width across the content area (color can safely run edge-to-edge) but its text remains aligned to the same central column. Vertical gaps of 32–48px separate the heading from the card, and the card from the band, maintaining clear section boundaries. The reading-list trigger floats in the right outer margin and uses minimal width so it doesn’t compete with the central column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a spacious, marketing-style density: large, readable typography and comfortable line heights. Within the card, the filter bar elements are well spaced horizontally and/or stacked vertically on small screens, and each article item has 12–20px of vertical breathing room. The card’s generous padding keeps the content feeling light, even with a longer list of articles. The olive band uses a single line (or two short lines) of uppercase text with ample vertical padding (24–32px) so it feels like a bold, but not heavy, narrative banner rather than a dense control cluster.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the bold red heading, then drops directly into the large white card. Inside the card, attention moves from the filter controls at the top downward through the article list, guided by strong left alignment and consistent spacing, echoing the mockup’s bullet-list reading rhythm. At the bottom of the card, the eye naturally continues downward into the high-contrast olive-green band, which acts as a narrative bridge and quick filter CTA for small-kitchen content. Throughout, a subtle floating reading-list trigger on the right serves as a peripheral, secondary attractor, similar to the mockup’s floating blue buttons, available at any scroll position but never interrupting the main vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description: Visually, the Blog & Resources page feels like a modern, editorial landing section built on a single, centered column. A warm, lightly textured background fills the viewport, while a clean central content strip, roughly two-thirds of the screen width, is centered with generous left and right margins. At the top of this strip, a bold red 'Blog & Resources' heading anchors the page. Directly beneath it, a large white card dominates the central area, with soft rounded corners and generous padding. The top portion of this card houses the blog filters and search in a single, cohesive bar; below that, a vertical list of article summaries flows down like well-spaced marketing bullets, left-aligned and easy to scan. After the last article, the card ends and there is a comfortable band of background whitespace before a full-width olive-green stripe cuts across the page. This stripe, still aligned to the same central column, carries an uppercase prompt about small-kitchen content, paired with a prominent quick-filter control. As the user scrolls, a small, colored reading-list control floats unobtrusively along the right edge of the viewport, in the outer margin, ready to slide in a reading-list panel when needed but always secondary to the main central storyline.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-page-shell spans the full viewport width and height, acting as the structural canvas. It uses a warm, subtly textured background to echo the homey, domestic feel. Centered within this shell is the primary content column: on desktop it occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (approximately 8–10 out of 12 grid columns), with equal left and right gutters taking about 15–20% each. This central column is aligned to the vertical centerline of the page and runs from the top padding (around 48–64px from the top edge) down to the footer. At the top of the column, the page shell renders the red page title and any brief description. Directly below that, separated by about 32–40px of whitespace, it contains the large white card that wraps the blog-filter-bar and article-list. Another 32–48px gap below the card leads into the full-width olive-green band that houses the small-kitchen-quick-filter. The reading-list-panel component is anchored within this shell but visually detaches from the column as a floating overlay along the right side. On tablet and mobile, the same linear vertical stacking is preserved, but the central column widens proportionally (up to nearly 100% on small screens) while maintaining generous vertical spacing between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static page container: full-width, min-height 100vh, manages a centered main column. All main components are stacked vertically within it in a linear flow. It also defines the right-side area used by the floating reading-list-trigger so that on wider screens the trigger sits in the outer gutter rather than overlapping the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-url-state-manager is a non-visual, structural component that lives near the top of the page shell’s DOM hierarchy. It does not render any visible elements, borders, or spacing. Conceptually it sits 'behind' the main content, listening to filter interactions and scroll positions to keep the URL in sync, but it does not create any boxes, rows, or columns in the visual layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and space-neutral: it occupies no visible space, does not affect flow layout, and has static positioning purely for logical/behavioral purposes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-filter-bar is positioned at the very top of the large white card, directly under the page heading, and spans the full internal width of that card. The card itself is centered and matches the main column width (roughly 60–70% of the viewport on desktop), with the filter bar sitting inside it with 24–32px of top padding and 32–40px of horizontal padding on both sides. The filter bar’s controls (e.g., category dropdowns, tag chips, search field) are left-aligned along a single horizontal row where space allows, with a comfortable 16–24px gap separating them, preserving the airy, marketing feel. On narrower viewports, these elements naturally wrap or stack vertically, maintaining at least 12–16px vertical spacing between rows. A subtle horizontal divider or increased spacing (about 16px) separates the bottom of the filter bar from the first article item, so users perceive it as the 'control header' of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main card: it scrolls with the page as part of the white card. There is no internal sticky behavior; the filter bar simply occupies the upper portion of the card and moves out of view as the user scrolls down the article list, keeping the flow feeling editorial rather than app-like.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list occupies the entire lower portion of the main white card beneath the blog-filter-bar. It aligns flush with the left and right padding of the card, maintaining the same 32–40px horizontal insets. Vertically, it begins roughly 16–24px below the filter bar (or a thin divider line), and then flows down as a single-column stack of article entries. Each article item reads like a richer, elongated bullet: on desktop each row uses a simple left-to-right structure with a small leading visual (e.g., category icon, thumbnail, or chevron-style marker) taking about 10–15% of the row width on the left, and the article title and teaser text occupying the remaining 85–90% on the right. Items are left-aligned and separated by 12–20px of vertical spacing, preserving a low-to-medium density. The card’s bottom padding (24–32px) creates a comfortable visual landing after the final article. On longer lists, this card stretches vertically and the entire page scrolls; there is no internal scroll container inside the card, maintaining a continuous editorial reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding within the main white card. The article-list simply grows as items are added and scrolls with the rest of the page content. On mobile, each article item collapses to a simple full-width block, with the icon or thumbnail stacked above or to the left of the text, while keeping the same generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-panel manifests as a subtle but always-available side utility, echoing the mockup’s floating blue buttons. In its collapsed state, it appears as a small floating pill or circular FAB anchored along the right edge of the viewport, roughly in the middle to lower third of the screen height. This trigger resides in the right gutter, just outside the main content column, so it never overlaps article text on desktop. It uses a contrasting accent color (e.g., a friendly blue) and a concise label or icon (e.g., a bookmark or heart) with a minimal badge count, and is surrounded by about 12–16px of padding to feel tappable yet unobtrusive. When activated, the panel slides in from the right as a vertical side sheet. On desktop, this sheet occupies approximately 25–30% of the viewport width (around 320–360px) and extends from near the top safe area (e.g., 64px from the top edge, to stay clear of any global nav) down to 16–24px above the bottom. It overlays the page content rather than pushing the main column, with a slight drop shadow and radius on the inner edge to keep the look soft and domestic. Inside the panel, a white background and 24–32px padding frame a vertical list of saved articles, each using a compact row layout similar to the main article list but slightly denser. On small screens, the panel repositions to slide up from the bottom, taking most of the viewport height (e.g., 80–90%) and full width, still feeling like an overlay sheet that can be dismissed to return to the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating and fixed. The collapsed trigger button is fixed to the right side of the viewport, maintaining a consistent position as the user scrolls. When expanded, the reading-list panel becomes a fixed overlay sheet that can scroll internally if its content exceeds the available height. It does not affect the layout flow of the central column—opening or closing it does not reflow the article list; it simply appears above the main content with an optional scrim behind it for focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The small-kitchen-quick-filter is presented prominently within a full-width olive-green horizontal band located directly below the main white card, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical whitespace. This band spans the entire width of the page shell and can be visually full-bleed (color touching the left and right edges of the viewport), but its textual content is constrained to the same centered column as the card, maintaining alignment and order. The band has generous vertical padding—roughly 24–32px at the top and bottom—so that it reads as a substantial banner rather than a thin strip. At the center of the band, a short line of uppercase text (e.g., 'FOCUSED ON SMALL KITCHENS?') is horizontally centered and set in a bold, clear typeface. Directly beneath or beside this text (depending on viewport width) sits the quick-filter control: a prominent pill-shaped button or toggle labeled something like 'Show small-kitchen articles only.' On desktop, the uppercase text and the pill can be on a single line, centered as a compact grouping; on narrower screens they can stack, with the text above and the button below, keeping at least 12–16px of vertical spacing between them. The color contrast between the olive band and the surrounding neutral background makes this section a clear narrative bridge and CTA, inviting users to pivot the article list to small-kitchen content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band within the vertical flow of the page. It remains anchored below the main card and scrolls like any other section. Activating the quick filter changes the state of the article list and URL (via the state manager) but does not change the physical position of the band; any active-state feedback (e.g., a highlighted pill or subtle text change) is handled in-place within this band.

name: Article Detail
title: Article
description: Displays a single article with full content, lets the user save it to a reading list, and provides access to related downloadable resources that link to their detail pages.

core_features: Load and display a single article’s full content, metadata, and images based on the articleId URL parameter using getArticleDetails.
Indicate whether the article is already saved to the reading list, and allow the user to save it via saveArticleToReadingList.
List related downloadable resources returned by getArticleDetails, clearly showing their type and whether they are free or paid.
Allow navigation from each related resource to its detail page on download_detail.html with the appropriate downloadId URL parameter.
Provide navigation back to the main blog/resources listing (blog.html) and to the cart (cart.html) while reading an article.
user_workflows:
Viewing an article from the blog:
1. User clicks an article on blog.html; browser navigates to article.html with ?articleId={id}.
2. On page load, the controller reads articleId from the query string.
3. The page calls getArticleDetails(articleId).
4. When the response is received, the article header, metadata, and content components render the title, category, tags, publish date, reading time, hero image, and full content.
5. The related resources list renders all related_resources from the response.
6. The reading list control initializes its state from is_saved_to_reading_list.

Saving an article to the reading list:
1. On article load, the page checks is_saved_to_reading_list.
2. If false, a “Save to Reading List” action is enabled.
3. User clicks the save action.
4. The page calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId).
5. On success, the reading list control updates its local state to indicate the article is saved (e.g., disables the button and/or changes its label) using the returned reading_list_item_id and saved_at if needed for display.
6. If the API returns an error (success=false), an error message is displayed and the control remains in the unsaved state.

Opening a related downloadable resource from an article:
1. User scrolls to the related resources section.
2. Each related resource item displays data from related_resources, including title, type, and a price label derived from price and is_free.
3. User clicks a related resource item.
4. The page navigates to download_detail.html with ?downloadId={download_id} for the selected resource.
5. The user can later navigate back to the article via browser back or links defined on other pages.

Navigating while reading an article:
1. In the article header or breadcrumb, user clicks a link to return to blog.html.
2. The page navigates to blog.html (optionally including query parameters determined by the target page, but not required here).
3. At any time, user can click the cart icon in the site header.
4. The page navigates to cart.html with no additional parameters from this page.
interactions: On page load (or when the articleId URL parameter changes), call getArticleDetails(articleId) and propagate the response to all bound components.
Clicking the “Save to Reading List” button triggers saveArticleToReadingList(articleId); on success, update the saved state to prevent duplicate saves; on failure, show an error notification.
Clicking a related resource card/row navigates to download_detail.html?downloadId={selectedResource.download_id}.
Clicking the breadcrumb or back link navigates to blog.html.
Clicking the cart icon in the header navigates to cart.html.
Optional: Clicking a tag chip can navigate to blog.html with a tag-related query parameter (e.g., blog.html?tag={tagName}), without calling additional APIs from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is driven by the articleId URL parameter and the responses from getArticleDetails and saveArticleToReadingList.

- Initial load:
  - The controller reads articleId from the query string (e.g., article.html?articleId={id}).
  - If articleId is present, the page calls getArticleDetails(articleId).
  - If articleId is missing or empty, the controller shows an error state indicating that no article can be loaded.

- After getArticleDetails response:
  - The Article object (title, content, category_name, tag_names, hero_image_url, published_at, reading_time_minutes) is stored in in-memory page state and bound to the article header and content components.
  - The boolean is_saved_to_reading_list is stored in state to determine the initial configuration of the reading list control (button enabled/disabled, label, icon).
  - The related_resources array is stored in state and passed to the related resources list component.

- Reading list state:
  - The initial saved/unsaved state is derived solely from is_saved_to_reading_list returned by getArticleDetails.
  - When saveArticleToReadingList(articleId) is called and returns success=true, the reading list component updates its local state to represent the saved status; subsequent clicks are either disabled or ignored.
  - This page does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence; any persistence beyond the current view is managed by the backend and reflected via getArticleDetails on subsequent visits.

- URL-driven navigation:
  - Selecting a related resource changes the browser location to download_detail.html?downloadId={download_id}; no additional API is called from this page for that interaction.
  - Navigating back to the blog and to the cart changes the browser location to blog.html and cart.html respectively, without modifying the state of this page.
  - If the user arrives at article.html again with a different articleId in the querystring, the controller detects the change (e.g., via a routing or popstate listener), resets local article state, and calls getArticleDetails with the new articleId.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of article data based on the articleId URL parameter, manages loading and error states, and distributes the getArticleDetails response to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read articleId from the querystring and call getArticleDetails(articleId).
onUrlChange: When articleId in the URL changes, clear current article-related state and call getArticleDetails(newArticleId).
onGetArticleDetailsSuccess: Store article data, is_saved_to_reading_list, and related_resources; notify child components to render.
onGetArticleDetailsError: Set an error state and trigger the error-notification component to display an appropriate message.

components.functionality: Provides basic navigation controls accessible from the article page, including a link back to the blog/resources listing and a cart icon that leads to the cart page.
event_handlers: onClickBackToResources: Navigate to blog.html using standard link navigation.
onClickCartIcon: Navigate to cart.html using standard link navigation.

components.functionality: Displays article metadata such as title, category name, optional tags, publish date, reading time, and the hero image, and optionally includes a breadcrumb-like link back to the blog listing.
event_handlers: onClickTag: (Optional) Navigate to blog.html with a tag query parameter, e.g., blog.html?tag={tagName}, without invoking new APIs from this page.

components.functionality: Renders the full article content returned by getArticleDetails, including any headings, lists, and inline images as provided in the content field.
event_handlers: onContentRendered: (Optional) Scroll to top or manage internal anchors if present; no external APIs are called.

components.functionality: Allows the user to save the current article to the reading list and reflects whether the article is already saved.
event_handlers: onInitState: Read is_saved_to_reading_list from the page controller’s article state to set the initial button state (enabled if false, disabled or visually marked as saved if true).
onClickSave: If the article is not yet saved, call saveArticleToReadingList(articleId).

onSaveSuccess: When saveArticleToReadingList returns success=true, update the control to the saved state and optionally store reading_list_item_id and saved_at for display; prevent additional save calls for this article.
onSaveError: Display an error message via the error-notification component and keep the control in the unsaved state so the user can retry.

components.functionality: Displays a list of downloadable resources related to the current article, with clear indication of resource type and whether each item is free or paid, and provides navigation to the resource detail page.
event_handlers: onInitResources: Receive the related_resources array from the page controller (populated by getArticleDetails) and render each item.
onClickResourceItem: Navigate to download_detail.html?downloadId={resource.download_id} when a resource is selected.
onEmptyResources: If related_resources is empty or undefined, hide the list or show a non-data-specific message indicating no related resources are available.

components.functionality: Shows error or status messages related to article loading and reading list operations.
event_handlers: onShowArticleLoadError: Triggered by the page controller when getArticleDetails fails; displays a generic error and may suggest reloading the page.
onShowSaveError: Triggered by the reading-list-control when saveArticleToReadingList returns success=false or encounters a network error; displays a generic retryable message.
onDismiss: Allows the user to close the visible message; this only affects local UI state and does not call any API.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strong top-to-bottom single-column narrative. The global site header sits at the very top, followed by an optional full-width error message band. Below that, a subtle page controller row aligns with the main content column. The core of the page is a large, centered white article card that contains the article header at the top and the full article body beneath it. After a clear vertical break, a full-width olive-green band with centered, uppercase copy bridges to the related-resources area. The related resources themselves are presented in a vertically stacked list aligned to the same central column. A floating reading-list action button sits to the right of the main column as a secondary, persistent control.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strong top-to-bottom single-column narrative. The global site header sits at the very top, followed by an optional full-width error message band. Below that, a subtle page controller row aligns with the main content column. The core of the page is a large, centered white article card that contains the article header at the top and the full article body beneath it. After a clear vertical break, a full-width olive-green band with centered, uppercase copy bridges to the related-resources area. The related resources themselves are presented in a vertically stacked list aligned to the same central column. A floating reading-list action button sits to the right of the main column as a secondary, persistent control.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered column occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 15–20% margins on each side. The article card uses generous internal padding (around 24–32px vertically and 32–40px horizontally). The olive band is full-width across the content area (edge to edge of the main layout, not the full browser chrome), with tall vertical padding so it feels like a substantial banner. The related resources list sits in the same central column width as the article card, with comfortable spacing between items. The floating reading-list control occupies a small footprint on the right side, overlapping the background rather than the primary column, so it does not compete for horizontal space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low-to-medium. The article card uses large typography and generous line height, with clear separation between header and body. Paragraphs are spaced with at least one base unit (8px) and section breaks with 16–24px. The related resources list uses clear separation between items (8–16px) and avoids cramming too many elements per row, leaning into simple, vertically stacked items. Overall, breathing room between sections (32–48px) keeps the page feeling editorial and approachable rather than crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the prominent site header and then immediately into the article header within the white card, which acts as the main focal block. The user’s eye moves downward through the article body in a consistent left-aligned text column. After the article, the strong contrast of the olive-green band pulls attention down and signals a narrative transition toward additional value (related resources). Below this band, the eye naturally continues into the related resources list. Throughout, a floating reading-list FAB on the right provides a peripheral but noticeable secondary action, while error notifications, when present, appear directly under the header so they are seen before the article without disrupting the main card structure.

layout.overall_description: The page reads as a tall, centered editorial column floating over a warm, subtly textured background. At the very top, the site’s navigation bar spans the full width, establishing the brand frame. Directly beneath it, any error message appears as a slim, full-width band that momentarily claims attention before the user’s gaze settles on the main content. Aligned to the center of the page, a wide but clearly bounded white card dominates the above-the-fold area; its top portion holds the article’s title and meta details, and its lower portion flows into the article body text with comfortable margins and generous line spacing. The card sits within wide left and right margins, making it feel important but not heavy. As the user scrolls and finishes the article, a slice of whitespace leads into a full-width olive-green band that cuts horizontally across the content area with bold, uppercase text, acting like a narrative bridge to deeper engagement. Below this banner, within the same central column width as the article card, the related resources list appears as a simple, well-spaced vertical list. On the right side of the viewport, a rounded floating action button hovers midway down the page, aligned roughly with the upper half of the article card; it stays visible during scroll, offering quick access to saving the article without intruding on the reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller sits directly below the site header (and below any error notification if present), aligned to the same central content column as the article card. It appears as a low-height horizontal strip that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with 16–24px top padding from the header and 16px bottom padding before the article card. Within this strip, breadcrumb or back-navigation elements are left-aligned while any secondary controls (like layout toggles) are right-aligned, but all content remains within the central column bounds, leaving wide margins on both sides. The styling is subtle (light text, minimal borders) so that it supports navigation context without pulling focus away from the large white article card directly beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that flows with the document, always positioned above the article card and below the header/error band, maintaining consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site header navigation occupies the very top of the page, spanning the full browser width. It has a height of approximately 64–80px, with content horizontally centered and aligned to the same central column as the rest of the page. Logo and primary navigation links sit in a horizontal row, with generous horizontal padding so they do not appear crowded. The header’s visual weight is solid but clean, using brand colors while remaining flatter and more structural than the content below. There is a clear visual separation (e.g., a slight shadow or subtle bottom border) that distinguishes it from the article content that begins below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky or fixed to the top of the viewport on scroll, ensuring navigation is always accessible while the main content scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header sits at the very top of the large central white article card, occupying the full card width inside its padding. The card itself is centered on the page and spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 32–40px horizontal padding and 24–32px padding above and below. Inside this, the article title appears as a bold, large-type heading, centered horizontally to echo the mockup’s dramatic top heading. Directly beneath the title, 8–16px down, the meta information (author, date, reading time, tags) is presented in a single or stacked row, with smaller text and possibly muted color. Overall, the header block consumes roughly the top 20–25% of the article card height above the fold, creating a strong first impression before the body text begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the top of the article card; as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen with the rest of the card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article content fills the remainder of the white article card beneath the article header. It is left-aligned within the card but still centered on the page overall, using a comfortable text measure (approximately 60–70 characters per line) that may be narrower than the full card width to enhance readability. Vertical spacing between paragraphs is at least 8–12px, with larger gaps of 16–24px between major subsections or visual elements (images, block quotes, lists). Any in-article lists echo the mockup’s simple icon-plus-text structure: a small chevron or bullet on the left with the list text to the right, aligned along a subtle internal column. The bottom of the article card ends with 24–32px of padding below the last line of text, then drops into a slice of background whitespace (32–48px) before the olive-green band appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the central white card; it does not fix or float, but naturally scrolls as part of the main document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list control appears as a floating action button on the right side of the viewport, visually detached from the main article card but vertically aligned with it. On desktop, it is positioned approximately in the upper-middle third of the viewport height (around the mid-height of the visible article card) and offset from the right edge by about 24–32px. The button itself is circular or pill-shaped, medium-sized, and uses a contrasting brand color (e.g., the blue seen in the mockup’s FABs) so it is visible but not overpowering. It casts a slight shadow over the textured background, not over the article card; it never overlaps the main text column. As the user scrolls through the article, the button remains in place, providing a constant, easy-to-reach way to add or remove the article from the reading list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport on desktop (floating FAB behavior). On smaller screens, it can reposition to the bottom-right corner above content with a slight bottom margin so it remains reachable without obscuring text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related resources list is placed below the olive-green bridge band, aligned to the same central column as the article card. After the band, there is a vertical gap of about 24–32px, then the list begins. It spans approximately 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with 24px top and bottom padding within its container to give the section a light but intentional feel (this container can be a subtle, low-elevation panel or simply content on the background). Each resource item is a horizontal row occupying the full list width, with 12–16px vertical spacing between items. Items follow a simplified version of the mockup’s bullet pattern: a small chevron or document icon on the left and the resource title and brief description on the right, all left-aligned. The overall density is moderate, allowing users to scan 3–6 resources comfortably without feeling cluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that appears after the olive band in the natural scroll order; it expands vertically as more resources are present but always maintains the central-column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notification appears as a slim, full-width band just below the site header and above the page controller. It stretches across the entire content area, matching the header’s width from edge to edge of the page, but its internal text is aligned to the same central column as the article card. The band has approximately 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, with a background color using the brand’s red accent to signal urgency, and white or very dark text for legibility. When present, it pushes the rest of the content downward, maintaining consistent spacing below (16–24px before the page controller), and when dismissed or absent, the main content shifts up to reclaim the space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing in the normal document flow directly beneath the header; it may slide in or out but does not overlay or float over the article card.

name: Download Detail
title: Download Detail
description: Displays a single downloadable resource based on the downloadId URL parameter and allows the user to add the free or paid download to the cart.

core_features: Read the downloadId from the querystring and load the corresponding downloadable resource via getDownloadDetails.
Display the resource’s title, description, price (including indication if it is free), file format, and delivery note.
Display a preview of the resource when a preview_image_url is available.
Provide an Add to Cart interaction that uses addDownloadToCart with the current downloadId and selected quantity.
Handle both free and paid downloads, adjusting labels and messaging based on the is_free flag.
Show loading and error states when fetching the download details or when adding the item to the cart fails.
Optionally show navigation links back to related articles and navigate to article.html with the appropriate articleId URL parameter when such relationships are available from the loaded data.
After a successful addDownloadToCart call, navigate to cart.html so the user can review their cart.
user_workflows:
View download details and add to cart from an article or blog listing:
1. User clicks a download link on article.html or blog.html that points to download_detail.html with a downloadId in the querystring.
2. On page load, the download-detail-loader component reads the downloadId from the URL.
3. The loader calls getDownloadDetails(downloadId) and sets the page into a loading state.
4. When getDownloadDetails returns successfully, the loader updates shared page state with the returned details (title, description, price, is_free, file_format, preview_image_url, delivery_note) and moves the page into a loaded state.
5. The download-info-panel and download-preview components render those details.
6. The download-purchase-panel initializes a default quantity and configures the Add to Cart button based on is_free and price.
7. User optionally adjusts quantity.
8. User clicks Add to Cart, triggering addDownloadToCart(downloadId, quantity).
9. While the request is in progress, the Add to Cart button is disabled and shows a pending state to prevent duplicate submissions.
10. If addDownloadToCart returns success, the page navigates to cart.html.
11. If addDownloadToCart returns failure, an error message from the response is shown in the download-purchase-panel and the button is re-enabled.

Handling free vs paid download messaging:
1. After getDownloadDetails resolves, the download-purchase-panel reads is_free and price from the shared download detail state.
2. If is_free is true, the panel displays messaging and button labeling appropriate for a free download (e.g., indicating no payment is required in checkout) while still routing through the cart.
3. If is_free is false, the panel shows the price per item, and any quantity-based calculated subtotal for display, without hardcoding any values.
4. The addDownloadToCart call is used in both cases; for free downloads the resulting cart status will later drive a no-payment-required flow at checkout.

Navigating back to related articles (when available):
1. After getDownloadDetails resolves, if the resulting data includes information that can be mapped to related articles (such as related article IDs provided by the backend in association with the download), the related-articles-navigation component renders one or more links.
2. Each link, when clicked, navigates to article.html with an articleId query parameter corresponding to the chosen related article.
3. No additional interfaces are invoked by this page for the navigation; it depends only on the IDs made available alongside the download details and uses URL-based navigation.

Error handling for missing or invalid downloadId:
1. If the page loads without a downloadId query parameter, the download-detail-loader sets the page into an error state indicating that the resource cannot be found, and no API call is made.
2. If a downloadId is present but getDownloadDetails(downloadId) returns an error or an invalid/empty response, the loader sets an error state explaining that the download details could not be loaded.
3. The download-error-state component renders this error state and hides or disables the download-info-panel and download-purchase-panel.
4. In all error cases, the Add to Cart action is disabled since a valid download resource is not available.
interactions:
Page load and parameter resolution:
- On initial load, the page reads the downloadId from the querystring.
- If downloadId is present, an automatic call to getDownloadDetails(downloadId) is triggered.
- If the user navigates to a different URL on the same page (e.g., download_detail.html with a different downloadId) and the application supports in-place routing, the loader detects the change and re-calls getDownloadDetails with the new ID.

Add to Cart button click:
- User presses the Add to Cart button inside the download-purchase-panel.
- The current quantity input value is validated (e.g., positive integer) by the component logic.
- On valid input, the component calls addDownloadToCart(downloadId, quantity).
- During the API call, the button is disabled and an in-progress state is shown to avoid duplicate submissions.
- On success, the component reads success, cart_id, cart_item_id, and message from the response and redirects the user to cart.html.
- On failure, the component displays the message value as an error and re-enables the Add to Cart button.

Quantity input change:
- User changes the quantity in a numeric input within the download-purchase-panel.
- The component validates the new value and updates internal state.
- Any quantity-based derived display (such as a non-persistent estimated line total) updates reactively based on price from getDownloadDetails and the current quantity value, without storing static prices.

Related article link click (if rendered):
- User clicks on a related article link in the related-articles-navigation component.
- The click handler constructs a URL to article.html with an articleId query parameter corresponding to the selected related article.
- The browser navigates to that URL; no page APIs are called for this interaction.

Retry loading after error:
- If an error state is shown (e.g., API failure), and a Retry button is offered, clicking it triggers a new getDownloadDetails(downloadId) call using the same downloadId from the URL.
- The loader enters a loading state, then either returns to the loaded state on success or shows error again on failure.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is controlled by the downloadId URL query parameter. When the page loads, it reads this parameter from the querystring. If downloadId is missing, the loader sets an error state and no API calls are made. If downloadId is present, the page transitions into a loading state while getDownloadDetails(downloadId) is called. The result of getDownloadDetails populates the current download detail state: fields such as title, description, price, is_free, file_format, preview_image_url, and delivery_note become the single source of truth for all downstream components. The page may hold a separate UI state for loading, loaded, or error, which determines whether components like download-info-panel and download-purchase-panel are rendered or disabled. A local UI state for the selected quantity is maintained within the download-purchase-panel but always combined with dynamic price data from getDownloadDetails for any calculations. When the user clicks Add to Cart, the page enters an add-to-cart pending state for that interaction, during which the Add to Cart button is disabled. On successful addDownloadToCart execution, the page state triggers navigation to cart.html, and no additional local state about the cart is persisted on this page. If the URL downloadId changes (for example, via internal routing or a manual URL change), the loader detects that the parameter value is different, resets the loading state, and calls getDownloadDetails again for the new ID, replacing the previous download detail state.

components.functionality: Reads the downloadId from the querystring, validates it, calls getDownloadDetails(downloadId), and manages global loading and error states for the page. On success, it exposes the download detail data to child components; on failure or missing ID, it sets an error state that other components can observe.

Displays error messages when the download details cannot be loaded (e.g., missing downloadId or failed getDownloadDetails call) and optionally renders a Retry control that instructs the loader to attempt getDownloadDetails again.

Shows the core metadata for the selected downloadable resource once it is loaded: title, description, file_format, price, is_free indicator, and delivery_note. It only renders when the loader indicates that valid data is available and hides or shows specific fields based on what is present in the getDownloadDetails response.

Renders a visual preview of the downloadable resource when preview_image_url is present in the loaded details. If no preview_image_url is available, the component renders nothing. It does not initiate any downloads; it only displays preview content based on the existing data.

Allows the user to set a quantity and add the current downloadable resource to the cart using addDownloadToCart. It reads price and is_free from the loaded details to adjust labels and optional subtotal display. On Add to Cart, it validates the quantity and calls addDownloadToCart(downloadId, quantity); on success it triggers navigation to cart.html, and on failure it shows an error message and restores the button state.

When related article identifiers are available in association with the loaded download details, this component renders a list of links back to those articles. Each link points to article.html with an articleId query parameter. It does not fetch articles itself; it only uses the relationship metadata provided alongside the download details, if present.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom, single-column flow. At the center of the page sits one dominant white content card that contains the core download experience: first the download information, then the preview, then the purchase/add-to-cart controls. Above this card (handled by the info panel) the title acts as the bold entry point. Below the card, a full-width olive-green band contains the related-articles navigation, acting as a narrative bridge to more content. Loader and error states temporarily replace the main card area but keep the same centered, single-column structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom, single-column flow. At the center of the page sits one dominant white content card that contains the core download experience: first the download information, then the preview, then the purchase/add-to-cart controls. Above this card (handled by the info panel) the title acts as the bold entry point. Below the card, a full-width olive-green band contains the related-articles navigation, acting as a narrative bridge to more content. Loader and error states temporarily replace the main card area but keep the same centered, single-column structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, all primary content (info, preview, purchase) is constrained to a centered column about 70–80% of the viewport width, equivalent to ~8–10 columns of a 12-column grid, with 10–15% margins on each side. The white card has generous internal padding (~24–32px vertical, ~32–40px horizontal). The olive-green band for related content is full-width across the main content area, with ample vertical padding (~24–32px) to feel like a substantial banner. Loader and error states occupy the same central column area and roughly 50–70% of the viewport height, keeping the focus in the middle of the screen.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium density with plenty of breathing room: large headings, comfortable line heights, and 16–24px vertical spacing between meaningful elements. Within the card, each grouping (info, preview, purchase) is clearly separated with spacing rather than tight stacking. The related-articles list uses list-item spacing of 8–16px vertically, so it’s easy to scan while still feeling light. Nothing feels crowded; this is more like a marketing or editorial page than a dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow is a strong vertical column. The eye lands first on the red-accented title in the info panel at the top of the card, then naturally moves downward through the explanation copy into the preview (a visual focal point), and then to the purchase panel where the main CTA lives. After the card ends, a slice of whitespace leads the eye into the contrasting olive-green band, which visually pulls the user into the related-articles navigation. Loader and error states, when present, sit in the same central region, immediately grabbing attention without introducing new layout patterns.

layout.overall_description: The Download Detail page presents a warm, focused, single-column experience centered on a large white card floating above a subtly textured, homey background. At the heart of the page, this card sits in the middle of the viewport, spanning roughly three-quarters of the screen width on desktop, with generous lateral margins that keep it feeling prominent yet not full-bleed. Inside the card, content flows vertically in three clear segments: a bold, red-accented title and descriptive info at the top; a comfortably sized preview of the downloadable resource in the middle; and a well-spaced purchase/add-to-cart panel at the bottom with a strong, full-width CTA button. The card’s interior uses ample padding so each chunk of information feels distinct and easy to read. When loading or if an error occurs, that same central area is taken over by a centered status display, preserving the familiar layout frame. Below the main card, separated by a calm strip of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band stretches across the page, with centered content that invites the user to continue into related articles. This band feels like a narrative bridge, using uppercase text and generous vertical padding to echo the marketing-style banner from the mockup. Overall, the page reads top to bottom in a single, confident column: core download details first, then a strong, colored bridge into additional supporting content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the central content column where the main white card normally sits. On desktop, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible portion of the page, roughly in the middle 60–70% of the viewport height. It aligns to the same width as the main card (about 70–80% of the viewport width, max ~960–1040px) so that the spinner and loading message feel anchored in the same structural grid as the rest of the content. Above and below the loader there is ample whitespace, ensuring the animation and text stand out without feeling cramped. When active, it visually replaces the card’s content rather than appearing as a small inline element, preserving the single-column focus.
