component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal document flow in place of the main card content, with its container centered in the viewport. The loader’s inner content is center-aligned horizontally and vertically within this container, but it does not use fixed or sticky positioning; it scrolls with the page if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state is presented as a white status card sitting in the same centered column as the main content card. It occupies the same approximate width (70–80% of the viewport) and uses generous internal padding (~24–32px vertical, ~32–40px horizontal). The primary error message is aligned toward the top center of the card, using a red accent icon or text to echo the main heading style, with the detailed explanation and possible retry or navigation actions stacked below in a single vertical column. There is comfortable whitespace above the card and below it before the start of any subsequent sections, making the error state feel like the dominant, self-contained section of the page when it appears. If the error prevents any download data from loading, this card replaces the main info/preview/purchase content entirely.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level card in the main single-column flow. It is not fixed or floating; instead, it anchors at the main content region and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The download info panel forms the upper section of the main white content card centered on the page. This card spans about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, with equal left and right margins of roughly 10–15%, and a maximum width around 960–1040px. Inside the card, the info panel occupies the full width of the card minus padding, positioned at the very top with generous top padding (~24–32px) separating it from the card edges. The download title is center or slightly left-of-center aligned within this column, using large, bold type with a red accent to mirror the prominent heading from the mockup. Below the title, any subtitle or descriptive paragraph flows in a single vertical stack, left-aligned, with 16–24px spacing between lines of copy blocks to maintain an editorial feel. Any meta information (file type, size, estimated reading time, etc.) appears beneath the description as a short, neatly spaced row or stacked micro-sections, still within the same column, using subtle color or weight changes rather than tight groupings. The bottom of the info panel leaves a clear vertical gap (~24px) before the preview component begins, visually separating the informational overview from the more visual preview while keeping everything clearly inside one unified card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives as the top segment within the main content card in the natural document flow. It stretches to fill the card’s width (minus padding) but does not scroll independently or use sticky behavior; it moves with the card as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The download preview sits directly beneath the info panel inside the same white card, maintaining the single-column, centered structure. It spans the internal width of the card (100% of the card’s content width) but preserves the horizontal padding so the preview never touches the card’s edges. On desktop, the preview typically occupies about one-third to one-half of the screen height, creating a strong visual focal area without overwhelming the text content above and below. The preview might present as a framed image or thumbnail of the resource, optionally with a light border radius and soft shadow, centered horizontally with equal whitespace on both sides. Any caption or brief explanatory line appears immediately below the preview image, left-aligned, with 8–16px vertical spacing. A comfortable gap (~24px) is maintained between the bottom of the preview and the start of the purchase panel, which follows, so the user clearly perceives the preview as its own focused section within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main card’s stacked content, it behaves like a responsive block that scales with the card width. It does not float or stick; it simply resizes and reflows within the single column as the viewport changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The purchase panel forms the bottom section of the main white card, directly beneath the preview and still within the same centered, single-column container. It spans the full content width of the card, with the same horizontal padding and a generous top margin (~24px) to visually separate it from the preview section. Internally, the panel is arranged as a vertical stack: price and key offer details appear at the top, left-aligned, using slightly larger or bolder text; any options (such as format selection or quantity) follow in their own rows with 8–16px vertical spacing; and the primary Add to Cart button sits as a wide, visually strong block at the bottom, often spanning nearly the full card width while respecting side padding. The button uses the brand’s bold accent color (likely red) and comfortable vertical padding to echo the marketing-style CTA philosophy of the mockup. Additional helper text (e.g., ‘Instant download’ or ‘No credit card required for free resources’) is placed beneath or beside the button with subtle typography and generous breathing room so it doesn’t clutter the CTA area. The bottom of the purchase panel aligns with the bottom of the card, leaving a clear block of padding (~24–32px) between the final element and the card’s bottom edge, maintaining the spacious, premium feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the final section of the main content card in the normal document flow. On long pages it scrolls naturally and is not fixed; the layout reserves right-side whitespace so potential future floating CTAs could be added without conflicting, but this panel itself remains non-floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles navigation sits below the main white card, separated by a slice of open background space of about 32–40px, and is contained within a full-width olive-green horizontal band that visually mirrors the mockup’s narrative bridge section. This band stretches across the entire content area of the page, from left to right edge of the viewport, while its inner content (heading and list of article links) is constrained to the same centered column width as the main card (approximately 70–80% viewport width, max ~960–1040px). At the top of the band’s inner content area, a short, uppercase heading such as ‘KEEP LEARNING’ or ‘RELATED ARTICLES’ is center- or left-aligned, using high-contrast light text against the olive background and generous top padding (~24px). Below this heading, the related articles are presented in a vertical list within the same column: each article entry is left-aligned, with 8–16px vertical spacing between items, and may include a title and optional short description, keeping density low. Padding at the bottom of the band (~24–32px) ensures the last link does not feel cramped against the band’s edge. The overall effect is a strong, banner-like strip that naturally continues the user’s journey from the download card into supporting content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits as a full-width horizontal band that follows the main card in the normal document flow. It is not sticky or floating; it scrolls with the page and anchors the bottom portion of the layout as a clear, secondary section.

name: Gift Cards
title: Gift Cards
description: Configure and purchase digital or physical gift cards for home decluttering and organizing services, then add them to the cart for checkout.

core_features: Load available gift card products (digital and physical) and gift card designs from the backend using getGiftCardOptions.
Allow users to select a gift card type (digital or physical) based on the loaded GiftCardProduct records, including honoring an optional type URL parameter for pre-selection.

Allow users to choose a gift card amount using predefined suggestions from the selected GiftCardProduct or by entering a custom amount within the product’s allowed min/max range, optionally pre-populated from an amount URL parameter.
Display available GiftCardDesign options and let users select one, with special support for neutral/minimal styles (including honoring a designStyle URL parameter when provided).
Provide fields for occasion (using the GiftCardConfiguration.occasion enum and optionally pre-populated from an occasion URL parameter), recipient name, sender name, and an optional personal message.
Allow users to specify a quantity of identical gift cards to purchase in a single configuration.
Validate the configuration (required selections, amount range, quantity) before submission and display inline error messages if needed.

On submission, call addGiftCardToCart with the selected product, design, amount, occasion, names, message, and quantity, then reflect success or failure and allow navigation to cart.html to choose shipping (for physical cards) and complete checkout.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Configure and add a new gift card from navigation
1. User opens gift_cards.html from the site header or footer without URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page calls getGiftCardOptions.
3. Once data is returned, the page filters to active gift card products and preselects a default type (for example, digital if available, otherwise another available product).
4. The user reviews the selected type and, if desired, switches to another type (e.g., from digital to physical). The selected GiftCardProduct becomes the active context for amount validation and labeling.
5. The user selects a suggested amount from the predefined amounts for that product or enters a custom amount. If a custom amount is entered, client-side validation ensures it falls within the product’s min_amount and max_amount values.
6. The user views the list of designs returned from getGiftCardOptions and selects one. Neutral/minimal options are clearly indicated so the user can pick a low-key design if desired.
7. The user selects an occasion from the supported GiftCardConfiguration.occasion values and enters recipient and sender names. Optionally, the user adds a short personal message.
8. The user adjusts the quantity field if they want multiple identical cards in the same configuration.
9. The user clicks the primary submission button. The page validates all required inputs (type, amount, occasion, design, quantity) and, if valid, calls addGiftCardToCart with the current configuration.
10. If addGiftCardToCart returns success, the page shows a success state and provides a way to continue to cart.html (e.g., via a "Go to Cart" button). If there is an error, the returned message is displayed and the user can correct the configuration and retry.

Workflow 2: Pre-populated configuration from a promotional link
1. User clicks a promotional link leading to gift_cards.html with one or more query parameters, such as type, amount, designStyle, and/or occasion.
2. On page load, the page parses URL parameters and stores them in initial page state, then calls getGiftCardOptions.
3. After data is loaded, the page attempts to:
   - Match the type parameter to a GiftCardProduct.type (case-insensitive). If a match is found and the product is active, it is preselected; otherwise, the default selection logic applies.
   - Pre-fill the amount field with the amount parameter, if present and within the selected product’s min_amount and max_amount. If out of range or invalid, the amount field is left empty for the user to correct.
   - Select a design based on designStyle by searching designs where style matches the parameter or, if the style indicates neutrality/minimalism, by preferring designs flagged as neutral or with an appropriate style. If no matching design is found, the component falls back to its default design selection strategy.
   - Pre-select the occasion dropdown if the occasion parameter maps to one of the allowed GiftCardConfiguration.occasion enum values; otherwise, the occasion field remains unselected.
4. The user reviews or adjusts any pre-populated fields (e.g., changes amount, switches design, adds names, edits the message).
5. The user clicks the submission button. The page validates the configuration and calls addGiftCardToCart.
6. On success, the user can navigate to cart.html to review the cart and select shipping for physical gift cards during checkout.

Workflow 3: Switching between digital and physical gift card types mid-configuration
1. The user starts configuring a gift card as in Workflow 1 and has already chosen an amount, a design, and filled in recipient/sender information.
2. The user changes the selected gift card type (e.g., from digital to physical) using the type selector.
3. When the type changes, the page updates the active GiftCardProduct context. The amount field is revalidated against the newly selected product’s min_amount and max_amount; if the existing amount is outside the new product’s range, a validation message is shown and the user is prompted to adjust the amount.
4. Design selections remain valid because designs are independent of product type. The selected design stays selected unless the user chooses a different one.
5. Recipient, sender, occasion, message, and quantity values are preserved across type changes so the user does not lose text they have entered.
6. The user revalidates the configuration visually, then submits. addGiftCardToCart is called with the currently selected gift card product and other configuration fields. On success, the user proceeds to cart.html.
interactions: On page load, a data-loading interaction calls getGiftCardOptions once, populating in-memory collections of GiftCardProduct and GiftCardDesign entities. While loading, the configuration UI is disabled; if the call fails, an error state is shown with a retry action that calls getGiftCardOptions again.

Gift card type selection is handled by interactive controls (e.g., radio buttons or a selector) bound to the list of active GiftCardProduct records. Changing the selected type updates derived state such as allowed amount ranges and any contextual helper text.

Amount selection allows clicking a predefined amount option (if any) or entering a custom amount field. Clicking a predefined option sets the amount input. Changing the amount input manually clears any previously selected predefined option. On blur or on change, client-side validation checks against the selected product’s min_amount and max_amount and marks the field invalid if out of range.

Design selection is handled via an interactive list/grid bound to GiftCardDesign data. Clicking a design card sets selectedDesignId. A user can toggle a filter for neutral/minimal designs; when enabled, the list is filtered using each design’s style and is_neutral flag and the current selection is checked for validity (if the current design is not in the filtered list, it may be deselected or shown as invalid until changed).

Occasion selection uses a dropdown bound to the GiftCardConfiguration.occasion enum values. Changing the dropdown updates the selected occasion in state. If an invalid or empty value is present on submission, validation prevents submission and shows an error near the field.

Recipient and sender name fields are plain text inputs. They update state on change. On submission, they are validated according to business rules (for example, required or optional depending on design) and errors are shown inline if validation fails.

The optional message field is a multiline text input. As the user types, the value is stored in state. It may enforce a maximum length using client-side validation; exceeding this length results in a validation message and disabled submission until corrected.

The quantity field is a numeric input or stepper. Increment and decrement controls adjust the quantity, constrained to positive integers. Direct entry is validated on change; non-integer or non-positive values trigger validation errors and prevent submission.

The main submission button triggers client-side validation across all configuration fields. If validation passes, it calls addGiftCardToCart with giftCardProductId, designId, amount, occasion, recipientName, senderName, message, and quantity from the current state. While the request is in flight, the button is disabled and a loading indicator may be shown.

After addGiftCardToCart returns, if success is true, a success message is displayed and the user is offered navigation to cart.html (e.g., via a dedicated "Go to Cart" button). If success is false or the call fails, the returned message or a generic error is shown, and the user can correct any issues and resubmit.

A secondary interaction updates the browser URL query parameters when key selections change (such as type and amount), so the current configuration can be shared or bookmarked. This update is done without reloading the page and does not rely on localStorage; when reloaded, the page re-parses the URL and reconstructs the initial state accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads the URL query parameters type, amount, designStyle, and occasion. These values are stored in an in-memory initialState object. The page then calls getGiftCardOptions. Once the API resolves successfully, the page determines the initial selected gift card type by matching the type parameter (if present) against the GiftCardProduct.type field of active products. If no valid match is found, a default product is chosen based on a deterministic rule (for example, preferring a digital type if present). The amount field is pre-filled from the amount parameter only if it is a valid number and within the selected product’s min_amount and max_amount; otherwise the amount field remains empty until the user interacts. The designStyle parameter is used to search the loaded GiftCardDesign collection: first by exact style match (case-insensitive), then by related attributes (e.g., is_neutral when the style implies neutrality). If a match is found, that design is preselected; otherwise, the page applies its normal default selection logic. The occasion parameter is mapped to the GiftCardConfiguration.occasion enum; only recognized values are applied to the occasion field; unrecognized values are ignored. After the initial application of URL parameters, page state is driven by user interactions, not by further automatic URL parsing. Optionally, when the user changes key configuration fields such as type, amount, or occasion, the page updates the current URL's querystring using the History API so that these values are reflected in the URL (e.g., updating type and amount). These updates do not trigger a reload and are used solely for shareable deep-linking. At no point does the page rely on localStorage or other browser storage; all persistent sharable state is encoded via the URL parameters, and all transient state (selected product, design, values in the form, validation flags, submission state) remains in memory. When the user navigates away (for example, to cart.html after a successful addGiftCardToCart call), the cart and gift card configuration are persisted on the backend via the GiftCardConfiguration and Cart/CartItem models, not in the gift_cards.html page state.

components.functionality: On component initialization, call getGiftCardOptions to retrieve the list of active GiftCardProduct options and GiftCardDesign options. Manage loading and error states, and provide the resulting data to child components responsible for type, amount, design, and details selection.

Parse URL query parameters (type, amount, designStyle, occasion) on page load and expose these as initial configuration hints. After configuration begins, optionally update the URL querystring when key fields (such as selected type, amount, and occasion) change, using the current in-memory state, without reloading the page.

Render interactive controls for selecting the gift card type based on the list of active GiftCardProduct records returned by getGiftCardOptions. Each option corresponds to a specific GiftCardProduct, including its type (digital or physical) and descriptive text. When the selected type changes, this component updates the active giftCardProductId in shared state and triggers revalidation of the amount field and any contextual helper text.

Allow the user to set the gift card amount for the currently selected GiftCardProduct. Display any predefined_amounts for that product as selectable chips or buttons and provide a numeric input for a custom amount. The component validates the entered or selected amount against the product’s min_amount and max_amount on change and on blur, marks the field as invalid if out of range, and updates the shared configuration state with the current amount value.

Display the list of GiftCardDesign options received from getGiftCardOptions and allow the user to choose one design. Support a toggle or filter control to focus on neutral/minimal designs (using style and is_neutral attributes). On initialization, use the designStyle URL parameter (if present) to preselect a matching design when possible. When the user selects a design, the component updates the selected designId in the shared configuration state.

Provide form fields for occasion, recipient name, sender name, and an optional message. The occasion field is implemented as a dropdown mapped to the allowed GiftCardConfiguration.occasion enum values. On initialization, the component applies the occasion URL parameter (if valid) to the occasion field. It keeps all field values in sync with the shared configuration state and performs basic validation (e.g., required checks, maximum lengths) before submission.

Allow the user to specify the number of identical gift cards to purchase for the current configuration. Enforce positive integer values, updating the quantity in the shared configuration state and showing validation errors if the value is invalid.

Summarize the current gift card configuration (selected type, amount, design, occasion, names, quantity) and provide the main action to add the configured gift card to the cart. On submission, run client-side validation across all fields and, if valid, call addGiftCardToCart using the configured giftCardProductId, designId, amount, occasion, recipientName, senderName, message, and quantity. Handle API responses by showing success messages, surfacing any returned error message, and exposing navigation to cart.html upon success.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All user-facing components are organized in a single, centered vertical flow. At the very top of the page (outside these components) sits a bold red page title and short supporting copy. Directly beneath, a large white card holds the step‑by‑step configuration experience: type, amount, design, recipient details, and quantity, each stacked vertically like distinct but related list items. Below this main card, a full-width olive-green band contains the final gift card summary and primary add-to-cart action, acting as a narrative bridge from configuration to checkout. Invisible/system components (loader and URL state manager) sit behind this structure without occupying visible layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All user-facing components are organized in a single, centered vertical flow. At the very top of the page (outside these components) sits a bold red page title and short supporting copy. Directly beneath, a large white card holds the step‑by‑step configuration experience: type, amount, design, recipient details, and quantity, each stacked vertically like distinct but related list items. Below this main card, a full-width olive-green band contains the final gift card summary and primary add-to-cart action, acting as a narrative bridge from configuration to checkout. Invisible/system components (loader and URL state manager) sit behind this structure without occupying visible layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The core content is constrained to a centered column approximately 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (roughly 8–10 of 12 columns), with 10–15% margins on each side. The white configuration card occupies this full central column with generous internal padding (~32px) and clear separation (~32–48px) from the olive band below. The olive summary band stretches full-width within the main content area (edge-to-edge of the central column, and visually full-bleed against the textured background), with taller vertical padding (32–48px) so it feels like a substantial banner. Vertical spacing between the internal form sections follows the same 16–32px rhythm, ensuring each step has breathing room.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is kept low-to-medium. Each selector or form section is visually separated with 16–24px of vertical space inside the card so it reads like a clean checklist rather than a crowded form. Labels, helper texts, and controls are stacked or aligned with comfortable line heights, and no more than one primary decision is asked per row. The summary band below is even more spacious, with a small amount of concise text and a primary button aligned in a single focal block to keep the end of the flow feeling light and decisive rather than busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves top-to-bottom: first the red heading and intro, then into the centered white card. Inside the card, the eye follows a clear sequence of sections from top (choose type) to bottom (choose quantity), each left-aligned and visually consistent, echoing the bullet-list structure from the mockup. As they reach the bottom edge of the card, the strong contrast and full-width presence of the olive-green band naturally pulls attention down into the summary/CTA. System components (loader, URL state manager) either overlay the center briefly or remain invisible, so they never disrupt this primary vertical reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Gift Cards page presents as a warm, marketing-style configuration flow centered on the screen. At the top, a bold red heading such as “Gift Cards” sits centered above a single paragraph of explanatory copy, both aligned within a wide central column framed by generous side margins and a subtle textured background. Immediately below, a large white card with soft corners and a light shadow floats in the center, occupying about three quarters of the page width on desktop. Inside this card, the gift card steps are stacked vertically in a calm, linear order—type selection, amount, design, recipient details, and quantity—each separated by comfortable vertical spacing so they read like a series of friendly checklist items rather than a dense form. Labels are left-aligned, primary inputs sit directly below or to the right within each section, and the overall rhythm matches the bullet-list layout from the mockup. Once the user scrolls past the last section, the white card ends and, after a shallow slice of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band fills the content area below. This band has generous vertical padding and centered text summarizing the gift card choice, paired with a prominent add-to-cart button, functioning as an editorial-style banner that concludes the journey. Background elements remain neutral and the entire composition stays vertically oriented and uncluttered, with any technical behaviors (loading and URL syncing) happening invisibly around the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader appears over the central white card area whenever gift card options are being fetched or recalculated. It is positioned centrally in the viewport, aligned to the same middle column as the card. When active, a semi-transparent white or light overlay covers the card’s entire surface (roughly 70–80% of viewport width and only as tall as the card), slightly dimming its contents while a spinner and short status message are centered both vertically and horizontally within the card area. Above and below the card, the page heading and olive summary band remain visible but visually secondary during loading. When not active, the loader occupies no physical space and is not visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay on top of the main white configuration card; fixed relative to the card’s position (not the full viewport), and appears/disappears without shifting surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render any visible UI and therefore does not occupy any explicit layout region. It lives logically alongside the main column but has no height or width; the page appears unchanged whether it is present or not. All visual space is reserved for the heading, white configuration card, and olive summary band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely functional and invisible; no spatial behavior. It synchronizes state with the URL in the background without affecting alignment, spacing, or scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The type selector sits near the top of the large white card, directly under the card’s internal title (if present) or the top padding. It spans the full width of the card’s content area (approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, minus 32–40px horizontal padding on each side) and is left-aligned. Inside this section, a clear section label like “Choose your gift card type” is positioned at the top-left, with brief helper text below if needed. The actual selection controls (e.g., two or three options like Digital / Physical / Both) appear in a horizontal row below the label on desktop, each as a pill or card-style toggle occupying equal width segments within this section, with 8–16px gaps between them. Vertical padding of about 24px separates this section from the card’s top edge and from the next section below, giving it the feeling of the first bullet item in a vertical checklist.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; it maintains its position as the first interactive step and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amount selector is placed directly below the type selector, within the same white card, separated by approximately 24px of vertical whitespace. It also spans the full inner width of the card’s content area. A left-aligned label such as “Select the gift amount” appears at the top of this section, aligned to the same left margin as the type selector label above, creating a strong vertical alignment line. Pre-set amount options (e.g., $50, $100, $200) appear as a row or wrapped grid of buttons or chips under the label, with any custom amount input positioned either to the right of the row or tucked below as a full-width field; all options together occupy about two-thirds of the section width, leaving comfortable right-side breathing room on larger screens. The section is tall enough to keep all options clearly separated with 8–12px gaps, preserving the airy, marketing-style feel of the mockup’s bullet list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the card, stacked directly under the type selector; it expands vertically as needed if additional preset amounts or helper text are present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The design selector sits immediately below the amount selector, again with about 24–32px of vertical spacing to signal a new step. It spans the full interior width of the white card. A left-aligned label such as “Pick a design” sits at the top; below it, a row or grid of card thumbnails (representing different gift card designs) is centered within the section but respects the left alignment of the label. On desktop, two to four design thumbnails appear per row, each thumbnail card being roughly one quarter to one third of the card’s width with equal horizontal spacing (8–16px) between them and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. This section is visually more image-heavy, so extra padding (approximately 32px top and bottom inside this section) keeps it feeling light and aligned with the mockup’s spacious bullet items. The entire block is still perceived as one ‘item’ in the vertical checklist of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; height is flexible based on the number of available designs and wraps to additional rows if needed while maintaining consistent left/right padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The details form occupies the central lower portion of the white card, stacked beneath the design selector with around 32px of vertical spacing to denote a shift from selection to personalization. It spans the full inner width of the card and may be taller than other sections due to multiple fields. A section title such as “Recipient details” aligns left at the top, with a short line of helper text below (for example, instructions on how the gift card will be delivered). Input fields (recipient name, email, message, sender name, delivery date, etc.) are arranged in a simple, vertical single-column layout, each field taking the full width of the card’s content area minus padding. Each field pair (label + input) has 8–16px vertical spacing, and there is 16–24px between groups of related fields. This layout keeps the form easy to scan top-to-bottom and echoes the structured but spacious bullet list feeling of the mockup, avoiding side-by-side field clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable along with the rest of the card content. As the most content-dense section, it expands vertically but remains visually contained by the card’s padding and consistent left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity selector is positioned near the bottom of the white card, directly below the details form, with approximately 24px of vertical spacing to separate it as the final configuration step. This section is shorter than the details form and spans the full inner width of the card. A left-aligned label like “Quantity” sits at the top, perhaps on the same baseline as a small inline hint (e.g., “You can buy multiple identical gift cards”) aligned to the right side of this section, creating a subtle two-column feel. Directly below the label on desktop, a compact numeric stepper control (minus button, quantity value, plus button) is placed, taking up roughly one quarter of the card’s width and aligned to the left. Additional explanatory text, if present, wraps below the control but stays within the same section. This area uses modest vertical padding (around 16–24px inside) so it feels connected to the summary that follows while still clearly contained within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the card’s content stack; its height remains relatively small and stable, with minor expansion if helper text is present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary and submit component is placed immediately below the white configuration card, separated from it by a slice of background whitespace of about 32px, mimicking the gap before the olive CTA band in the mockup. It appears as a full-width olive-green band stretching across the entire central content area (edge-to-edge of the same 70–80% column, and visually full-bleed against the textured background). The band has generous vertical padding—approximately 32–48px top and bottom—so it feels substantial and banner-like. Within the band, content is centered both horizontally and vertically: a concise line or two of uppercase or semi-uppercase text summarizing the gift card (e.g., “READY TO SEND A LITTLE LESS CLUTTER?” plus the selected amount and type) is centered at the top of the band’s content block, spanning about 60–70% of the band’s width, with balanced margins on left and right. Directly beneath this text, the primary “Add to cart” button is centered and slightly larger than regular buttons, with enough surrounding whitespace (16–24px) to make it the clear focal point. On wider screens, secondary information (like total price or a brief reassurance line) can align directly under the button or flank it in a subtle two-column layout, but the overall impression remains that of a bold, centered CTA strip concluding the vertical flow from the card above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored immediately below the main white card; it scrolls naturally with the page. On smaller screens, it may appear directly after the card content without any horizontal scrolling, keeping the CTA always reachable but not fixed or floating.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Review and manage all items in the cart, adjust quantities and shipping methods, and view updated totals before proceeding to checkout.

core_features: Fetch and display the current cart with all items, including item type, name, quantity, unit price, line subtotal, and shipping method if set (via getCart).
Visually highlight a specific cart item based on the optional highlightItemId URL parameter.
Allow users to update quantities for cart items and automatically recalculate cart totals (via updateCartItemQuantity followed by getCart).
Allow users to remove items from the cart and update cart totals accordingly (via removeCartItem followed by getCart).

For items with shippable options (e.g., products and physical gift cards), retrieve available shipping methods (including any Standard Shipping option) and let users select one (via getShippingMethodsForCartItem and setCartItemShippingMethod).
Display cart-level monetary summary: subtotal, shipping total, discount total, total, and any applied promo code (via getCart).
Provide a clear call-to-action button to proceed to checkout and navigate to checkout.html.
user_workflows:
Review cart on page load:
1) User opens cart.html (optionally with ?highlightItemId={cart_item_id}).
2) Page initializes and calls getCart with no parameters.
3) Page renders a list of cart items and the cart totals using the getCart response.
4) If highlightItemId is present and matches a cart_item_id from getCart, that item is visually highlighted and scrolled into view.
5) User reviews each item’s details, shipping method (if any), and subtotal.

Update cart item quantity:
1) User interacts with a quantity control for a specific cart item (e.g., increment/decrement or direct input).
2) Component validates the new quantity (e.g., must be a positive integer) in the UI.
3) On confirmation, the page calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity).
4) If success is true, the page immediately calls getCart again to refresh all items, line subtotals, and cart totals.
5) The updated cart is re-rendered, including the changed quantity and recalculated totals.
6) If success is false, an inline error message is shown for that item using the returned message.

Remove a cart item:
1) User clicks a Remove action on a specific cart item.
2) Optional: a confirmation dialog is displayed in the UI (handled client-side, no API call).
3) On confirmation, the page calls removeCartItem(cartItemId).
4) If success is true, the page calls getCart again to refresh the remaining items and cart totals.
5) The item is no longer visible in the list; updated subtotals and totals are shown.
6) If the cart is now empty, an empty-cart component is displayed instead of the item list.
7) If success is false, an inline error message is shown for that item using the returned message.

Choose or change shipping method for a cart item:
1) For each cart item that supports shipping options, the user opens a shipping method selector (e.g., dropdown or modal).
2) When the selector is opened (or when the item first renders, depending on implementation), the page calls getShippingMethodsForCartItem(cartItemId).
3) The component lists all returned shipping methods, including any method whose code is 'standard' (which business logic expects to be a lower-cost option).
4) User selects a shipping method.
5) The page calls setCartItemShippingMethod(cartItemId, shippingMethodId) with the selected method.
6) If success is true, the component updates the displayed shipping method name for that item and then calls getCart to refresh shipping_total and total for the entire cart.
7) Updated totals are shown to the user; if success is false, an inline error message is displayed using the returned message.

Proceed to checkout:
1) After reviewing items, quantities, and shipping selections, the user clicks the Proceed to Checkout button.
2) Client-side validation can ensure the cart is not empty before navigation (based on getCart data).
3) If valid, the page navigates to checkout.html with no additional required URL parameters.
4) The checkout page then handles contact and payment details using its own interfaces.
interactions: Page load: on DOM ready, parse the URL for highlightItemId, then call getCart; use the response to render all cart items and totals.
Item highlight: if highlightItemId matches a cart_item_id in the getCart response, apply a visual highlight state and optionally auto-scroll to that item.
Quantity change: when the user changes a quantity control and confirms, call updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity); on success, immediately call getCart to re-render items and totals.
Quantity validation: on blur or on change of the quantity input, enforce positive integer constraints in the UI; prevent calling updateCartItemQuantity with invalid values.
Remove item: when the user clicks the Remove button/link for a row, optionally confirm in the UI, then call removeCartItem(cartItemId); on success, call getCart and update the visual list and totals.

Open shipping selector: when the user clicks the current shipping method label or a "Change" control on a cart item, call getShippingMethodsForCartItem(cartItemId) if methods have not yet been loaded for that item; display the returned methods in a selector.

Select shipping method: when a user selects a shipping option in the selector, call setCartItemShippingMethod(cartItemId, shippingMethodId); on success, update the item’s shipping method display and call getCart to refresh shipping_total and total.

Error feedback: if any updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or setCartItemShippingMethod call returns success=false or fails, show a context-specific error message (e.g., near the item) using the returned message and do not change local state until getCart is successfully re-fetched.

Empty cart handling: if getCart returns an empty items array (or subtotal/total indicating emptiness), render an empty-state component instead of the main cart table while still showing a disabled Proceed to Checkout button or hiding it based on design choice.
Proceed to checkout: when the user clicks the Proceed to Checkout button and the cart is not empty, navigate to checkout.html; no calls to the provided interfaces are necessary at this moment.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver is the URL parameter highlightItemId, which is read each time cart.html is loaded or reloaded. When present, the page attempts to match highlightItemId to a cart_item_id returned by getCart. If a match is found, the corresponding item is marked as 'highlighted' in the client-side state, which controls both its visual emphasis and optional auto-scroll behavior. This highlight state is not persisted beyond the current page view and is reset when the page is reloaded without that parameter. All other cart state (items, quantities, shipping methods, totals, and the applied_promo_code) is treated as authoritative when returned by getCart after each operation. After any mutating operation (updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, setCartItemShippingMethod), the page refreshes the cart by calling getCart again instead of storing or deriving long-lived values from previous responses. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; navigation between pages (e.g., to checkout.html) relies solely on URL-based routing.

components.functionality: Initializes the cart page by calling getCart, manages loading and error states, and passes the loaded cart data (cart_id, items, subtotal, shipping_total, discount_total, total, applied_promo_code) to child components.
Reads the highlightItemId URL parameter on page load and determines which cart item (if any) should be highlighted, exposing a highlightedCartItemId state to other components.

Displays all items returned by getCart as a structured list or table, showing item name, item_type, quantity, unit_price, line_subtotal, shipping_method_name (if present), and any highlight state determined by cart-highlight-manager.
Provides interactive controls for changing the quantity of a specific cart item (e.g., input field and +/- controls), validates the input, and triggers API updates via updateCartItemQuantity.
Allows the user to remove a specific cart item, calling removeCartItem and triggering a cart refresh on success.

For a given cart item, loads and displays available shipping methods using getShippingMethodsForCartItem, allows the user to choose one (including a Standard Shipping option if provided), and applies the selection via setCartItemShippingMethod.

Shows the cart’s financial summary using the getCart response: subtotal, shipping_total, discount_total, total, and any applied_promo_code. Updates whenever the cart is reloaded after quantity, removal, or shipping changes.

Displays information about any promo code currently applied to the cart, using the applied_promo_code field from getCart. This component is read-only on this page, as no promo application interface is provided.

Replaces the standard cart item list when getCart returns no items, indicating that the cart is empty and guiding the user to add items (navigation links handled via standard anchor tags, not via provided APIs).
Allows the user to continue from the cart to the checkout process by navigating to checkout.html when the cart has at least one item.
Aggregates and displays error messages resulting from failed calls to updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, setCartItemShippingMethod, or getCart itself, in a consistent area of the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page uses a linear, single-column, top-to-bottom flow. A bold centered heading (outside the listed components) introduces the page. Directly beneath it sits a large, centered white card that contains the main cart content: promo info (if any) at the top, then the vertical list of cart items, then the shipping method selector. Below the white card, separated by a comfortable slice of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band holds the cart totals and the primary proceed-to-checkout button. System messages (errors) appear just below the heading, and utility behaviors (loading, highlights) overlay or enhance these core sections without breaking the vertical flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page uses a linear, single-column, top-to-bottom flow. A bold centered heading (outside the listed components) introduces the page. Directly beneath it sits a large, centered white card that contains the main cart content: promo info (if any) at the top, then the vertical list of cart items, then the shipping method selector. Below the white card, separated by a comfortable slice of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band holds the cart totals and the primary proceed-to-checkout button. System messages (errors) appear just below the heading, and utility behaviors (loading, highlights) overlay or enhance these core sections without breaking the vertical flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the main white cart card occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, leaving about 15–20% horizontal margin on each side. It has generous internal padding (around 24–32px vertically and 32–40px horizontally). The olive-green band stretches full width across the same central content area (edge-to-edge of the main layout column), with tall vertical padding (~24–32px) to feel like a substantial banner. Vertical gaps of roughly 32–48px separate the heading, the card, and the olive band. Floating or overlay elements (loader, error notifications) occupy minimal additional space and overlay existing content rather than pushing it down.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content maintains a low-to-medium density that feels editorial and marketing-driven rather than like a cramped dashboard. Cart item rows are vertically spaced with about 12–16px of breathing room between them. Text uses larger type and generous line height, and controls (quantity and remove) are visually grouped but not crowded. The promo banner inside the card, the shipping selector area, and the totals in the olive band all have clear padding and separation, making each section scannable at a glance.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the bold red cart heading at the top center, then moves down to any error notification just underneath if present, and then into the main white card. Within the card, the eye scans top-to-bottom: first the applied promo banner (if visible), then the vertically stacked cart items, each read left-to-right (product info on the left, quantity/remove controls on the right). After the final item and the shipping selector, the eye is naturally pulled downward to the contrasting olive-green band where the totals are displayed, followed by the prominent proceed-to-checkout button. Overlay elements like loader and item highlight effects support this flow without competing with it.

layout.overall_description: The cart page sits on a warm, lightly textured background that keeps the experience feeling homey and domestic. At the very top of the centered content column is a bold, red, marketing-style heading like “Your Cart,” anchored in the middle with ample whitespace above and below. Immediately beneath this heading, any cart error messages appear as a slim, noticeable strip, still centered and aligned with the main column. Below this, the main white card dominates the page: it is a wide, softly shadowed rectangle occupying about two-thirds of the page width, with generous padding all around. At the top edge inside this card, an optional promo banner runs horizontally, followed by a vertical stack of cart items. Each item reads clearly from left to right: descriptive details on the left, and a compact control cluster on the right for quantity changes and removal, with consistent spacing between rows. At the bottom of the card, a distinct subsection handles the shipping method, separated by extra vertical space or a subtle divider line. After a clean band of background whitespace, an olive-green horizontal band spans the central width of the layout. Inside this band, the cart totals are displayed in large, easy-to-scan typography, with the primary proceed-to-checkout button centered beneath or beside the totals, serving as a strong visual bridge to the checkout flow. System overlays such as the loader briefly dim or cover the card content with a centered spinner, while subtle highlight effects momentarily emphasize updated items. If the cart is empty, the main card and band are replaced by a single, friendly empty-state card in the same central position. Overall, the page feels linear, calm, and persuasive, guiding the user smoothly from review to checkout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart data loader appears as an overlay centered on top of the main content column when data is being fetched or updated. On initial page load, it covers the area where the main white cart card and olive-green band would appear. Visually, this can be a semi-transparent white veil aligned with the card’s width (about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered), with a spinner or subtle loading indicator in the exact middle of that region. It should not extend fully edge-to-edge across the entire viewport; instead, it stays constrained to the main content column so the page maintains its centered, editorial feel even while loading. When performing smaller updates (like changing quantity), this overlay can shrink to just cover the white card’s interior, leaving the heading and background visible but dimming the card content slightly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a temporary overlay fixed to the position and size of the main white card (and, if needed, the olive band), blocking interaction with underlying cart components while visible but not reflowing the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart highlight manager does not occupy its own block of space; instead, it enhances the existing cart item rows within the main white card. When an item is updated (for example, quantity changed or newly added), that specific row receives a subtle, warm highlight effect—such as a soft tinted background or a brief glow around its edges—within the same left-to-right row footprint. The highlighted area spans the full width of the row from the left edge of the product info column to the right edge of the control column, maintaining the consistent vertical spacing between items. The highlight respects the card’s padding and never extends beyond the card’s inner boundaries, preserving the clean, aligned stack of items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-structural, inline effect applied within cart-item-list rows; does not change layout or dimensions, only temporarily alters background/outline styling of specific rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list is the primary content area inside the main white card. After any applied promo banner at the top of the card, the item list begins as a vertical stack of rows, each row using a two-column layout reminiscent of the chevron-and-text pattern from the mockup. Each item row spans the full interior width of the card and is divided into a left content area (~70% of the card width) and a right control area (~30% of the card width). The left area is aligned flush with the card’s left padding and holds the product name, brief description, and price, all left-aligned with generous line height. The right area is aligned to the card’s right padding and vertically centers the quantity control and remove button in a single horizontal cluster. Vertically, each item row has 12–16px of top and bottom padding, with 8–16px of space between rows, giving the list a relaxed, scannable rhythm. The list stops a comfortable distance (24–32px) above the shipping-method-selector, which feels like a separate subsection within the same card. On long carts, the list simply grows downward, but the card remains notably centered with wide side margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the main white card, expanding as items are added; it scrolls with the page and does not have its own internal scroll on desktop unless explicitly needed for very long lists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-quantity-control lives on the right side of each cart item row, within the right control area of the white card. It is positioned toward the left side of that control column, so there is a small gap (8–12px) between it and the remove button on its right. The control itself (e.g., minus button, numeric value, plus button) is horizontally aligned and vertically centered relative to the item’s text on the left, making the row feel balanced. Its overall width is modest—roughly one-third of the right control area—ensuring it does not visually overpower the item description. There is at least 8–12px of padding around the control elements so that they feel tappable/clickable without creating clutter. On smaller screens, the quantity control can drop to a line directly below the product text but still aligned to the right edge of the card, preserving a clear, stacked relationship between information and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each cart-item row, anchored to the right side of the row’s control column; moves with the row as the list scrolls and adapts to responsive stacking on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-remove-button is positioned immediately to the right of the quantity control within the right control area of each item row. It appears as a compact text link or icon button with subtle but clear styling, aligned horizontally with the quantity control and vertically centered relative to the row. There is a small horizontal gap (8–12px) between the quantity control and this button, ensuring they are perceived as separate actions. The button sits flush with or slightly inset from the card’s right padding, maintaining a clean right edge when scanning down the list. Its size is intentionally smaller and visually lighter than the primary CTA elements elsewhere on the page, so it remains available but not distracting in the main reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within each cart-item row, anchored to the far right of the control cluster; it scrolls naturally with the item list and does not affect surrounding layout when hidden for unavailable states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-method-selector sits inside the main white card, directly below the cart-item-list. It occupies a full-width subsection that starts after a noticeable vertical gap (24–32px) or a subtle horizontal divider line that visually separates it from the item rows above. Within this subsection, a label or short explanatory text is left-aligned, while the actual selector (e.g., dropdown, segmented control) sits to the right on desktop, together forming a single, clear horizontal row. The entire selector area respects the card’s side paddings, so label and control never touch the card edges. Vertically, the selector area has 16–24px of padding above and below its controls, making it feel like a distinct block within the card. On mobile, the label and selector can stack vertically, with the selector centered or left-aligned, but still constrained within the same card footprint and positioned just above the bottom edge of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the main white card; it remains attached to the card and scrolls with the page, expanding downwards if more options or helper text are displayed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-totals-summary is housed within the olive-green band that sits below the main white card. This band spans the central content width (aligned with the card above) and has generous vertical padding (~24–32px). The totals summary is centered horizontally within the band, with the key figures (subtotal, shipping, total) stacked in a short vertical block. Larger, bold typography is used for the grand total, slightly above or directly above the proceed-to-checkout button. The totals block occupies roughly the central 40–50% of the band’s width, leaving comfortable margins on either side so the olive color acts as a visual frame. There is clear vertical spacing (12–16px) between each line item and additional spacing (16–24px) between the totals block and the proceed-to-checkout button below. The overall effect is a clean, banner-like panel that makes it easy to digest the numbers at a glance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section inside the full-width olive band; it remains centered and scrolls naturally as part of the page, with no sticky behavior by default on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-promo-display appears as a slim, full-width banner at the very top inside the main white card, directly beneath the card’s top edge and above the cart-item-list. It runs from the card’s left padding to its right padding, following the same interior width as the item rows. The banner uses a contrasting but harmonious color (for example, a soft green or muted accent) with slightly smaller but clear uppercase or semi-bold text, centered or left-aligned. It has 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px of top/bottom spacing from the card edges and the first item row, respectively. When visible, it acts like a friendly informational stripe announcing the applied promotion without dominating the card. When no promo is applied, this area collapses, and the cart-item-list moves up to sit closer to the top of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline banner fixed at the top of the main white card’s content area; appears and disappears without affecting the card’s overall width, only adjusting the vertical stacking of interior elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-state replaces both the main white cart card and the olive-green band when the cart has no items. In this state, a single, centered white card occupies the same horizontal space as the normal cart card (about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered with wide side margins). Inside, there is generous vertical padding (32–40px) and a spacious layout: a friendly heading centered near the top, a brief explanatory message below, and optionally an illustration or icon in the middle. A secondary or tertiary action button such as “Browse Services” sits centered near the bottom of the card. There is ample whitespace between each element, creating a calm, welcoming message encouraging the user to continue exploring. No olive band is shown underneath; instead, additional background whitespace appears below the card to maintain balance on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in the main content column, taking the vertical position where the main cart card and olive band would normally appear; it scrolls with the page but generally fits within the first viewport height on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proceed-to-checkout-button is the focal call-to-action within the olive-green band beneath the main cart card. It is centered horizontally, directly below the cart-totals-summary block, with a comfortable vertical gap (16–24px) separating the totals and the button. The button is large and wide, spanning roughly 40–60% of the content width on desktop, but still leaving visible olive margins on either side so it feels contained rather than edge-to-edge. Vertically, it has generous padding (12–16px) inside the button, with prominent text in uppercase or strong weight. The button sits with additional space (16–24px) beneath it before the bottom edge of the band, anchoring it visually in the center of the banner-like section. On mobile, this button can stretch closer to full width of the main column and may adopt a sticky behavior pinned to the bottom of the viewport while the user scrolls, still visually linked to the olive band styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the olive band, it is static on desktop and scrolls normally; on smaller screens it can optionally become a sticky element anchored to the bottom of the viewport to keep the primary action always accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-error-notification appears just below the main page heading and above the main white card, aligned with the central content column. It takes the form of a slim, full-width notification strip that spans the same width as the card (about 60–70% of the viewport), with a distinct background color (e.g., red or amber) to signal urgency. The text is left-aligned and concise, with 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding on both sides. There is a small vertical gap (12–16px) between this notification strip and the top of the white card to keep them visually separate. For transient or global errors, the notification may also appear as a floating toast anchored near the top center, overlaying a small portion of the card without pushing it down, but still within the same horizontal alignment. It never spans the full viewport edge-to-edge, instead staying within the main column for a cohesive, editorial look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Typically occupies an inline block between the heading and the main card; in some cases may appear as an absolute-positioned toast overlay anchored to the top of the content column without affecting overall layout height.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Review order details, enter contact information, optionally apply a promo code, and create an order before proceeding to the payment step.

core_features: Fetch and display a real-time checkout summary (items, shipping, discounts, totals, applied promo code) using getCheckoutSummary.
Allow users to enter and apply a promo code to the current cart using applyPromoCodeToCart and see updated totals.
Allow users to remove any applied promo code from the cart using removePromoCodeFromCart and refresh the summary.
Collect customer contact details (name and email) required to create an order in preparation for payment.
Create an order from the current cart using startCheckout with the entered contact details and handle success or error responses.
Navigate to payment.html with the generated orderId after a successful startCheckout call.
Provide navigation back to cart.html so users can adjust items before finalizing checkout.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial checkout load
1. User arrives at checkout.html, typically from cart.html.
2. The page reads URL parameters (if present) such as promo, name, and email to determine initial state (pre-filled promo code and contact fields).
3. The checkout-summary-loader component calls getCheckoutSummary.
4. The response is stored in page state and propagated to the order-items-list, promo-code-form (to show applied promo), and order-totals-summary components.
5. The user sees the list of items, shipping information, discount (if any), and the order total, along with empty or pre-filled contact fields.

Workflow: Applying a promo code
1. The user enters a promo code string into the promo-code-form input.
2. The user submits the promo form (e.g., clicks an Apply button).
3. The promo-code-form component validates that the input is non-empty and then calls applyPromoCodeToCart with the user-entered promo code.
4. If the API returns success=true, the component updates any local promo state and triggers the checkout-summary-loader to call getCheckoutSummary again so all totals reflect the discount.
5. The applied promo code and updated totals are displayed, including discount_total and the new total.
6. If success=false, the component displays the message from applyPromoCodeToCart.message as an inline error and leaves the previous summary unchanged.

Workflow: Removing a promo code
1. When a promo code is applied (applied_promo_code is present in the latest getCheckoutSummary result), the promo-code-form component displays a Remove or Change action.
2. The user activates this action.
3. The component calls removePromoCodeFromCart with no parameters.
4. If success=true, the component clears any promo-related local state and triggers checkout-summary-loader to call getCheckoutSummary again.
5. The UI updates to show no applied promo code and revised discount_total and total.
6. If success=false, the component shows the returned message as an error and leaves the previous summary visible.

Workflow: Entering contact information
1. The user enters their full name into a required name field in the contact-and-payment-step-form.
2. The user enters their email into a required email field, with basic format validation.
3. As the user types, the component updates internal form state; optional URL parameters (name, email) may have pre-populated these fields on load.
4. If the user attempts to proceed to payment with missing or invalid contact details, the component shows validation errors and prevents the startCheckout call.

Workflow: Creating an order and continuing to payment
1. With valid contactName and contactEmail in the form, the user activates the Continue to Payment button within the contact-and-payment-step-form.
2. The component performs final client-side validation.
3. On success, it calls startCheckout with contactName and contactEmail.
4. If the API returns success=true, the component extracts order_id from the response.
5. The page navigates to payment.html with a URL parameter orderId set to this order_id (e.g., payment.html?orderId={order_id}).
6. If success=false, the component displays the message from the response as a blocking error and does not navigate away, allowing the user to fix input or retry.

Workflow: Returning to cart to adjust items
1. The user clicks a Back to Cart link or breadcrumb in the checkout-navigation component.
2. The component navigates to cart.html, optionally preserving origin context via a query parameter (e.g., cart.html?from=checkout) so the cart page can adjust its behavior if needed.
3. No interfaces are called on this action; only navigation occurs.
4. When the user later returns to checkout.html, the entire initialization workflow (including getCheckoutSummary) runs again to reflect any changes.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization (onLoad)
- Trigger: checkout.html is loaded.
- Behavior: checkout-summary-loader calls getCheckoutSummary.
- Behavior: promo-code-form inspects the URL for a promo parameter. If present and non-empty, it pre-fills the promo field and optionally triggers applyPromoCodeToCart with that value, then asks checkout-summary-loader to refresh summary.
- Behavior: contact-and-payment-step-form reads name and email URL parameters (if any) to pre-fill contact fields.

Interaction: Applying promo code (onSubmit)
- Trigger: User submits the promo-code-form.
- Behavior: Validate that the promo code field is not empty.
- Behavior: Call applyPromoCodeToCart(promoCode=<user-input>). Disable the Apply button while waiting.
- Behavior: On success=true, clear any previous promo error message, update the displayed applied promo code, and trigger checkout-summary-loader to re-call getCheckoutSummary.
- Behavior: On success=false, show applyPromoCodeToCart.message as an error and do not refresh the summary.

Interaction: Removing promo code (onClick)
- Trigger: User activates the Remove promo action when a promo is applied.
- Behavior: Call removePromoCodeFromCart().
- Behavior: On success=true, clear the promo input and any applied promo state, then trigger checkout-summary-loader to call getCheckoutSummary.
- Behavior: On success=false, show removePromoCodeFromCart.message as an error and maintain current summary.

Interaction: Editing contact fields (onChange / onBlur)
- Trigger: User edits the name or email fields in contact-and-payment-step-form.
- Behavior: Update local form state with each change.
- Behavior: Optionally perform real-time or on-blur validation (e.g., email format) and show inline feedback without calling any interfaces.
- Behavior: Keep Continue to Payment disabled or show validation messages until required fields are valid.

Interaction: Continue to Payment (onSubmit)
- Trigger: User submits the contact-and-payment-step-form (e.g., clicks Continue to Payment).
- Behavior: Perform client-side validation of name and email.
- Behavior: If invalid, prevent form submission, show errors, and do not call startCheckout.
- Behavior: If valid, call startCheckout(contactName=<form name>, contactEmail=<form email>). Disable the Continue to Payment button and show a loading state while waiting.
- Behavior: On success=true, navigate to payment.html?orderId=<order_id> using the returned order_id.
- Behavior: On success=false, show the returned message as an error, re-enable the button, and keep the user on checkout.html.

Interaction: Back to Cart navigation (onClick)
- Trigger: User clicks Back to Cart in checkout-navigation.
- Behavior: Navigate to cart.html, optionally appending a from=checkout query parameter.
- Behavior: Does not call any interfaces.

Interaction: Refreshing summary after changes (custom events)
- Trigger: applyPromoCodeToCart or removePromoCodeFromCart returns success=true.
- Behavior: checkout-summary-loader is notified via a custom event (e.g., onCartPricingChanged) and calls getCheckoutSummary again.
- Behavior: order-items-list, promo-code-form, and order-totals-summary re-bind to the updated summary data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are used to control and initialize page state without relying on localStorage.

Supported parameters (all optional):
- promo: A promo code string to be auto-applied on load.
  - On page load, if promo is present and non-empty, promo-code-form pre-fills its input with this value.
  - promo-code-form may automatically call applyPromoCodeToCart with this value once, then triggers checkout-summary-loader to refresh via getCheckoutSummary.
  - If the promo is invalid, the error from applyPromoCodeToCart is displayed; the URL is not modified by the page.

- name: A URL-encoded string used to pre-fill the contact name field.
  - On load, contact-and-payment-step-form reads this parameter and initializes its name value.
  - The user can still edit this field; changes are kept in memory only.

- email: A URL-encoded string used to pre-fill the contact email field.
  - On load, contact-and-payment-step-form reads this parameter and initializes its email value.
  - The user can edit this value; changes do not update the URL.

- from: An origin indicator (e.g., from=cart) used to inform navigation logic and optional UI cues.
  - checkout-navigation reads this parameter but does not alter interface behavior.
  - When the Back to Cart link is clicked, the page navigates to cart.html, optionally preserving or setting from=checkout.

- Other state:
  - Cart identity is managed entirely by the backend session or environment; no cart_id is passed via URL to interfaces because getCheckoutSummary and other assigned interfaces do not accept such parameters.
  - The current cart and pricing state are always fetched dynamically from getCheckoutSummary; no hardcoded item, price, or promo data is stored in the page.
  - The created order is represented by order_id returned from startCheckout and is passed forward via the payment.html?orderId={order_id} URL parameter on navigation.
  - All transient UI state (form contents, validation flags, loading states, and API responses) is held in in-memory page state only and is reset on full page reloads.

components.functionality: Central data-loading component that retrieves the current checkout summary from getCheckoutSummary and exposes it to dependent components (order-items-list, promo-code-form, order-totals-summary). It ensures the summary is up-to-date on initial load and after promo-related changes.

Displays the list of items included in the checkout summary, including quantity, unit price, line_subtotal, and any associated shipping method name per line. It is read-only on this page and reflects the current state of the cart as provided by checkout-summary-loader.

Manages the lifecycle of applying and removing a promo code for the current cart. It allows manual entry of a promo code, optional auto-application from the promo URL parameter, calls applyPromoCodeToCart and removePromoCodeFromCart, shows success or error messages, and notifies checkout-summary-loader when cart pricing should be refreshed.

Displays the aggregated monetary values from the checkout summary, including subtotal, shipping_total, discount_total, total, and the currently applied promo code (if any). It updates whenever checkout-summary-loader fetches new data.

Collects the user's contactName and contactEmail, performs client-side validation, and initiates order creation via startCheckout when the user chooses to continue. On a successful response, it redirects to payment.html with the orderId URL parameter set to the returned order_id. On error, it displays descriptive feedback and allows retry.

Provides navigation actions specific to the checkout step: a Back to Cart link and coordination with the contact-and-payment-step-form's Continue to Payment action. Back to Cart navigates to cart.html, optionally preserving context with a from query parameter. It does not call any interfaces directly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top‑to‑bottom narrative similar to the mockup: page title at the top center, followed by a single prominent white card that contains the checkout summary (order items, promo code, totals) as one coherent block. Below that, a full‑width olive-green band acts as a narrative bridge (“Next, tell us how to reach you”) leading into a second central white card for contact details. Checkout navigation controls float at the right edge of the viewport as secondary actions, and the loader overlays only the summary area when data is fetching.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top‑to‑bottom narrative similar to the mockup: page title at the top center, followed by a single prominent white card that contains the checkout summary (order items, promo code, totals) as one coherent block. Below that, a full‑width olive-green band acts as a narrative bridge (“Next, tell us how to reach you”) leading into a second central white card for contact details. Checkout navigation controls float at the right edge of the viewport as secondary actions, and the loader overlays only the summary area when data is fetching.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered main column approximately 8–10 columns wide within a 12‑column grid (roughly 60–70% of viewport width) with 15–20% margins on each side on desktop. Both main white cards (summary and contact form) occupy this central column with generous internal padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal). The olive band spans the full content width with generous vertical padding (24–32px) to read as a distinct horizontal slice. The floating navigation occupies a narrow strip on the right, overlapping the background but not the central column. Vertical gaps between major blocks (card → band → card) are 32–48px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low-to-medium: information is grouped into clear blocks, with 16–24px vertical spacing between list items and field groups. Text uses large, legible type with comfortable line height, and key rows like totals and primary buttons receive extra breathing room. The summary card feels like an editorial review section; the contact card feels like a structured but airy form. Nothing is edge-to-edge crowded; every element has at least one base unit (8px) of internal breathing space, usually more.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves straight down the center: red page heading → summary card (items list first, then promo code, then totals as a natural climax) → olive-green band that visually pulls the eye further down → contact form card as the next step. Within each card, content is left-aligned and stacked vertically to reinforce the reading flow. Monetary values and key labels align in consistent columns to make scanning easy.
