The floating navigation on the right sits just outside the main reading path as a persistent, peripheral control; the loader briefly overrides the summary card, drawing attention to the loading state without shifting layout.

layout.overall_description: The checkout page presents as a clean, centered column on a soft, homey background. At the top, a bold red heading introduces the step, sitting above a large, rounded white card that contains all order summary details. This card is visually anchored in the middle of the viewport, spanning about two-thirds of the page width with generous side margins and comfortable padding inside. Within it, the order items list fills the upper portion, the promo code section sits just below as a smaller sub-block, and the order totals are clearly emphasized at the bottom of the card. A slice of background separates this card from a full-width olive-green horizontal band that stretches across the central content area, with uppercase, centered text bridging the storyline into the contact step. Directly beneath this band, another white card, equal in width to the first, holds the contact-and-payment step form, laid out in a clear vertical flow. Off to the right, a slim stack of floating checkout navigation controls sits mid‑screen, aligned with the main content but not overlapping it, providing next/back actions without interrupting the vertical reading path. When data is loading, a soft overlay appears only over the summary card, with a centered spinner, preserving the overall layout while signaling state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay confined to the area of the main summary card in the central column. When active, it dims only the region occupied by the order summary cluster (order-items-list, promo-code-form, and order-totals-summary) while leaving the page heading, olive band, contact form card, and floating navigation visible. The overlay follows the same width as the card (approximately 60–70% of the viewport centered) and aligns flush with the card’s rounded corners, giving the impression of the card going into a ‘paused’ state. A circular spinner or loading indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within this overlay, becoming the focal point without changing surrounding layout or page scroll position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned absolutely over the summary card’s bounding box within the main column; appears and disappears without shifting layout, and scrolls with the page since it is bound to the card rather than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order items list sits at the top of the first large white summary card, directly below the page heading with a vertical gap of about 32–40px. The card itself is centered on the page, spans roughly 8–10 grid columns (about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop), and uses 24–32px vertical and 32–40px horizontal padding so the items never touch the edges. Inside the card, the order items list occupies the upper two-thirds of the card’s height, with each item presented as a horizontal row: a small visual or marker area on the left (analogous to the chevron in the mockup) and the item’s descriptive text and metadata on the right, both left-aligned. Items stack vertically with 12–16px of space between rows, keeping the list comfortably scannable without feeling cramped. The top of this list aligns visually with the card’s top padding, reinforcing the card-as-a-single-block feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main summary card; it expands vertically as items are added, pushing the bottom of the card down while maintaining the fixed central width and outer margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo code form is positioned directly below the order items list within the same summary card, separated by a subtle horizontal divider or 16–24px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full internal width of the card, maintaining the same left alignment as the items list text. The form is laid out as a horizontal row within the card: a label or hint text aligned to the left, followed by a promo code input field that occupies roughly 60–70% of the card’s width, and an ‘Apply’ button aligned to the right edge or snugly to the right of the input. There is 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the row to keep it feeling substantial but compact relative to the more content-heavy items list. This area reads like a concise, functional stripe in the lower middle of the card, still clearly part of the summary block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the summary card; maintains its position as the middle segment of the card, with its height determined by its content but surrounded by consistent card padding and spacing to adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order totals summary occupies the bottom portion of the same white summary card, forming the visual conclusion of the card’s content. It begins immediately below the promo code form, separated by either a fine divider line or 16–24px of whitespace, and uses slightly increased vertical padding (24–32px) to feel weightier. Inside this block, line items such as subtotals, discounts, and taxes are arranged in a tidy vertical stack, each line using a two-column pattern: the label left-aligned, the amount right-aligned, mirroring the mockup’s consistent left icon/right text alignment but focusing on numbers. The final ‘Total’ row is emphasized with bolder or larger text and possibly slightly tighter spacing to the top of that row, drawing the eye to it as the key figure. Overall, this section occupies about the lower quarter of the card’s height, framed by the card’s bottom padding, with the total row sitting comfortably above the card’s lower edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, always anchored to the bottom of the summary card content. As the items list grows, this block moves downward but retains its relative spacing to the promo section and the card’s bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-and-payment step form is housed in a second large white card centered directly beneath the olive-green narrative band. The band itself sits 32–48px below the summary card, spans the full content width, and contains a single line of uppercase, centered text; immediately below it, after another 24–32px of whitespace, the contact form card appears. This card matches the summary card in width and general styling, reinforcing the single-column rhythm. Internally, the form is laid out in a simple vertical flow: key sections (e.g., contact details, address, scheduling preferences) stack top-to-bottom with 24px gaps between groups and 12–16px between individual fields. Labels and inputs are left-aligned, with most fields spanning nearly the full width of the card’s inner area; any side-by-side fields (like first/last name) should evenly split the inner width while maintaining generous spacing between them. The card’s padding mirrors the summary card (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal), giving the form an airy, approachable feel even with multiple fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it follows the summary card and olive band in the vertical sequence and extends downward as needed, keeping the same fixed central width and margins as the card above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout navigation is presented as a compact stack of action buttons floating along the right side of the viewport, visually separate from but vertically aligned with the central content. On desktop, it appears roughly centered vertically on the screen, offset from the right edge of the main content column by 24–32px so it sits over the background area, not over the cards. The stack typically includes a primary ‘Continue’ or ‘Create Order’ button and a secondary ‘Back’ or ‘Edit’ control, arranged vertically with 8–12px between them, styled as pill or rounded buttons similar in spirit to the mockup’s floating FABs. These buttons are smaller than the main card buttons and do not span the full height or width, so they read as peripheral helpers rather than the main content. On narrower viewports, this navigation can dock to the bottom of the screen as a slim full-width bar, but in all cases it remains visually subordinate to the central cards while always accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/floating relative to the viewport on desktop, maintaining its position on the right side as the user scrolls through the summary and contact form cards, without overlapping the central content. On mobile, it can become a sticky bottom bar to preserve accessibility while respecting reduced horizontal space.

name: Payment
title: Payment
description: Completes payment for an existing order by displaying the final order summary and available payment methods, handling both online and deferred payment scenarios.

core_features: Load the order’s final payment summary using getPaymentSummary(orderId), including items, subtotal, shipping, discounts, total, and any applied promo code.
Retrieve available payment methods (online and offline) using getPaymentOptions and present them for user selection.
Support online payment flows where the user selects an online method, enters payment details, and submits payment via submitPayment(orderId, paymentMethodCode).

Support deferred or non-online payment flows (e.g., pay at appointment or no payment required) where the user confirms the choice and the page finalizes the order via submitPayment without requiring online payment details.
Prevent duplicate submissions by disabling the main payment action while submitPayment is in progress and handling success/failure responses.
Handle special states such as orders that are already paid or cancelled by adjusting available actions and messaging accordingly.
Optionally redirect to index.html after a successful payment, passing URL parameters to indicate payment completion for a success banner on the homepage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Access Payment Page from Checkout
1. User completes checkout and is sent to payment.html with an orderId URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page reads orderId from the query string.
3. The page calls getPaymentSummary(orderId) to load the final order summary.
4. In parallel, the page calls getPaymentOptions to retrieve available payment methods.
5. The user reviews the order summary (items, totals, promo code, shipping) and the list of payment options.
6. If the order status from getPaymentSummary indicates that payment is already completed or the order is cancelled, the page displays a read-only summary and disables payment actions.
7. If the order status indicates pending payment, the user proceeds with one of the payment workflows below.

Workflow: Online Payment (e.g., card or digital wallet)
1. After order and payment methods are loaded, the user selects an online payment method from the list returned by getPaymentOptions where is_online is true.
2. The page shows a payment details form appropriate for online methods (e.g., fields for card or wallet authorization), but does not send these details to the backend via the provided interfaces.
3. The user fills in the required fields and clicks the main payment action button (e.g., Place Order / Pay Now).
4. The page validates the form client-side (e.g., required fields, basic format checks). If validation fails, it shows inline errors and does not call submitPayment.
5. If validation passes, the page calls submitPayment(orderId, paymentMethodCode) with the selected payment method code.
6. While submitPayment is pending, the page disables the payment action button and may show an in-progress state.
7. On response: if success is true and payment_status indicates a successful payment and order_status reflects payment completion, the page shows a success confirmation state and may trigger a redirect to index.html with URL parameters indicating payment success.
8. If success is false or payment_status indicates failure, the page displays an error state, keeps the current selection and input intact, and allows the user to retry or choose a different method.

Workflow: Pay at Appointment (Deferred Payment)
1. The user reaches payment.html with orderId and the page loads order and methods as above.
2. getPaymentOptions returns a non-online payment method corresponding to paying later (for example, a method where is_online is false and the code represents a deferred option).
3. The user selects this non-online payment method.
4. The payment details form remains hidden or minimal because no online payment details are needed.
5. The user clicks the main confirmation button to finalize the choice.
6. The page calls submitPayment(orderId, paymentMethodCode) with the deferred payment method code.
7. On a successful response, with payment_status and order_status updated to reflect that the order is confirmed without online payment, the page displays a confirmation state describing that payment will occur later and may redirect to index.html with appropriate URL parameters.
8. If the response indicates failure, the page shows an error state while allowing the user to retry or switch to an online payment method if available.

Workflow: No Payment Required (Zero Total or Fully Discounted)
1. The user reaches payment.html with orderId and the page loads the summary using getPaymentSummary(orderId).
2. The page detects from the summary that the total is zero, or that getPaymentOptions includes a method representing no payment required.
3. If a no-payment-required method is present, the page preselects it to simplify the flow.
4. The user confirms that no online payment is needed by clicking the main confirmation button.
5. The page calls submitPayment(orderId, paymentMethodCode) using the no-payment-required method code.
6. On a successful response (e.g., payment_status indicates no payment needed and order_status indicates completion), the page shows a confirmation state and may redirect to index.html with URL parameters indicating a completed order that required no payment.
7. If the response indicates failure (for example, if the backend rejects the order state), the page displays an error state and asks the user to retry.

Workflow: Handling Missing or Invalid Order ID
1. The user navigates directly to payment.html without an orderId query parameter or with an invalid value.
2. On initialization, the page attempts to read orderId from the URL. If none is found, it does not call getPaymentSummary.
3. The page shows a validation error state explaining that the order cannot be loaded.
4. Optionally, the page presents navigation options, such as links to return to the checkout or home page, using standard URL navigation (e.g., checkout.html or index.html) without calling additional interfaces.
5. If an orderId is present but getPaymentSummary(orderId) fails or returns an unexpected status, the page displays an error message and similarly suggests navigation options.

Workflow: Redirect After Successful Payment
1. After a successful submitPayment response (success is true and order_status indicates a completed or paid order), the page updates internal state to reflect completion and displays a confirmation.
2. The page can then automatically redirect the user to index.html or allow the user to click a button to go there.
3. The redirect includes URL parameters such as paymentStatus and orderId (for example, index.html?paymentStatus=success&orderId=...), so the homepage can display a contextual success banner based on these parameters.
4. The Payment page does not rely on local storage; it uses only these URL parameters and the interfaces’ responses to manage state.
interactions: On page load (initialization), read the orderId from the payment.html query string and, if present, trigger getPaymentSummary(orderId) and getPaymentOptions. Handle loading, success, and error states for both calls.

When getPaymentSummary(orderId) returns, bind the returned order data (status, items, subtotal, shipping_total, discount_total, total, promo_code) to the order summary component and update the page state to reflect whether payment can be taken (e.g., only when status indicates pending payment).

When getPaymentOptions returns, bind the list of payment methods to the payment method selector, mark a default selection according to business rules (such as the first eligible method or a special method for no payment required), and update the UI accordingly.

When the user clicks on a payment method option, update the selected payment method in page state, refresh any per-method descriptions, and show or hide the online payment details form depending on is_online.
When the user enters or edits fields in the online payment details form, update the local form state and validation status, but do not call any interface until the user submits.

When the user clicks the main payment action button (e.g., Place Order), validate the current selection: ensure that an eligible payment method is selected, ensure order status is still pending payment, and optionally ensure that any required online payment details are present and valid.

If validation passes, call submitPayment(orderId, paymentMethodCode) using the currently selected payment method; disable the main action button and show an in-progress state while waiting for the response to prevent double submission.

When submitPayment resolves successfully (success is true), update the page state with the returned payment_status, order_status, and payment_id, display a success confirmation, and optionally trigger redirect logic to index.html with URL parameters indicating success.

When submitPayment indicates failure (success is false or payment_status denotes a failed payment), display an error message and keep the current form values and method selection so that the user can fix issues or select another method and retry.

When the user clicks a navigation link or button (e.g., Back to Checkout or Return to Home), the page performs a standard URL navigation using the appropriate target URL and optional parameters instead of using local storage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the orderId URL parameter on payment.html and the responses from getPaymentSummary and getPaymentOptions. On initialization, the page reads orderId from the query string (e.g., payment.html?orderId=...). If orderId is missing or empty, the page displays an error state and does not call getPaymentSummary. If orderId is present, the page calls getPaymentSummary(orderId) to retrieve the order’s final payment summary. The order status from this response determines whether the user is allowed to submit payment (only when the order is pending payment). The same response provides all monetary values for display and logic (for example, detecting zero totals to enable a no-payment-required flow). Independently, the page calls getPaymentOptions (which has no parameters) and uses the returned list to populate the payment method selector. The selected payment method is kept as in-page state and is not stored in the URL, but its code is passed to submitPayment(orderId, paymentMethodCode) when the user submits. After submitPayment returns, the page updates its state to reflect the payment result: it may mark the order as completed in UI based on order_status, and it can optionally redirect the user to index.html while appending URL parameters (such as paymentStatus and orderId) to inform the homepage to show a success banner. At no point does the page persist state in localStorage or other client-side storage; all durable state is controlled by URL parameters and the server responses from the three assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, data loading, state management, and high-level navigation for the Payment page.
Displays the final order summary for review, including each order item, quantities, per-line subtotals, subtotal, shipping, discounts, total, and any promo code from getPaymentSummary.
Lists available payment methods returned by getPaymentOptions, indicates which are online versus offline, and allows the user to select one for the current order.

Captures any required details for the selected online payment method (such as card or wallet-related fields) and validates them before allowing submission. This component does not directly call submitPayment; instead, it exposes validated state to the controller and triggers submission events.

Provides primary actions for completing or cancelling the payment step, including the main payment/confirmation button, potentially a back navigation button, and a display of the order total next to the action.

Shows contextual status messages: initial loading, order already paid, order cancelled, payment in progress, payment success, and payment error states based on responses from getPaymentSummary and submitPayment.
Manages URL-based navigation after payment events, such as redirecting to index.html with URL parameters indicating payment status and orderId for downstream components to interpret.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered vertical column that mirrors the mockup: a bold, centered page heading at the top, followed by a prominent white content card containing the main payment flow (order summary, payment method selection, and payment details). Below this card, a full-width olive-green status band acts as a narrative bridge and reinforcement of payment state or next steps. Secondary actions are kept peripheral via a compact, partially floating action treatment that doesn’t interfere with the top-to-bottom reading path.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered vertical column that mirrors the mockup: a bold, centered page heading at the top, followed by a prominent white content card containing the main payment flow (order summary, payment method selection, and payment details). Below this card, a full-width olive-green status band acts as a narrative bridge and reinforcement of payment state or next steps. Secondary actions are kept peripheral via a compact, partially floating action treatment that doesn’t interfere with the top-to-bottom reading path.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is constrained to roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop (around 8–10 of 12 columns), centered with generous left and right margins (15–20% each). The white card is padded generously (about 24–32px vertically and 32–40px horizontally). The olive-green status band runs full width across the browser but its inner content is aligned to the same centered column. Vertical spacing between major blocks (heading → card → band) follows the 32–48px rhythm to clearly separate sections while preserving continuity.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is low-to-medium density, similar to a marketing/editorial layout. The order summary, payment methods, and form fields are vertically stacked with ample breathing room: list items and form rows have 8–16px vertical spacing, and section groups are separated by 24–32px. Larger typography and comfortable line heights maintain readability even when there’s detailed transactional information. The design avoids cramming controls; every section feels scannable at a glance.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is led down a strong vertical path: first the red-accented heading at the top center, then directly into the central white card. Inside the card, the flow is from top (order summary) to middle (payment method selection) to bottom (payment details and primary action), all left-aligned for clarity. After the card, contrast pulls the eye into the olive-green status band, which summarizes state or next steps. Any floating or sticky actions on the right edge are visually lighter and smaller, acting as secondary attractors that remain available without disrupting the primary reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Payment page sits on a warm, subtly textured background with a single, centered content column. At the top, a large, red-accented heading announces the payment step, with generous whitespace above and below to set it apart. Directly beneath, a substantial white card dominates the center of the viewport, spanning about two-thirds of the width and framed by comfortable margins on both sides. Inside this card, the order summary appears first, followed by a vertically stacked list of payment methods, then the payment details form, all separated by consistent, airy spacing. The bottom of the card houses the primary payment actions, aligned horizontally but contained within the same card. As the user scrolls, a compact, right-side sticky treatment keeps critical actions within reach without intruding into the main column. Below the card, with a clear slice of whitespace separating it, an olive-green band stretches across the full width of the page; within it, a single line of centered, uppercase text communicates current payment status or guiding instructions, acting as a narrative bridge to whatever comes next in the journey. The entire composition feels like a modern, marketing-style detail page for a home decluttering service—professional, warm, and easy to follow from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-page-controller defines the full-page canvas and main column structure. It stretches across the entire viewport, applying the warm, subtly textured background and centering a content column that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop. This column is horizontally centered, leaving generous side margins (approximately 15–20% of the viewport width on each side). At the top of this column, it places the primary page heading in large, red-accented text, centered horizontally with around 32–48px of whitespace above and below. Below the heading, it vertically stacks the main white card containing the order summary, payment methods, payment details, and the action bar, followed by a clear gap and then the status banner. All child components align to this same central column, keeping the layout cohesive and mirroring the single-column marketing layout from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-viewport container that manages vertical stacking and centralized column alignment; it does not scroll independently but sets up the overall flow and spacing for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel sits at the top of the main white card and spans the full internal width of that card. It occupies roughly the top quarter to third of the card’s height, depending on content length. It is left-aligned within the card with generous internal padding: approximately 24–32px from the top of the card and 32–40px from the left and right edges. The summary content is arranged in a vertically stacked, easily scannable list—line items (e.g., service name, date, room count, price breakdown) separated by 8–12px vertical gaps, with section headers or total lines given slightly more breathing room (16–24px) to stand out. The typography is slightly larger and bolder for key figures (like the total), echoing the marketing-style emphasis from the mockup while still feeling like part of a structured document. A subtle divider (e.g., a thin, light-gray line or a 16–24px spacer) at the bottom visually separates the order summary from the payment-method-selector that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the white card, always occupying the top-most region inside the card and anchoring the user’s understanding of what they are paying for.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-method-selector is placed directly beneath the order-summary-panel, inside the same white card. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the summary and the top of the selector, maintaining the open, spacious feel. The selector spans the full internal width of the card and uses a vertical list layout similar in rhythm to the bullet list in the mockup. Each payment method is presented as a horizontal row: a small icon or radio button on the left and a text label (with optional supporting text) on the right, all left-aligned. Each row is comfortable in height (around 48–64px tall), with 8–16px of vertical spacing between rows. The list stays within the card and does not scroll independently; instead, the entire page scrolls, preserving the single-column editorial feel. If there are section labels (e.g., ‘Pay now’ vs ‘Pay later’), they act like mini headings with slightly larger type and a bit of extra spacing above (16–24px) to echo the structured bullet sections of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the card, contributing to a continuous top-to-bottom reading flow between the order summary above and the payment details form below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-details-form is positioned immediately below the payment-method-selector, still within the boundaries of the same central white card. It starts after a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px, preserving the clear segmentation between ‘choose method’ and ‘enter details’. The form spans the full internal width of the card but keeps fields left-aligned in a one- or two-column layout depending on screen width; on desktop, form fields might occupy around 70–80% of the card width, leaving a subtle right-side breathing margin that keeps the layout light. Fields are stacked vertically with about 12–16px between individual inputs and 16–24px between logical groups (e.g., card information vs billing address). Labels are aligned above fields and set in readable type with generous line height, matching the editorial, domestic feel. The bottom of the form transitions smoothly into the payment-action-bar area, with a 24–32px vertical gap or subtle divider to signal that the user is reaching the ‘confirm and pay’ step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the white card, growing vertically as needed and pushing the card’s bottom edge downward while preserving the overall single-column, scrollable page behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-action-bar anchors the bottom of the main white card. It appears as a horizontal strip within the card, beginning around 24–32px below the last form field. This strip spans the full internal width of the card with 16–24px vertical padding. Primary actions (e.g., ‘Complete Payment’) are right-aligned within the strip, using a prominent button that visually echoes the strong color accents from the mockup (e.g., red as the primary). Secondary actions (like ‘Back’ or ‘Pay later’) are placed to the left of the primary button or subtly to its left with less visual weight. The overall look is substantial but not heavy: plenty of white space around the buttons ensures they feel approachable rather than cramped. To echo the mockup’s floating side FABs, when the page scrolls such that the white card is taller than the viewport, a compact, mini version of the primary action appears as a floating pill or button along the right edge of the viewport, roughly centered vertically. This floating element is narrower and visually lighter than the main in-card bar, ensuring it acts as a convenient shortcut rather than a new focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static at the bottom of the white card. Additionally, it exhibits a secondary sticky/floating behavior: once the primary button would otherwise scroll out of view, a compact floating button appears on the right side of the viewport (mid-height), remaining visible as the user scrolls while not overlapping the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-status-banner is positioned below the main white card, separated from it by a band of whitespace approximately 32–48px tall. It presents as a full-width olive-green horizontal band that extends edge-to-edge across the browser window, creating a strong visual break and narrative bridge similar to the band in the mockup. Within this band, the content is constrained to the same centered main column as the card: the text is centered horizontally and vertically in the band, set in uppercase or strong caps with ample letter spacing to feel like a declarative status or instruction (e.g., ‘PAYMENT PENDING – WE’LL CONFIRM YOUR BOOKING SHORTLY’). Vertical padding within the band is generous—around 24–32px top and bottom—making the band feel substantial and banner-like. This component visually signals current payment state and next-step expectations, drawing the eye naturally after the user completes or reviews the main actions in the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width banner anchored below the central card; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not remain fixed, but its color contrast and centralized text give it strong visual impact when in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The redirect-handler is largely non-visual under normal conditions, operating behind the scenes to manage navigation after payment actions. When it needs to surface feedback (for example, while redirecting to an external payment provider or returning to the site), it temporarily overlays a subtle, centered feedback layer within the main content area. This transient layer appears as a small, softly rounded white panel centered within the viewport (roughly 30–40% of viewport width), dimming the background content with a light, translucent scrim while leaving the overall page context visible. The panel’s text is center-aligned, concise, and uses the same clean typography as the rest of the layout, with plenty of padding (24–32px) to maintain the calm, homey feel. Once navigation completes, the overlay disappears, returning the user to the primary card and status banner context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally invisible and non-spatial, but when active it behaves as a temporary centered overlay with a translucent backdrop, fixed to the viewport so it stays in place as long as the redirect or navigation state is ongoing.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays dynamic background information about the professional organizer, organizing approach, service areas, and contact details, using getAboutPageContent. Provides navigation pathways to related action pages.

core_features: Fetch and display About page content (headline, story, qualifications, service areas, contact details) using getAboutPageContent.
Show an organizer story section sourced entirely from the API, without hardcoded copy.
List professional qualifications from the API as a structured, readable list.
List covered service areas from the API, with optional focusing/filtering driven by URL parameters.
Display a contact section with dynamic email and phone, enabling mailto and tel interactions.
Provide clear navigation CTAs to services.html, packages.html, assessment.html, and gift_cards.html, optionally enriched with URL parameters.
Support in-page navigation to specific sections (story, qualifications, service areas, contact) using URL parameters instead of localStorage.
user_workflows:
View About information:
1. User navigates to about.html (optionally with URL parameters like ?section=story).
2. The page controller component calls getAboutPageContent once on load.
3. When the promise resolves, the headline, story, qualifications, service_areas, contact_email, and contact_phone are stored in page state.
4. Individual components (story, qualification list, service area list, contact section) render using the bound data from getAboutPageContent.
5. If a section URL parameter is present, the page scrolls/focuses to the corresponding section after content is rendered.

Navigate to a specific informational section from another page:
1. User clicks an About link elsewhere, e.g., about.html?section=qualifications.
2. about.html loads and getAboutPageContent is called.
3. After data loading, the in-page navigation logic reads section=qualifications.
4. The page scrolls to or focuses the qualifications section component and visually marks it as active (purely via state, not static text changes).
5. User can then use the in-page navigation links to switch to other sections; the URL is updated accordingly (e.g., about.html?section=service-areas) without reloading the page.

Check if their location is served:
1. User opens about.html, possibly with a service_area URL parameter, e.g., about.html?service_area=encoded-area-name.
2. Page loads getAboutPageContent and obtains the service_areas list.
3. The service areas component checks for a service_area query parameter.
4. If present and matching an entry in service_areas, that entry is marked as focused/selected in state (e.g., scrolled into view or highlighted).
5. User can click different service area entries to update the focused service_area parameter in the URL (without reload) and see the selection change.

Start exploring services from About page:
1. User scrolls to the cross-links/CTA section.
2. User clicks "Explore Services" CTA.
3. Browser navigates to services.html, optionally including a ref parameter (e.g., services.html?ref=about) for contextual navigation.
4. User continues their journey on services.html based on that page’s own functionality.

Similarly for other CTAs:
- "View Packages" → packages.html (e.g., packages.html?ref=about).
- "Request an In-Home Assessment" → assessment.html (e.g., assessment.html?ref=about&service_area=<selected_area_from_state_if_any>).
- "Send a Gift Card" → gift_cards.html (e.g., gift_cards.html?ref=about).

Contact the organizer using About page information:
1. User navigates to about.html, optionally with ?section=contact.
2. After getAboutPageContent resolves, the contact component renders dynamic contact_email and contact_phone.
3. User clicks the email link; the component constructs a mailto URL using contact_email and triggers the default email client.
4. Or, user clicks the phone link; the component constructs a tel URL using contact_phone, triggering a call-capable app on supported devices.
5. No message is stored or sent via the website backend from this page; it only exposes dynamic contact details.
interactions:
Page load:
- Event: window load / about-page-init onInit.
- Action: Call getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
- Result: Store response object (headline, story, qualifications, service_areas, contact_email, contact_phone) in page state; notify child components to render.

In-page section navigation (e.g., Story, Qualifications, Service Areas, Contact):
- Event: onClick on a section navigation item.
- Action: Update URL parameter section=<target-section-id> using history.replaceState or history.pushState, then scroll/focus the corresponding section.
- Result: Visible section focus changes; URL encodes current section without reload.

URL-driven section focusing:
- Event: onInit or onPopState (back/forward navigation).
- Action: Read section from window.location.search; if matches a known section id, set internal state currentSection to that value.
- Result: Page automatically focuses the correct section after content is loaded or when browser navigation occurs.

Service area focusing via URL:
- Event: onInit or onPopState.
- Action: Read service_area from window.location.search; compare against entries in service_areas returned by getAboutPageContent (e.g., via normalized string matching).
- Result: If a match is found, the corresponding item is marked selected in state and optionally scrolled into view.

Service area selection via click:
- Event: onClick on a service area list item.
- Action: Set selectedServiceArea in component state; update service_area query parameter in the URL (without reload).
- Result: Selected service area is visually distinguished; subsequent CTAs (e.g., to assessment.html) can append this selected area as a URL parameter.

Contact email click:
- Event: onClick on email link.
- Action: Construct mailto:<contact_email> and navigate to it.
- Result: Default email client opens with the To field prefilled from getAboutPageContent.contact_email.

Contact phone click:
- Event: onClick on phone link.
- Action: Construct tel:<contact_phone> and navigate to it.
- Result: On supported devices, calling interface opens with number from getAboutPageContent.contact_phone.

CTA clicks to other pages:
- Event: onClick on "Explore Services" button.
- Action: Read current selectedServiceArea and other contextual state; construct URL services.html with query parameters (e.g., ?ref=about&service_area=<selectedServiceAreaIfAny>); navigate.
- Similar handlers:
  - onClick on "View Packages" → navigate to packages.html with optional ?ref=about.
  - onClick on "Request an In-Home Assessment" → navigate to assessment.html with ?ref=about and optional service_area.
  - onClick on "Send a Gift Card" → navigate to gift_cards.html with ?ref=about.

Browser back/forward navigation:
- Event: window onPopState.
- Action: Re-parse URL parameters (section, service_area); update currentSection and selectedServiceArea state; scroll/focus relevant section and service area item.
- Result: In-page view stays consistent with browser history navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic:
about.html uses URL query parameters to control which section is focused and which service area is highlighted, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage. Recognized parameters include: section – indicates which major section to focus (e.g., 'story', 'qualifications', 'service-areas', 'contact'). On load and on history navigation, the page controller parses this value and sets a currentSection state; a side effect scrolls or focuses the corresponding section component. The UI provides in-page navigation elements that update this parameter via history APIs when clicked, keeping the URL in sync with the visible section.

service_area – optionally indicates a specific service area to highlight. After getAboutPageContent returns the service_areas array, the service areas component compares the decoded service_area parameter with entries in that array. If a match is found based on a consistent comparison rule (e.g., case-insensitive and trimmed), the component sets selectedServiceArea state and visually marks the matching item as selected. Clicking on a different service area item updates selectedServiceArea and replaces the service_area parameter in the URL, without reloading the page.

ref – used when constructing outbound navigation URLs (e.g., services.html?ref=about). While about.html itself does not require a ref parameter for internal behavior, it may read any existing ref query parameter to maintain or forward context to downstream pages. ref values are treated as opaque strings; the About page does not enforce or rely on specific values.

The core page data (headline, story, qualifications, service_areas, contact_email, contact_phone) is not encoded in the URL and is always fetched fresh via getAboutPageContent on each page load. There is no use of localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies for About-related state; all state is in-memory and derived from the API response plus the current URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page, calls getAboutPageContent, manages loading/error state, and orchestrates section focusing and service area selection based on URL parameters. Distributes the fetched content to child components as props or via a shared state mechanism.
Provides in-page navigation between major About sections (story, qualifications, service areas, contact). Reflects and updates the current section based on URL parameter section and user clicks.
Displays the main About page headline retrieved from getAboutPageContent. Serves as a simple textual title for the page content (not a marketing hero).

Renders the organizer's story text returned from getAboutPageContent.story. Supports basic text layout, and can be programmatically focused based on the section=story URL parameter or in-page navigation.

Displays an ordered or unordered list of professional qualifications derived from getAboutPageContent.qualifications. Each qualification entry is rendered directly from the API data without hardcoded labels. The section can be focused when section=qualifications in the URL.

Displays a list of service areas obtained from getAboutPageContent.service_areas. Supports highlighting a selected service area based on the service_area URL parameter or user clicks. Exposes the selected service area to the page controller so outbound links (e.g., to assessment.html) can include it as a query parameter.

Displays dynamic contact information (email and phone) from getAboutPageContent.contact_email and getAboutPageContent.contact_phone. Provides actionable mailto and tel links built at runtime from these values.

Provides navigation CTAs to related action pages (services.html, packages.html, assessment.html, gift_cards.html). Uses current URL parameters (e.g., ref, service_area) and internal state (such as selectedServiceArea) to construct destination URLs with appropriate query parameters, without relying on any additional API.

Handles reading and writing of URL query parameters (section, service_area, ref) in coordination with the page controller and other components. Ensures that changes in UI selection are reflected in the URL and that direct URL access or browser navigation correctly updates the page state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered main column with a strong top-to-bottom reading flow. The page opens with a bold, centered About heading, followed immediately by a large white content card that combines the organizer’s story and qualifications in one continuous block. Below that, a second white card holds practical details: service areas and contact info. After a clear vertical gap, a full-width olive-green CTA band bridges to related action pages. A slim, floating section navigation on the right side lets users jump between sections without interrupting the main linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered main column with a strong top-to-bottom reading flow. The page opens with a bold, centered About heading, followed immediately by a large white content card that combines the organizer’s story and qualifications in one continuous block. Below that, a second white card holds practical details: service areas and contact info. After a clear vertical gap, a full-width olive-green CTA band bridges to related action pages. A slim, floating section navigation on the right side lets users jump between sections without interrupting the main linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain the main content to roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (equivalent to about 8–10 columns of a 12-column grid) and center it horizontally, leaving 10–15% margins on each side. Major white cards (story/qualifications and service areas/contact) occupy this central column with generous internal padding (24–32px vertical, 32–40px horizontal). Vertical spacing between major blocks (between heading and first card, between cards, and between the second card and the olive CTA band) is about 32–48px. The olive CTA band spans the full content width (edge-to-edge within the main page container) with tall vertical padding so it reads like a substantial banner. The floating section navigation occupies a narrow vertical strip on the right, overlapping the margin without encroaching on the main column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium density feel. Use large, comfortable typography with generous line-height so paragraphs of the story read easily. Bullet lists (qualifications and service areas) follow the mockup’s pattern: each line has a chevron icon and comfortably spaced text with 8–16px vertical spacing between items. Card padding is generous so text never feels cramped near edges. Group related content within cards into clearly separated blocks with small subheadings or subtle dividers to keep scanning effortless.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from top to bottom: the red About heading grabs attention first, centered near the top. The gaze drops directly into the large white card where the story appears first, then continues downward into the qualifications list. After exiting the first card, the user naturally continues to the second white card with service areas and contact info. The high-contrast olive-green CTA band at the bottom acts as a strong visual pull and narrative bridge to other key pages. The section navigation floats on the right as a secondary, peripheral set of controls, available but not competing with the primary vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits on a warm, slightly textured background that feels domestic and welcoming. At the very top center, a bold red About heading introduces the page and anchors the grid. Directly beneath it, a large white card dominates the central column, spanning roughly three-quarters of the page width with generous side margins. Inside this card, the organizer’s story flows in a few roomy paragraphs at the top, followed by a vertically stacked list of qualifications, each line starting with a small chevron icon and a bold keyword, creating a rhythm similar to a marketing feature list. After a comfortable vertical gap, a second white card of similar width appears, presenting service areas as another chevron-based list, followed seamlessly by neatly laid-out contact details towards the card’s bottom. As the user scrolls past these cards, the page transitions into a full-width olive-green band across the main content area, with centered uppercase messaging and links that clearly invite visitors to view services, book a session, or explore related pages. Along the right side of the viewport, hovering over the background and aligning vertically with the main cards, a slim stack of floating section-nav buttons allows quick jumps to Story, Qualifications, Service Areas, Contact, and CTAs without breaking the calm, linear reading experience. The entire composition feels like a modern, friendly marketing page: large type, generous white space, and clear, card-based sections that tell a cohesive story from introduction to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller establishes a centered, single-column layout over a warm, lightly textured background that fills the full viewport width and height. It defines a maximum content width (around 1040px) and centers this content horizontally within the window, effectively creating large left and right margins on larger screens and maintaining a consistent 12-column-inspired grid. All visual sections (headline, main cards, CTA band) align to this central axis. Vertical scrolling is the primary interaction, with generous top and bottom padding (approximately 48–64px) around the overall content stack so nothing feels cramped against the browser edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as the main scrollable container for the page, occupying the full viewport; establishes the central grid and background but does not appear as a visible component itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation appears as a vertical stack of compact, pill-shaped or circular buttons on the right side of the screen, floating over the background rather than embedded in the central column. It is vertically centered relative to the viewport (approximately midway down the screen) and horizontally positioned just outside the main content column, overlapping the right margin but never covering any card content. Each nav item is arranged one above the other with 8–12px of vertical spacing, creating a slim, unobtrusive rail. Icons or short labels (e.g., Story, Qualifications, Areas, Contact) are clearly legible but kept small compared to main headings. On scroll, this navigation remains sticky within the viewport, maintaining its relative position so users can jump to any section at any point without disrupting the primary vertical reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating and sticky on the right side of the viewport; anchored to the page controller’s right margin and remains visible as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The headline section is positioned at the top of the main content column, centered horizontally. It consists primarily of a bold, red About heading rendered in large typography. The heading spans roughly 60–70% of the central column, with text center-aligned, leaving ample whitespace to its left and right. There is generous top padding from the page edge (about 48–64px) and a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px above the first large white card. If a subtitle or short introduction line is present, it sits directly beneath the main heading, still center-aligned but in a smaller, calmer grey tone, keeping the focus on the red title while subtly setting context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow at the top of the content stack, aligning the central column and serving as the visual entry point to the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The organizer story occupies the upper portion of the first and largest white card, which sits directly beneath the headline, centered within the main column. The card itself spans roughly 75–80% of the page width (within the defined max width), with soft corners and a subtle drop shadow or elevation so it clearly lifts off the warm background. Inside this card, the story text forms a block at the top: a section title (e.g., My Story or Meet [Name]) is left-aligned within the card, followed by 1–3 short paragraphs of narrative text. The text block leaves at least 32px of padding on all sides, adhering to the generous card padding pattern. Below the story paragraphs, a vertical gap of about 24px separates this narrative from the qualifications list, which shares the same card to maintain continuity and hierarchy (story first, then evidence/credentials).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered as the upper content block within the first main white card; static within the normal column flow, expanding vertically as the story content grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The qualifications list continues inside the same white card directly below the organizer story, sharing the card’s width and padding. A small, left-aligned subheading (e.g., Qualifications or Why You Can Trust Me) introduces the list and sits about 24px below the story text. The list itself is a vertical stack of items, each row occupying the full inner width of the card. On each row, a small chevron icon sits on the far left, occupying roughly 5–10% of the card’s width, with the qualification text to its right spanning the remaining width. Text is left-aligned, with bold or colored keywords at the start of each bullet to echo the marketing-style emphasis seen in the mockup. Vertical spacing between list items is comfortable (8–16px), and the list ends with a consistent bottom margin (24–32px) before the bottom edge of the card, ensuring a visually balanced card with plenty of breathing room beneath the final bullet.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list embedded in the lower portion of the first main white card, expanding vertically as items are added; respects the card’s internal padding and does not break the card boundary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service areas section is presented in a second white card placed below the first card, maintaining the same width, centering, and visual style. A vertical gap of about 32–40px of background space separates the two cards, preserving them as distinct but related blocks. At the top of this second card, a left-aligned heading (e.g., Service Areas) appears, followed by a chevron-style list similar to the qualifications. Each service area line is a horizontal row with a chevron icon on the left and text on the right, aligned into a simple two-column row. The card padding mirrors the first card: 24–32px vertically and 32–40px horizontally, with 8–16px vertical spacing between each area to keep the density low-to-medium. This list typically occupies the upper two-thirds of the card’s height, leaving room below for the contact info to follow within the same card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the second main white card, stacked directly under the first card; resizes vertically based on the number of service area items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact info section occupies the lower portion of the same second white card that contains the service areas, creating a consolidated practical-information block. A subtle divider (a light horizontal line or extra 16–24px of vertical gap) separates the last service area bullet from the contact info. A small heading (e.g., Get in Touch or Contact) is left-aligned and sits at the top of this lower segment. Below it, contact details (phone number, email, possibly a small address or service region note) are laid out in a clean, vertically stacked format, left-aligned and easy to scan. Each contact line has 8–12px of vertical spacing. If icons are used (phone, email), they appear in a narrow column on the left similar to the chevron pattern, keeping a consistent visual language. The bottom padding of the card (24–32px) ensures there is comfortable space beneath the last contact line before the card’s edge, giving the entire block a balanced, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block positioned at the bottom of the second main white card, following the service areas; adapts its height based on the volume of contact details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cross-links section appears as a prominent full-width olive-green band directly beneath the second white card, separated by about 32–48px of background space. This band spans the entire width of the main content container and visually contrasts with both the background and the white cards, creating a strong horizontal anchor. Inside the band, content is centered horizontally and vertically: typically a single line or short block of uppercase or semi-uppercase text that feels like a statement or prompt (e.g., READY TO DECLUTTER YOUR HOME?), followed by or combined with inline links or buttons to related pages (Services, Book a Session, Before & After, etc.). The text and links sit within a generous vertical padding of 24–40px, making the band feel substantial and banner-like. Text is kept to a moderate width (about 60–70% of the central column) so it doesn’t stretch too wide, maintaining readability. This section clearly reads as the narrative bridge and CTA that concludes the About story and invites next steps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band within the main content container; fixed in the vertical sequence below the cards and remains in place as the user scrolls past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visible UI; it operates behind the scenes to synchronize the URL with the user’s current section or selected anchors (for example, updating the hash when the user clicks a section navigation button or scrolls to a new section). It does not occupy any space on the page and does not affect the layout of visual components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely behavioral; no spatial footprint and does not influence document flow or positioning.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Displays all policy information for the decluttering and organizing services and shop, with section-based navigation and deep links.

core_features: Fetch and display the latest policy content (privacy, booking, shipping, returns/refunds, digital usage, gift card terms) using getPoliciesContent.
Provide in-page navigation between policy sections (e.g., privacy, booking, shipping) with the currently active section reflected in the URL via a section query parameter.
Support deep-linking to a specific policy section using URL parameters so external links or emails can open this page directly at a relevant section.
Allow users to copy or share a direct link to any specific policy section, including the appropriate URL parameters.
Surface contextual navigation to the cart (e.g., from shipping or returns sections) that routes to cart.html using standard URL navigation.
user_workflows:
View all policies in default order:
1. User opens policies.html with no query parameters.
2. The page controller component calls getPoliciesContent once on load.
3. The returned object with privacy_policy, booking_policy, shipping_policy, returns_refunds_policy, digital_usage_terms, and gift_card_terms is stored in page state.
4. The section navigation component sets a default active section (e.g., the first available) and updates the URL to include ?section=<default_key> if no section is present.
5. All policy sections are rendered, with the active section highlighted in the navigation and optionally scrolled into view.

Open the page directly at a specific policy section from an email or external link:
1. User clicks an external link that points to policies.html?section=<section_key>.
2. On load, the page controller parses the section query parameter.
3. The page controller calls getPoliciesContent and stores the result.
4. Once content is available, the controller validates the requested section_key against the known set of policy keys.
5. If valid, the controller sets that section as active, updates the navigation highlighting, and scrolls the corresponding section into view.
6. If invalid or missing, the controller falls back to a default section and normal rendering.

Navigate between policy sections via in-page navigation:
1. User is on policies.html viewing one section.
2. User clicks another section in the section navigation component.
3. The navigation component triggers an onSectionChange handler with the selected section key.
4. The page controller updates the activeSection state, updates the URL query parameter ?section=<new_key> via history.replaceState or similar, and scrolls the new section into view.
5. The navigation component highlights the new active section; the content component ensures the relevant text is visible.

Copy a direct link to a specific policy section:
1. User hovers over or focuses a policy section heading.
2. A small 'Copy link' or 'Link to this section' control becomes visible for that section.
3. User clicks this control.
4. The section-anchor-link component constructs a URL to policies.html including ?section=<section_key> for that section.
5. The URL is copied to the clipboard (where supported) and/or displayed in an accessible message so the user can manually copy it.
6. The URL can be shared via email or other channels and will deep-link back to the same section on future visits.

Navigate from policy content to the cart page from contextual text (e.g., shipping or returns sections):
1. User is reading shipping_policy or returns_refunds_policy content.
2. Near the relevant text, an inline 'View cart' link is rendered by a contextual cart link component.
3. User clicks the link.
4. The browser navigates to cart.html, optionally with a source query parameter (e.g., cart.html?source=policies) to indicate the origin.
5. The Policies page does not perform any cart logic; it only navigates away.

Refresh or revisit the Policies page while retaining the last viewed section:
1. User is viewing a specific section and the URL includes ?section=<section_key>.
2. The user refreshes the browser or reopens the same URL from history.
3. On load, the page controller re-parses the ?section query parameter.
4. After calling getPoliciesContent, the controller sets activeSection from the URL parameter again.
5. The user sees the same section they were viewing before refresh, ensuring consistency.
interactions: On page load: the page controller parses URL parameters, initializes activeSection state, and calls getPoliciesContent to retrieve all policy content before rendering section components.

Click on a section item in the section navigation: updates the activeSection state, updates the URL ?section parameter without reloading the page, scrolls the associated policy section into view, and updates navigation highlighting.

Click on a 'Copy link to this section' control next to a section heading: constructs a URL with the correct ?section parameter, copies it to the clipboard when possible, and triggers a non-intrusive confirmation message.

Click on a contextual 'View cart' link inside or near shipping/returns sections: triggers a simple navigation to cart.html (optionally including a source query parameter) using a standard anchor link; no API calls are made on click.

Browser back/forward navigation when section changes have updated the URL: when the user uses the back or forward buttons, the page controller listens for history changes, re-reads the current ?section parameter, and updates activeSection and scroll position to match.

Error handling for getPoliciesContent failure: if the interface call fails or returns incomplete data, the page controller displays a generic fallback message component (without hardcoded policy content) explaining that policy content cannot be loaded at the moment and suggesting a retry or later visit; a retry control re-invokes getPoliciesContent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Policies page uses URL query parameters (not localStorage) to manage state, primarily the `section` parameter. On initial load, the page controller reads `window.location.search` and attempts to extract `section`. Valid values correspond to known policy keys (e.g., keys that map to privacy_policy, booking_policy, shipping_policy, returns_refunds_policy, digital_usage_terms, gift_card_terms). If `section` is missing or invalid, the controller sets a default active section and updates the URL to include that default via history.replaceState to avoid a full reload. When the user clicks a navigation item to change sections, the controller updates the in-memory `activeSection` state and updates the `section` query parameter in the current URL (also via history.pushState or replaceState) so the browser history records the section change. The UI listens to `popstate` events so that using the browser back/forward buttons re-synchronizes `activeSection` with the `section` query parameter and scrolls to the appropriate section. Optional secondary parameters such as `source` may be appended when linking out (e.g., cart.html?source=policies_shipping), but the Policies page itself does not depend on them for its internal behavior. All visible content is driven solely by the latest response from getPoliciesContent; no policy text, titles, or values are hardcoded into the page.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of policy content via getPoliciesContent, manages active section state based on URL parameters, handles error and loading states, and passes the appropriate content slices to child components.

Displays a list of policy sections derived from the keys available in the getPoliciesContent response, highlights the currently active section, and allows users to switch sections. It notifies the page controller when a different section is selected.

Renders the privacy-related policy text and anchors it as a scroll target for the privacy section. It listens for changes to the activeSection state to determine whether to emphasize or automatically scroll into view.
Renders the booking, rescheduling, and cancellation policy content for services and packages, anchored as the booking section. It participates in the section navigation and link copy behavior.

Displays shipping policies for physical products and gift cards, including delivery time and cost information contained in the shipping_policy field. It also hosts contextual navigation to the cart through a child cart-link component.
Displays return and refund policies for products and gift cards using the returns_refunds_policy content. It also embeds contextual cart navigation for users reviewing order-related policies.
Renders digital usage terms for downloadable resources and digital content, using the digital_usage_terms field and exposing the same deep-link and active section behaviors as other panels.
Displays the gift card terms for digital and physical gift cards based on the gift_card_terms content, and integrates with navigation highlighting and link copying.

Provides a control near each policy section heading that generates a URL to policies.html with the corresponding section key in the query string, and copies that URL to the clipboard or exposes it for manual copying.

Renders a simple link to the cart page in locations where the shipping or returns/refunds policy references carts or orders. This link navigates to cart.html and may append a source query parameter to indicate that the user came from a specific policy section.
Displays a non-content-specific loading indicator while getPoliciesContent is in progress, and hides itself when the policy data has been successfully loaded or an error state is shown.

Handles display of a generic error message when getPoliciesContent fails or returns an unusable result, without rendering any hardcoded policy text. Offers a retry control that re-triggers the getPoliciesContent call via the page controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single main column aligned within what would reasonably be a 12‑column grid, with content constrained to a centered column (approximately 8–10 columns wide on desktop) and large outer margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered main column that flows linearly from top to bottom. At the top of the content column sits a bold, centered page heading (handled by the page controller). Directly beneath it, a prominent white card holds the policies section navigation as a vertical list of clickable rows, visually echoing the chevron-bullet list from the mockup. Below that card and a slice of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band (as part of the section-navigation area) provides a narrative bridge that cues users to scroll into the detailed policies. The detailed policy content panels are then stacked vertically within the same central column, one after another. Floating controls for anchor links and contextual cart access sit to the right side of this column, vertically centered, and remain visually peripheral to the main reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered main column that flows linearly from top to bottom. At the top of the content column sits a bold, centered page heading (handled by the page controller). Directly beneath it, a prominent white card holds the policies section navigation as a vertical list of clickable rows, visually echoing the chevron-bullet list from the mockup. Below that card and a slice of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band (as part of the section-navigation area) provides a narrative bridge that cues users to scroll into the detailed policies. The detailed policy content panels are then stacked vertically within the same central column, one after another. Floating controls for anchor links and contextual cart access sit to the right side of this column, vertically centered, and remain visually peripheral to the main reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered and constrained to about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop (roughly 8–10 columns of a 12-column grid), leaving 15–20% margins on each side with a warm, lightly textured background. The navigation card and each policy content panel are full-width within this column, with generous internal padding (around 24–32px vertically and 32–40px horizontally). Vertical spacing between major sections—heading, nav card, olive band, and each policy card—is in the 32–48px range to keep sections clearly separated but visually connected. The olive band spans the full width of the content area (edge-to-edge within the main page content, not the browser window chrome) and uses taller vertical padding so its single line of text feels like a substantial banner. The floating controls on the right use minimal width—roughly a small stack of compact buttons occupying no more than 10–15% of the viewport width—and are inset from the right edge and from the top/bottom to avoid overlapping the primary content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Information is presented with low-to-medium density. The navigation list uses comfortable vertical spacing between rows (around 12–16px), with each row feeling like a distinct tappable item. Policy content panels use roomy line height and larger typography, with clear separation between paragraphs and subheadings. No component appears cramped; ample white space around and inside cards allows dense policy text to feel more like editorial content than legal fine print. Bullet lists within policy panels (if present) follow the same 8–16px vertical rhythm, and headings have extra margin above them to support scanning.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow pulls the eye from top to bottom in a deliberate sequence. The user’s attention starts at the red-accented page heading, then immediately drops into the large centered white navigation card. Within that card, consistent left-aligned chevron icons guide the eye down the list of sections. After the final nav item, the viewer’s gaze naturally continues down to the high-contrast olive-green band, which acts as a narrative bridge, signaling that detailed explanations follow. Scrolling down, the stacked white policy panels maintain this vertical reading rhythm. The floating anchor and cart controls on the right are visually noticeable but small and color-distinct (blue or similarly accented), so they register as handy tools in peripheral vision rather than interrupting the main reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Policies page sits on a warm, subtly textured background with a strong single-column structure centered on the screen. At the top of this column, a bold red-tinted “Policies” heading introduces the page, breathing with generous space above and below. Directly beneath it, a large white card spans roughly two-thirds of the viewport width, containing a neatly stacked vertical list of policy sections, each row marked by a chevron icon on the left and clear text on the right—this card feels like the primary gateway into the page’s content. Below the card, after a comfortable buffer of whitespace, a full-width olive-green band cuts horizontally across the column, holding a short line of uppercase text that bridges users from the overview into the detailed policy content. As the user scrolls, they encounter a sequence of white content panels—Privacy, Booking, Shipping, Returns & Refunds, Digital Usage, and Gift Card Terms—each centered within the same column, with generous padding and spacing to make lengthy policy text feel approachable and easy to skim. On the right edge of the viewport, a small vertical cluster of floating blue-accented buttons provides quick anchor-link and cart access while staying out of the main reading lane. When loading or when errors occur, subtle centered status indicators appear within the column, temporarily taking over the core content area while preserving the overall spacious, editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overarching canvas and central column. It applies a warm, off-white or lightly textured background across the full viewport width and height. Within this, it centers the main content column, which has a maximum width of roughly 960–1040px (about 8–10 columns on a 12-column grid) and horizontal padding of 24–32px on smaller screens. All major components—the page heading, section navigation card, olive band, policy panels, and status indicators—are stacked vertically inside this central column with 32–48px gaps between them. The controller also reserves a narrow vertical slice along the right side of the viewport for floating actions, ensuring the floating buttons don’t overlap the main text column. On mobile, the central column expands to fill most of the viewport width with consistent side padding, and floating elements can reposition toward the bottom-right while still being managed by this controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a centered, scrollable main content column with full-height background; manages the overall stacking order and allocates a reserved strip on the right for floating controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation sits just below the main page heading, occupying the first major piece of content real estate in the central column. It is rendered as a prominent white card that spans the full width of the content column (about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop), with rounded corners and a subtle shadow to lift it from the background. Inside the card, a vertical list of policy sections (Privacy, Booking, Shipping, etc.) is arranged as stacked rows, each row being a horizontal two-column layout: a left-aligned chevron icon in a narrow column (about 10–15% of the card’s width) and the section title and short description in a wider column (about 85–90%). Each row has generous vertical padding (12–16px) and clear separation from the next, so the list feels airy and highly scannable. The entire card has 24–32px padding on the top and bottom and 32–40px padding on the sides. Immediately beneath the card, still conceptually part of this navigation section, an olive-green full-width band spans edge to edge of the content column with tall vertical padding (24–32px) and a centered line of uppercase text, acting as a narrative bridge and prompting users to scroll for details or jump into sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the main column; the white card and the olive band scroll with the page and stay grouped visually at the top of the content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Privacy policy panel is the first detailed content block after the olive band. It appears as a large, centered white card that matches the width of the navigation card, preserving the single-column rhythm. There is a 32–48px gap of whitespace between the olive band and the top of this card to clearly differentiate it as a new section. The card itself uses ample internal padding (around 32px top and bottom, 32–40px left and right) and contains a left-aligned section heading at the top, followed by paragraphs and subheadings stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. If there are bullet lists, they align flush left within the text area, with consistent 8–16px spacing between bullet items. The overall height of the card may be significant due to long policy text, but the generous line height and spacing keep it easy to scan.
